title: Penn State suspends Delta Chi Fraternity for hazing
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's chapter of Delta Chi was placed on indefinite suspension for a minimum of four years, effective April 14, after a university investigation into hazing allegations, according to a statement on the Penn State Student Affairs website.

The Penn State Office of Student Accountability and Conflict started an investigation in January of 2026 after they had received reports of alleged physical and mental abuse, sleep deprivation and excessive alcohol intake. It was placed on interim suspension while the investigation was ongoing.

After the four year suspension, Delta Chi may be allowed to return back to campus but may be placed on two years of conduct probation.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse star Rocquette Allen named Big Ten Freshman of the Year
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After earning six Big Ten Freshman of the Week honors throughout the season, Rocquette Allen’s standout season has paid off.

Allen was named the Big Ten Freshman of the Year on Tuesday after earning a unanimous selection to the Big Ten All-Freshman Team last week.

Rocquette Allen rockin' Freshman of the Year! 🎸 #B1GWLax pic.twitter.com/CafKaadPYv— Big Ten Lacrosse (@B1GLacrosse) April 28, 2026

The Lonetree, Colorado, native finished off her first regular season with the Nittany Lions with 45 goals and 22 assists, tied for the most goals scored on Penn State.

Throughout the year, Allen scored six hat tricks, including four five-goal games.

The freshman star kicked off the season with back-to-back five-goal performances in Games 2 and 3 against UMass and Bucknell, before scoring the game-winning goal in overtime against Pitt.

“This year alone she’s had a lot of success,” coach Kayla Treanor said. “Knowing Roc, it’s only going to continue to climb. Just when you think that’s it for her, there’s always going to be something.”

Ten days later, Allen scored her third hat trick in just her sixth collegiate game. A little over a month later, that high-scoring success continued with a near-upset over Maryland, where she scored five of the team’s 13 goals.

Under Thursday night lights against Oregon, Allen put up her final five-goal game of the season, before closing out the regular season with a hat trick against Rutgers.

“At the end of the day, there’s more to give and more to prove,” Allen said.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball freshman Allison Oneacre named top-ten third baseman by Softball America
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State Softball Allison Oneacre is a top-10 third baseman.

ao stayin in the spotlight.#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/Ex3pKOZpZW— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) April 28, 2026

Penn State has had a record-breaking season so far this year. With the new addition of freshman talent to the squad, Allison Oneacre has made her first year one to remember.

Softball America announced Tuesday afternoon its top ranks for third basemen. The Alpharetta, Georgia, native was ranked No.6 in the country.

Having one of the best freshman seasons in Penn State history, Oneacre has been unstoppable since putting on the blue and white jersey.

In her rookie season, Oneacre has a .0974 fielding percentage and a .388 batting average.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Weekly recap | Penn State softball continues historic 2026 season
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State had a successful week on the diamond, as it is in the final stretch of the 2026 season.

The Nittany Lions faced off against Saint Francis for their last nonconference game of the regular season on Tuesday, finishing with a rule-run victory. Securing a dominant 12-0 win in five innings, the blue and white tallied 10 runs in the bottom of the second.

Heading to Champaign, Illinois, to face off in its final road series of conference play, Penn State went up against the Fighting Illini for a Big Ten weekend.

Leaving Illinois victorious, as the blue and white were able to secure its third conference sweep of the 2026 campaign.

Within the past week, the Nittany Lions 2026 squad has stamped their names in the Penn State history books. Here is an inside look of what went right for the blue and white as it is close to the end of the regular season.

A record-breaking team

The 2026 softball season has been one of the best in Penn State history, and this past week the Nittany Lions continued to prove it.

Shortly after its run-rule victory against the Red Flash, the blue and white are currently ranked No. 1 in the nation in walks, as the squad has surpassed 250 walks drawn this season.

Penn State’s star freshman infielder Allison Oneacre was named a NFCA National Freshman of the Year top-25 finalist. The Alpharetta, Georgia, native has had a phenomenal season in the blue and white jersey, as she is just shy of a .400 batting average on the year.

Just before its series finale at Eichelberger Field, the squad continued marking its names in the history books by being the highest-scoring offense and owning the single-season RBI record, both surpassing 300 this year.

Closing their weekend against the Fighting Illini, three home runs in the series finale tied the record, then ultimately surpassed the single-season mark for homers. The 2026 squad set a new record of home runs with 76 balls out of the park so far this campaign.

Top Nittany Lions

It is no lie that the Penn State roster has a lot of young talent, as the 2025 signing class has been the biggest playmakers. It’s also been proven that the Nittany Lion lineup can produce strong appearances in the circle and at the plate.

Freshman outfielder Breanna Hanik had a time in her home state over the weekend. The Oak Lawn, Illinois, native launched a home run in every single game against the Fighting Illini.

Hanik has been productive at the plate, launching a two-run homer in the series opener and a three-run homer in the finale.

Oneacre continues to creep into the blue and white record books. The infielder’s performance Saturday afternoon proved just that, as she smashed her 17th home run of the season, while going 3-for-4 in the batter’s box with two RBIs.

The dominant performance from the freshman class didn't end there, as pitchers Brevyn Kellepouris and Mackenzie Duncan played their roles well in the circle.

Kellepouris got the start for Saturday’s game, as she pitched 2.1 innings and was able to keep the scoreboard clean by striking out an Illinois batter.

Duncan came in relief, pitching 3.2 innings, sending three Fighting Illini back to the bench, allowing only a single hit. The Winter Garden, Florida, native was not only able to keep a clean scoreboard to close out the game, but was able to keep zeros in the walk and run columns.

Making her return in the final stretch of the season, junior infielder Kaylie Walters appeared and quickly got back into the groove on both sides of the ball.

Walters was a force to be reckoned with Friday, as she went 3-for-4 at the plate with four assists, three putouts and a run scored. Making her return, starting in all four games this past week, the Philadelphia native had a flawless performance at shortstop.

The blue and white have gone 4-0 in the last week, making its overall record on the season 32-17 and 11-10 in Big Ten play. With a current four-game winning streak, the Nittany Lions are set to return to Happy Valley to host No. 4 Nebraska to close out the regular series.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: 2026 NFL Draft takeaways | What to make of all 8 former Penn Staters drafted
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Twenty former Nittany Lions declared for the NFL draft after a 7-6 season, but just eight of those former Penn State players heard their name called during the draft in Pittsburgh.

Despite multiple players sliding from where they were expected to be selected, streaks continued for the Nittany Lions, as it marked the ninth straight year with Penn State having at least two draft picks in the first two days.

It was also the fifth time in the last six years that at least eight players were selected in the draft, showcasing the overall depth of the team’s draftees this year.

Here’s a look at what’s next for all eight of the former Nittany Lions drafted in the 2026 NFL Draft.

Vega Ioane, Baltimore Ravens, 1st round

The first former Penn Stater off the board and the first interior offensive linemen drafted, Vega Ioane was selected with the 14th overall pick by the Baltimore Ravens.

Ioane provided reliability at left guard for two straight years, starting in every game he played in during his final two seasons.

The former Nittany Lion will likely immediately slot in as the starting left guard for the Ravens as the team made an emphasis to add protection to Lamar Jackson. He’ll push out Andrew Vorhees who was slated to start there before Ioane was drafted.

Baltimore ranked toward the bottom in sacks allowed last season, and the addition of Ioane, who didn’t allow a single sack during his two years as a starter will add strong blocking behind a run-heavy offense and a growing passing offense with Jackson at the helm.

Drew Allar, Pittsburgh Steelers, 3rd round

Somewhat unexpectedly, Drew Allar was the second former Penn Stater selected in the draft.

Allar was selected with the No. 76 pick in the third round as the fourth quarterback off the board.

The quarterback position is still up in the air for the Pittsburgh Steelers as they await the decision of Aaron Rodgers. If Rodgers doesn’t return and ends up retiring, Allar could be in for a competition for the starting job with Mason Rudolph and Will Howard.

Allar is in a strong position with new head coach Mike McCarthy, who’s been key in the development of Rodgers with the Green Bay Packers and the growth of Dak Prescott during his short stint as the head coach of the Dallas Cowboys.

If Allar can develop under McCarthy he could carve out a strong role with a chance to be a long term starter for the Steelers.

Drew Shelton, Dallas Cowboys, 4th round

Drew Shelton heard his name called early on in the third day, being selected by the Dallas Cowboys with their first pick of the day at pick No. 112.

Shelton showed signs early in his career, playing as a fill-in during his freshman season. While he provided consistency on the blindside, he likely won’t see immediate playing time.

The former Nittany Lion will begin as a strong depth piece for the Cowboys, with opportunities to work his way into a starting role if they move around linemen to fill in for the underperformers.

Dani Dennis-Sutton, Green Bay Packers, 4th round

The biggest faller of any former Penn Stater was Dani Dennis-Sutton, but his destination was a strong one and a good fit for him.

Dennis-Sutton was selected in the 4th round by the Packers at No. 120 and could push for playing time early in his career.

He will join a team who recently lost Rashan Gary to free agency and looks to replace his production. While Dennis-Sutton likely won’t do that immediately, he proved he could be a viable pass rusher opposite to an elite one during the 2024 season when he was the second defensive end alongside Abdul Carter.

Dennis-Sutton will be on the opposite side of a different former Penn Stater, where he’ll attack the quarterback alongside Micah Parsons. While Dennis-Sutton struggled with consistency in his final year, having the help of Parsons and others could help catapult his pass rushing ability.

Zakee Wheatley, Carolina Panthers, 5th round

Another player that slid in the draft unknowingly, Zakee Wheatley was taken with the 151st overall pick in the fifth round of the draft by the Carolina Panthers.

Wheatley was the lead safety in his final campaign with the Nittany Lions after becoming the “turnover king” in 2024.

In a game that’s growing into the utilization of more safeties, Wheatley slots in as the backup strong safety behind Tre’von Moehrig. Wheatley will join fellow former Nittany Lion Nick Scott in the Panthers’ safety room. Scott currently lines up as the starting free safety.

Wheatley’s ability to fly around the field could result in some more playing time, and with the growing need of safeties, he could make an instant impact despite being a fifth round selection.

Nick Singleton, Tennessee Titans, 5th round

Nick Singleton found himself in a good situation after getting selected with the 165th pick in the fifth round by the Tennessee Titans.

Singleton’s injury and dip in production last season likely dropped him a bit, but he ended up in a strong situation with a running back room of Tony Pollard and Tyjae Spears.

The Titans ranked 30th in the NFL in rushing yards per game, rushing for 93.5 yards per game in 2025, and with Singleton’s breakaway speed ability, that number could take a rise.

Every team is making an emphasis to have multiple running backs play, and with the underwhelming performance from the Titans’ running backs last season, Singleton’s likelihood to play in his rookie year is high, especially with him being fully back from his injury.

Zane Durant, Buffalo Bills, 5th round

Despite being undersized, Zane Durant was selected with the No. 181 pick in the fifth round by the Buffalo Bills.

Durant’s productiveness and strong testing helped him jump into the fifth round. However, the Bills’ defensive scheme slots him as a defensive end given his size.

He’ll be behind the likes of T.J. Sanders and Ed Oliver who’re the probable starters, but the defensive line is a consistently rotating position, allowing for a multitude of players to see time.

Kaytron Allen, Washington Commanders, 6th round

Kaytron Allen was the final former Penn Stater selected in the NFL draft, with the Washington Commanders snagging Allen at the 187th pick.

Allen’s speed likely played a factor in his draft position, as he didn’t test at either the NFL Scouting Combine or Penn State’s pro day.

He’ll join an intriguing running back room that features Jacory Croskey-Merritt as the starting back. Allen will have a chance to prove himself as a reliable tailback in any of the camps, with every running back on the team being at training camp to end the summer.

The Commanders have a player who’s proven himself as a consistent runner despite the questions of his testing numbers, with the film and production likely helping his odds of seeing playing time during the regular season.

title: Weekly recap | Penn State men’s volleyball comes up big in EIVA Tournament
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Last week, Penn State looked to take home its fourth-straight EIVA Tournament title.

On Wednesday, the EIVA Tournament began from Rec Hall, as the blue and white earned the No. 1-seed and hosting rights to the tournament.

Granted a first-round bye, the Nittany Lions played their first game of the tournament in the semifinal round against Charleston on Thursday. The five-set victory propelled them to the EIVA Championship on Saturday.

Facing the No. 6-seed Princeton squad, the Nittany Lions were able to earn the EIVA Tournament title after sweeping the Tigers 3-0.

Here’s a look at what happened last week in the EIVA Tournament.

Offense-heavy performance

The blue and white looked calm and composed on offense throughout the entirety of the EIVA Championship match, and played a solid offensive game in the semifinal, too.

While it took the blue and white some time to get into motion against Charleston, once it did, the offensive pressure was on.

Sean Harvey had 19 kills in Thursday’s match. This marked his seventh straight game with double-digit kills. The EIVA Player of the Year also tallied a service ace, finishing the match with a total of 21.5 points.

In the EIVA Championship, the redshirt sophomore had 12 kills, which tallied his eighth straight game with double-digit kills. He also had another service ace with zero service errors on the night.

Harvey wasn’t the only Nittany Lions that performed well offensively over the week, though.

Tristan Hassell had a strong game against the Golden Eagles in the semifinal. The freshman had 16 kills and hit .448 on Thursday. He totaled a massive 19 points and created a major impact in the five-set victory.

In total, the blue and white had 60 kills to 18 attack errors in the semifinal match. Even more remarkably, the EIVA Championship match saw 42 kills from the Nittany Lions, with only 9 attack errors, hitting a collective .398.

Lots of digs

While offense was a standout in both matches of the EIVA Tournament, no games can be won without equally as strong defense. And part of that defense comes from digs.

As always, Ryan Merk was a focal point for the blue and white’s defense. The libero had seven digs in the semifinal and 13 digs in the championship match.

Merk finished Thursday’s game in a three-way tie for the most digs on the Penn State squad. Bennett Wilson also marked seven digs, as did Harvey.

As a whole, the Nittany Lions had a total of 29 digs against Charleston and 35 digs against Princeton.

Strengths in blocking

While digs are important, blocks are just as critical to stopping the opposing attack.

In the semifinal against the Golden Eagles, Owen Rose tallied five blocks. He totaled the same amount in the championship match.

Rose has had a great season as a middle blocker. Currently ranked No. 1 in blocks in the NCAA, the redshirt senior has been consistently reliable for the Nittany Lions.

Jaxon Herr and Hassell also had solid blocking performances against the Golden Eagles in the semifinal, with each putting up three blocks.

Herr had an additional three on Saturday against the Tigers, too.

Across the board, it seemed the blue and white came ready to battle on both sides of the ball last week.

During Selection Sunday, it was announced that the blue and white are set to face UC Irvine in the first round of the NCAA Tournament. The match will be at 9 p.m. on Friday at Pauley Pavilion in California.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Column | So long Barcelona, hello Chick-fil-A
author: Daniela White | The Daily Collegian
description:
After my first week in Barcelona, I knew I would never want to leave. After my first month in the city, I loathed how fast time was moving; how quickly each day began and ended. But now that I have three days left in this beautiful city, I have never wanted to be home more now than ever.

Putting that thought into writing, however, fills me with an insurmountable amount of guilt. Why on earth would I want to go back to my quiet hometown where I’d be bored out of my mind, instead of hoping to stay here for just one more day longer? Well, here’s my answer: I am so out of my mind exhausted, and I want Chick-fil-A.

After four months of traveling nearly every weekend, drinking and clubbing until 5 a.m. at least twice a week and blowing through all the money in my savings account, I need a goddamn break. I need at least 48 hours in my room at home, in quiet solitude with nothing but Netflix shows and American fast food to keep me company.

After living with six girls for four months in a four-bedroom apartment, as opposed to four roommates in Penn State in a two-story apartment, I need a break from civilization and I need good, greasy calories.

And while eating tapas and drooling over the paella in Spain has been a great hallmark of my time abroad, I desperately need all the food I’ve been missing from home. Chick-fil-A, Chipotle, KFC (they have it here in Spain but it’s nowhere near the same), Moby Dick (a kebab restaurant in Maryland), Starbucks’ grilled cheese and Pink Drink, Kung Fu Tea, and plenty more. If I had a dollar every time I thought about any of these places, I’d be able to afford another weekend trip in Europe.

For my final days here, while lounging at the beach or walking along the beautiful streets alight with spring’s warm sun, I’ve been looking forward to the drive home from the airport when I arrive back in America. I’ve been looking forward to hugging my dog, stealing some food from the pantry and binge-watching movies in my room. Just having these thoughts makes me so guilty yet excited, but I learned I need to give myself grace.

I’ve been on a strict schedule of partying until the sun rises and hopping on flights at 4 a.m. for months now, and not only my mind, fizzled out with balancing classes, writing columns, applying to internships, keeping up friendships here and in the states and traveling on top of all of that, but my body too is exhausted.

The days I usually wake up around 9 a.m. have slowly turned to noon, my eyes fighting to stay awake as the day is already halfway finished for some. Sleeping here makes me feel even more guilty, like I’m wasting all my precious time here to catch another hour of sleep instead of walking around.

I’m in a constant battle of soaking up everything in Barcelona while making sure I keep myself sane and healthy. So yeah, I think a couple days in my childhood room would do me some good.

I also feel like no abroad student would want to admit all of this, but at this point, I don’t care. If I could suddenly transport home with a Chick-fil-A eight-piece chicken nugget meal with medium fries and a Diet Coke placed in front of me instead of spending another day here, it would be the easiest decision of my life.

I have loved my time in Barcelona so immensely. I have learned so much about the city and myself, and I know I’ll be begging to come back in just a few weeks. I know I’ll forever be changed, but I know that my time here has come to a bittersweet end.

I knew that the day would come for me to take one last Uber ride to the Barcelona El-Prat airport, and it just so happens that I want that to take place just a couple of days earlier.

MORE COLUMNS

title: ‘No longer the athlete you once have been’ | Former Penn State men’s lacrosse players reflect on season-ending injuries
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
A little over 20 minutes into Penn State’s first NCAA quarterfinal appearance in a few years, then-junior Jack Posey’s career changed in 2023 when he made a simple, routine play. As he planted his leg and made the cut, he fell to the ground in pain and knew something serious had happened.

The Nittany Lions were experiencing one of their best seasons in recent years, facing Army in the quarterfinals with a spot in championship weekend on the line.

For the South Riding, Virginia, native, that was a dream come true after coming in as an underrated recruit. With a full-time role in the starting lineup in 2023, the big game turned into a nightmare moment for him.

“I was filled with rage, anger, sadness and a heavy wave of disappointment,” Posey said. “We were one game away from the Final Four, and all the work I had put in, all the sacrifices, it almost felt like it was for nothing. It was just like, ‘how cruel can this world be?’”

The mental impact of a season-ending injury was once rarely discussed in locker rooms and the news. But it has now become part of everyday conversations as similar themes have surfaced this season.

Current athletes, including quarterback Drew Allar, faced intense scrutiny and heightened pressure this season, highlighting the mental health challenges tied to injury and time away from competition.

“If you’re the type of person that has a strong sense of social competitiveness, which many athletes get, you get frustrated like you’re almost being left behind,” Dr. Andrew Friesen, a mental performance consultant and associate professor at Penn State Berks, said. “It hits on mental health from a number of different perspectives, and the most obvious is the loss of physical capacity.”

This becomes even more prevalent when an athlete is dealing with a longer-term injury, or one that is lingering or persistent throughout the season, or even their career, Friesen added.

“When we’re looking at an actual loss of physical capacity, you’re no longer the athlete you once have been,” he said. “If you were the type of athlete that may have been on the cusp of making the jump to professionals, now you have a very easy excuse for someone to look you over. All these factors are all body blows that you have to absorb for your mental health.”

That same season, Kevin Parnham broke his collarbone in the season opener, missing nearly two months of action during his sophomore season.The year before, the Tewksbury, New Jersey, native started in all 14 games.

“Sitting on the sidelines through those practices (and games) is one of the worst things,” Parnham said. “That’s when you realize just how badly you want to get back.”

While Parnham returned at the end of March and played the rest of the season, including through the NCAA quarterfinal and semifinal that Posey missed, he experienced déjà vu his junior season.

Parnham started the season opener against Colgate the following year and broke his collarbone again, despite having a metal plate in his shoulder, ultimately missing over two months of action this time around.

“(Repeated injuries) take its toll because everyone comes here to play at a certain level,” Billy said. “They have certain expectations, and when those expectations aren’t met, it’s a challenge.”

When the two lacrosse stars were injured during their careers, those challenges were met with constant support from teammates, family, friends and the athletics department as a whole.

After Posey was helped off the field and into the locker room during the NCAA quarterfinals in Philadelphia, Pat Kraft, the Vice President for Intercollegiate Athletics, was one of the first people in the locker room to check on Posey.

“He was very accessible to the students,” Posey said. “He cared so much about us and our success and our mental health, just putting us in positions to be successful.”

In addition to Kraft being there to support Posey during the game, he continued to be a familiar face in his recovery process, meeting with him in the fall to check on his progress and overall well-being.

“He really means all that, and man, I can’t thank him enough,” Posey said. “He’s just an incredible leader of men and women. He was telling how the guys still needed me to go out and lead them. He came in and saw me crying, met me where I was and pushed me to be the leader that he knew I needed to be.”

During the long stretches of their careers where Posey and Parnham couldn’t compete, let alone practice, they began to find new roles within the team to use their expertise.

“We let (athletes) know that there’s other ways that they can help the team,” Dr. Gregory Billy, 15-year team physician for Penn State Athletics, said. “They can assist the team as an assistant or athlete coach, and they find that that role can be very gratifying for them.”

As the two progressed through their injuries and worked their way back to full strength, they finished off their collegiate careers and both landed spots in the Professional Lacrosse League.

“If they’re able to get through the hard part and deal with it from a mental point of view, they’re going to be stronger because of it,” Billy, who is also the primary care team physician for Penn State Football, said. “They’re not going to take things for granted. They’re going to appreciate things, and a lot of times, can be a much better teammate moving forward.”

At Penn State, student-athletes have access to a wide variety of resources through the Athletic Wellness and Therapy Services Department, including baseline mental health screenings, assessments on every athlete before the season and more.

“(Each school) is going to differ according to different NCAA recommendations that say mental health is important, but it’s up to each individual institution to offer their services,” Billy said. “At Penn State, especially in the last few years, we’ve really upgraded the capacity with counselors and mental health professionals to really provide the support we need for all the athletes.”

While Posey and Parnham never sought direct mental health support from the university and athletics department, they received support from their teammates and coaches frequently.

“They’re always just looking out for you,” Parnham said. “During those times of injury, you learn how much they care about you as a person, how much they love you. They do such a good job.”

While the resources are available at Penn State, arguments have been made that student-athletes don’t get the support that they need. Friesen feels, however, that the resources are readily available. Instead, it’s the athletes who aren’t actively seeking them out that’s the issue.

Since mental health isn’t as easily tracked or diagnosed as an injury in sports, the resources can be provided, but more duty falls on the athletes to utilize them.

“We expect mental health to be under attack in sports,” Friesen said. “We don’t have something as tangible as a broken bone to tell us that we’ve now reached the tipping point in terms of, ‘I need help,’ it’s a lot more hazy. We don’t have that compass.”

Editor's note: This story is part of the Penn State Student Media Network’s (PSSMN) 2025–26 enterprise reporting project. This year’s topic is mental health. PSSMN is comprised of CommRadio, The Centre County Report, The Daily Collegian, The Lion and PSNtv. Read more of the series here.

title: Penn State DE Jordan Mayer no longer with program
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Defensive end Jordan Mayer has departed from Penn State following two seasons with the Nittany Lions, a university spokesperson confirmed.

A former in-state 3-star prospect, Mayer committed to Wisconsin for his true freshman season. However, he didn’t appear at all in 2023 before transferring to Penn State for the 2024 season.

In 2024, Mayer primarily contributed on special teams, playing in all 15 games and recording 1.5 sacks. He had an assisted sack in the White Out against Washington on Nov. 9 and a solo sack against Maryland on Nov. 30.

The following season, Mayer was out for most of the year with an undisclosed injury.

Mayer wasn’t included in the roster page given to the media for the Blue-White Practice on Saturday. With 12 defensive ends, including Mayer and incoming freshman Elijah Reeder, there was a lot of competition for Mayer to contend with.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's basketball earns commitment from Georgia's Enjulina Gonzalez
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's squad is growing stronger.

The Lady Lions earned a commitment from former Georgia guard Enjulina Gonzalez on Monday, marking the sixth player on next year's squad.

Gonzalez spent one season with Georgia, averaging 3.2 points, 1.7 rebounds and 1.3 assists per game. The Miami native averaged 47.7% shooting from the floor.

Prior to her time with the Bulldogs, Gonzalez spent one year at Miami (Ohio), where she averaged 16.9 points per game. Gonzalez appeared in 30 games, making 26 starts.

Gonzalez began her career at Mercer, where she spent two years.

During the 2022-23 season, the freshman made 15 starts in 28 appearances. Gonzalez averaged 10.2 points per game and 51.2% shooting. In Year 2, she played just three games, missing the rest of the season to injury.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State WR Liam Clifford invited to Indianapolis Colts' rookie minicamp
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Clifford family might have a second son in the NFL.

Wide receiver Liam Clifford was invited to the Indianapolis Colts' rookie minicap.

Clifford’s collegiate journey was a roller coaster. Originally a 3-star wide receiver out of Cincinnati, Clifford joined his brother, Liam, and saw little in-game action. He redshirted as a true freshman and subsequently made eight catches for 89 yards in his second year.

Building up more and more playing time as he went along, he caught his first touchdown pass on Sept. 21, 2024. Later that season against UCLA, Clifford had a career day, making three receptions for 107 yards, including a career-long 57 yard catch.

His second and final touchdown of his career was on a blocked punt, which he scooped and ran in for a 6-yard touchdown, coincidentally against the Bruins in 2025.

Midway through his fifth year as a Nittany Lion, however, Clifford was pulled away from receiving duties and into the quarterback room due to injuries amongst the other signal callers, stunting his final opportunities to perform in college at his primary position.

“Overall, just being a guy ever since I've been at Penn State, being a guy who can plug in anywhere at receiver, playing all four positions across the board, outside in those slots,” Clifford said at Penn State Pro Day. “At the end of the year, even switching the quarterback and being able to kind of be a next man up wherever I need to be. Even on special teams, too.”

Clifford wasn’t expected to be drafted, and his time at Penn State didn’t give much for scouts to glean. But he'll still get a chance to make the NFL.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball final series to be streamed on Big Ten Network
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
All three games of Penn State's final regular season series against No. 4 Nebraska are set to be nationally televised on Big Ten Network.

not one game… not two… but THE WHOLE DANG SERIES. Catch us on BTN this weekend📺#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/eG2O9EV26U— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) April 27, 2026

This comes after the trio of games was initially scheduled to be streamed for subscribers of Big Ten Plus.

This also calls for some schedule changes. The series kicks off at 5 p.m. Friday as planned, Saturday's contest is now set for 2:30 p.m., with Sunday's finale set for a noon first pitch.

title: Penn State softball's Breanna Hanik named Big Ten Freshman of the Week
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another Penn State rookie is making waves; outfielder Breanna Hanik was named Big Ten Freshman of the Week, for the first weekly accolade of her career.

bree bree’s built diff.#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/5JmNdKYm7f— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) April 27, 2026

In three games against Illinois last weekend, Hanik went 7-of-12 with three home runs and seven RBIs.

Her batting average across the trio of games totaled .583, with an OPS of 2.032.

title: Penn State notebook | Softball coach Clarisa Crowell talks sweep of Illinois, upcoming season finale
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State swept Illinois in its final series of April. This was a crucial sweep for the Nittany Lions, who now own an 11-10 conference record and stand at 32-17 overall as they make a push toward the postseason.

The blue and white cap off its 2026 regular season this weekend, welcoming No. 4 Nebraska. Coach Clarisa Crowell met with the media on Monday to discuss the team's record-breaking weekend as well as the outlook for its season finale.

Resiliency and broken records

Penn State had a task last weekend: to go 1-0 three times. The Nittany Lions completed their assignment and outscored Illinois by a 26-4 margin across all three games.

“It just shows the toughness and the resiliency of our team, and I'm just proud of them,” Crowell said. “It doesn't matter who the team is and what the record says; it's hard to beat any team three times.”

The Nittany Lions didn’t just sweep the Fighting Illini, but they broke some records while doing it. The blue and white set new single-season records with its 355 runs scored and 75 home runs, both of which were originally broken just a year ago. Additionally, Penn State now has 315 RBI, tying last year's team for the most in a season.

“All the credit to our kids, we had a great weekend, we broke some records along with it,” Crowell said. “We don't like to talk about it all that often; however, when it does happen, you know, we certainly acknowledge.”

Hanik shines in home state

Freshman outfielder Breanna Hanik, a native of Oak Lawn, Illinois, produced a stellar performance in her home state.

“I feel as if oftentimes we're always talking about Allison Oneacre and Brooke Klosowicz, but Breanna Hanik has really stepped up for us, offensively and defensively, in particular this past weekend,” Crowell said. “She had a tremendous weekend, and it's hard not to mention her.”

Hanik went 7-for-12 across all three games, totaling seven RBIs and knocking a home run in each game. The freshman’s long ball in Game 3 was the record breaker for Penn State.

“She’s jumping out of the stat sheet and it's hard not to notice it,” Crowell said. I mean, the kid hit .583, three home runs and slugging percentage was over 1.000.”

Defense

The story of Penn State’s season has been its surging offense, but against Illinois, its defense showed out as well.

Notably, in Friday’s opener, the Nittany Lions turned double plays in back-to-back innings, making sure the game's momentum stayed on their side.

“We've never turned two double plays in a game, so that was a big deal for us. Our defensive coach, Coach Foster, says this all the time, it's a momentum killer when a defense can do that to an offense,” Crowell said. “I mean, if we don't make those double plays, the game might be different; that's how impactful both of those plays were.”

Crowell credited these defense plays to having Kaylie Walters back in the field at shortstop. The junior returned from injury in mid-April after being sidelined since early March.

“I’m not sure we make those plays if Kaylie is not at shortstop because of just how good she is,” Crowell said. “We got our shortstop back, and it's certainly made a huge difference.”

Nebraska series

Penn State is closing its 2026 campaign with a tough test, welcoming No. 4 Nebraska. The Nittany Lions have been battle-tested this season, having already played four teams that are currently ranked in the top-25.

“We’ve played a pretty tough schedule, so our kids are well prepared for this weekend,” Crowell said. “We're as ready as you can be to play Nebraska, and we're excited for the challenge.”

The Cornhuskers own a 1.94 ERA, which is the best mark in the Big Ten and also ranks fifth nationally. Additionally, Nebraska’s .338 team batting average is third-highest in the conference.

This being said, Crowell’s squad isn’t worried about the pressure falling on their shoulders.

“Honestly, when I look at this series, the pressure really is on Nebraska, so for us, in a way that's a good thing, because we can just go out there and compete,” Crowell said. “This past weekend, we needed to win that, and this weekend is, hey, we'll want to go 1-0, but they're the ones that's kind of buying in for the championship. So we're just excited we get to come home and be back on Beard Field.”

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State punter Gabe Nwosu earns rookie minicamp invite from Pittsburgh Steelers
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After earning the starting role in 2025, punter Gabe Nwosu will have a chance to earn a spot in the NFL.

Nwosu was invited to the Pittsburgh Steelers rookie minicamp.

At 6-foot-6, 313 pounds, Nwosu was a dominant punter with 15 of his 38 attempts being pinned within the 20 yard line. The Maryland native averaged 46 yards per punt with a career-long 68-yard punt coming against Michigan State in November.

While Nwosu has only punted for one year with Penn State, he’s been the starting kickoff specialist for three seasons, sending 36 of his 74 kicks for touchbacks in 2025.

“It’s a blessing being able to not experience it alone, but experience with your brothers too,” Nwosu said at Penn State Pro Day, referencing long snapper Tyler Duzansky and punter Riley Thompson. “There’s not too many specialist rooms in America that are able to do what we’ve done, so it’s been a blessing to do that.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State men's basketball forward Hudson Ward commits to Idaho
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Former Penn State forward Hudson Ward is set to join his third team in his collegiate career.

Ward has committed to transfer to Idaho after spending last season with Western Michigan, his agency WEAVE announced Monday. He has two seasons of eligibility remaining.

Together with Hudson Ward we are excited to announce his commitment to the University of Idaho. #WEAVE 🧶 pic.twitter.com/Z5YsclQc0B— WEAVE (@WEAVE) April 27, 2026

The Alberta, Canada, native played in 13 games for Western Michigan this past season, averaging 4.5 points and 3.4 rebounds per game. He entered the transfer portal on April 6, following the team's head coaching change from Dwayne Stephens to Kahil Fennell this offseason.

Ward spent just one season in Happy Valley, tallying five rebounds, one assist and one block across eight appearances in 2024-25. He was a 247Sports composite 4-star in the 2024 class and the program's seventh-highest rated recruit in history.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Pitaya opens in downtown State College
author: Kelsey Bonifer | For The Collegian
description:
Pitaya, a women's fashion boutique, is opening a new location Tuesday at 324 E. College Ave., in the former Auntie Anne's space.

Pitaya is known for its trend-focused apparel and regularly changing inventory. Whether customers are looking for a weekend wardrobe or stopping by between classes, the boutique wants to offer a welcoming shopping experience to everyone.

The store will open at 10 a.m., but early arrivals are welcome.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: SMU hires former Penn State women's basketball head coach Coquese Washington
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn State head coach has a new gig.

SMU is hiring Coquese Washington as an assistant coach, the team announced Monday.

Washington went 209-169 while at the helm of the Nittany Lions' program from 2007-19. During her stint at Happy Valley, Penn State won three Big Ten titles and appeared in four NCAA Tournaments.

After departing the Nittany Lions, Washington was on staff at Oklahoma and Notre Dame before becoming the head coach at Rutgers from 2022-26.

"I am honored to join the SMU Women's Basketball staff, partnering with (head coach) Adia (Barnes) to build this program into an ACC powerhouse and perennial NCAA Tournament team," Washington said in a news release. "Adia's competitive fire and championship pedigree make this a truly special opportunity. SMU has the resources, commitment and leadership needed to compete at the highest level. I'm incredibly excited to bring my experience, energy and passion to a program with championship aspirations."

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

author: The Daily Collegian Football Staff
description:
On this week’s episode of "The 107k Podcast," co-hosts Lexie Linderman, Andrew Mercer and Noah Aberegg discuss their takeaways from Tuesday’s media availability, before diving in on what they’re paying attention to at Blue-White Practice on Saturday.

To round it out, the crew gives one bold prediction for Penn State players at the NFL draft this weekend.

title: Former Penn State OL Donovan Smith announces retirement from NFL
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
A former Nittany Lion has announced the end of his career.

Offensive tackle Donovan Smith announced his retirement from the NFL on Sunday, officially ending his nine-year playing career.

Smith played for Penn State from 2011-14, starting in 22-of-23 games during his final two years in Happy Valley.

The Maryland native was drafted by the Tampa Bay Buccaneers in the second round of the 2015 NFL Draft. Smith played eight years with the Buccaneers, all as a left tackle protecting the blindside for the likes of Jameis Winston and Tom Brady.

Smith was a part of the Buccaneers' Super Bowl team in 2021 and earned his second ring in his final year of action with the Kansas City Chiefs in 2023.

Across his nine years, Smith started in 136 games, starting every game he's played in.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Stock up, stock down | Young players emerge, backup quarterbacks struggle at Blue-White Practice
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Practice No. 15 is over.

Penn State wrapped up spring ball with Blue-White Practice on Saturday, giving fans a look at the new era under Matt Campbell.

Here’s what’s trending up and down for the Nittany Lions with 131 days until the season opener.

Stock up: Josiah Zayas

Although it lost A.J. Harris and Elliot Washington II this offseason, the cornerback room returned most of its important talent, with Audavion Collins, Zion Tracy, Daryus Dixson and Jahmir Joseph all coming back to Happy Valley.

But one freshman cornerback made the biggest play of Blue-White Practice. Freshman Josiah Zayas recorded one of the team’s two interceptions during the 7-on-7 and 11-on-11 reps, returning the pick nearly 50 yards for a touchdown.

“I've definitely seen a lot of maturity from him,” Tracy said. “He was a younging that came in, was asking questions that certain freshmen wasn't asking, or his head was in certain places where you wouldn't think with him being that young, coming from high school is going to be thinking about football.”

The 4-star cornerback was initially committed to Matt Campbell at Iowa State but decommitted once he left and pledged to Penn State in January.

Zayas is one of seven cornerbacks on Penn State’s squad with a year or less of collegiate experience, meaning his highlight play on Saturday was a step in the right direction for his young career.

Stock down: Backup quarterbacks

Penn State had just one quarterback from last year’s team return for this year in redshirt junior Jack Lambert. Five quarterbacks have since transferred or committed to the Nittany Lions, providing numerous options as Rocco Becht and Alex Manske continue to recover from injury.

Despite Connor Barry, Peyton Falzone, Jack Lambert and Kase Evans all participating in various parts of Saturday’s practice, none emerged unscathed.

Barry had the most successful day with two touchdowns — one rushing and one throwing to tight end Brian Kortovich — but still struggled with a cross body interception during the 7-on-7.

Falzone came to Penn State as one of two commits on National Signing Day in December, but also threw an interception on a near 50-yard pick six.

While Lambert and Evans didn’t produce turnovers, they had their fair share of youthful moments. Among the struggles was what would’ve been a sack for Evans during the non-contact portion of 11-on-11.

Stock up: Zay Robinson

With wide receivers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen continuing to recover from injury, Penn State sought production from some of its younger wide receivers on Saturday.

Among the receivers to stand out was redshirt freshman Zay Robinson, who transferred to Penn State from Iowa State.

Robinson participated in a few of the first-team drives, stepping up in the absence of the two injured receivers. During one of the team’s two 7-on-7 drives, Robinson reeled in a pass from Becht, taking it in for a touchdown.

Despite a wide receiver room with nearly a dozen options for the quarterbacks, dropped passes and errors made by some allowed Robinson to stand out from the rest of the pack on Saturday.

Stock down: Future of Blue-White Game

Since the first intrasquad spring scrimmage in 1951, Penn State’s annual spring game has undergone a variety of changes.

The Nittany Lions took the field for the first-ever Blue-White Practice on Saturday, combining live scrimmage reps with stretching and individual drills.

While the event brought in far fewer fans than previous years, a few thousand were still in attendance despite cooler weather and pouring rain.

Penn State’s lack of an actual spring scrimmage is standard with trends across the country, but is a step in the wrong direction for a potential Blue-White Game return in the future.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State WR Malik McClain signs with New York Jets as UDFA
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Nittany Lion is expected to go to the big leagues.

Malik McClain, who played at Penn State in 2023, is expected to sign with the New York Jets, according to Matt Zenitz.

During his time in college, McClain suited up for Florida State, Penn State and Arizona State, combining for 26 starts.

McClain had his best scoring seasons with the Seminoles, scoring two touchdowns during his freshman year and three in his sophomore year. At Penn State, he recorded six catches for 71 yards and a touchdown.

McClain redshirted his first season at Arizona State, playing in just six games with two catches for a touchdown, including a 42-yard touchdown reception from running back Cam Skattebo in the College Football Playoffs.

In his final season with the Sun Devils, McClain made his biggest impact, tallying 31 receptions for a career-high 441 yards and one touchdown.

title: Former Penn State women’s basketball guard Kiyomi McMiller transfers to Florida
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the third time in as many years, former Penn Stater Kiyomi McMiller has found a new home.

McMiller announced her commitment to Florida on Monday, joining former Lady Lion Moriah Murray with the Gators.

The Silver Spring, Maryland, native spent the past year with the Lady Lions, where she set multiple top-10 marks in program history, recording 21.6 points, five rebounds and 4.5 assists per game.

Prior to her time in Happy Valley, McMiller became a star at Rutgers, where she started in 21 games her freshman year, leading the Scarlet Knights with 18.7 points per game.

Now, McMiller will have an opportunity to play for new coach Tammi Reiss and the Gators, who's coming off an 18-15 record last year.

gettin’ an early start on Sunday😎#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/a1p6wNktSU— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) April 27, 2026

title: Report: Penn State wrestling's Lucas Cochran enters transfer portal
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
After more than three weeks of the transfer portal being open, a Nittany Lion has put his name into the portal.

Penn State heavyweight Lucas Cochran reportedly entered the transfer portal, according to the FloWrestling portal tracker.

Cochran was stuck behind highly ranked heavyweights and 197-pounders during his time with the Nittany Lions. However, he has multiple ranked wins himself, proving his capabilities for his next home.

The Perry, Utah, native wrestled to a 36-8 record across his four seasons with a win over then-No. 8 Yuraslau Slavikouski while wrestling up to heavyweight and a victory over then-No. 27 Remy Cotton at 197 pounds, both in 2024.

Cochran was expected to be in a battle with Cole Mirasola this past season for the heavyweight spot following Greg Kerkvliet's departure due to eligibility, but Mirasola's win meant another year for Cochran as a backup.

He'll enter the transfer portal with one year of eligibility remaining and could provide a successful heavyweight to whatever team he transfers to.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another NHL draftee has landed in State College.

Justin Poirier, a standout forward who was selected No. 156 by the Carolina Hurricanes in the 2024 NHL Entry Draft, has reportedly committed to Penn State, per Brad Elliott Schlossman.

Maine F Justin Poirier (CAR 5th rounder) is headed to Penn State.Latest updates: https://t.co/CT4S5Ps7ry— Brad Elliott Schlossman (@SchlossmanGF) April 27, 2026

title: Senior Column | Leaving a piece of my heart in Happy Valley
author: Rachael Keller | News Editor
description:
On a very cold September morning in 2022, a completely different version of myself stepped foot on Penn State’s campus for the first time. A campus I had no desire to spend four years of my life at.

My dad and step mom convinced me to take the journey, with my dad insisting I just give the school a look and see the area.

At the risk of sounding too cheesy, it was love at first sight. The massive campus, downtown, people and air were all so quintessentially “college,” which was perfect for me.

When I got my acceptance email, I was at work and started crying in the backroom. I knew the next four years would be some of the best in my life, and I knew there was one organization I needed to join.

The Daily Collegian.

It was everything I wanted. I spent months reading articles, made sure to visit its booth first at the involvement fair freshman year and ensured my application was pristine.

Now, looking back at that young girl sitting in the office for her interview, I don’t even recognize her. See, when I first arrived on this campus and began my candidacy, I was a very shy, nervous person. I never liked talking to new people, I didn’t want to put myself out there and I second-guessed every decision I ever made.

Naturally, journalism was the perfect profession for me.

Despite this, I knew I had to grow because I loved writing and everything The Collegian did. I knew I needed to push down my fears and begin my life.

So, that’s exactly what I did. I began telling myself that embarrassment was just a social construct and I hit the town for my mock article.

When I joined the first News Budget meeting as a candidate in Fall 2023, I remember looking at all of the talented people around me. I felt like a minnow in a very big ocean, but I knew that I wanted to become a shark, or at least a bigger fish. Therefore, I, again, shoved down all my fears and picked up my very first, real article.

This article, “Professors share how ‘late’ scheduling procedures can impact learning,” is now framed in my apartment.

Over the years, I became a mental health and wellness reporter, and later a culture and identity reporter. These two beats gave me some of my best work, most cherished memories and impactful connections.

When summer 2025 rolled around, I knew I wanted a change, so I decided to apply for news editor and I got it. Spring 2025 was also when I decided to graduate a year early, which meant I needed to take 12 credits over the summer and do a Maymester.

This was one of the best summers of my life. Though it was a lot of hard work, late nights and rainy days in Ireland, I have never had more fun. I loved being a news editor and knew I would never want to stop. And that’s still where I am now, three semesters later.

If you would have told 16-year-old me she would be leading a team, she would have laughed in your face before asking you to go into a store with her because she was too nervous to go in alone.

If it wasn’t for the three years I’ve spent at the Collegian, I would have let fear and anxiety rule my life.

I want to offer some advice I could have used many years ago: Life will pass you by if you let it.

If you don’t want to go out because you’re tired, take a nap and get ready. If you’re afraid of embarrassment, don’t be; no one will remember in two weeks. If you are standing in the way of your own happiness, MOVE. No one else can dictate the course of your life, except you. Don’t be like me and wait until you're 18 to realize that fear is inevitable — you just have to swallow it and go at life headfirst.

The Collegian gave me my confidence, assurance in my career and, most importantly, a family.

Daniela White was one of the first people I ever met on staff. She was an anchor during my time as a reporter. Eventually, in fall 2025, she became my co-editor, where she became a sounding board for ideas, kind words and true friendship. I will always cherish her.

Nadya Solometo and I navigated our first semester editing together during the summer. Though we made some mistakes and asked hundreds of questions, I wouldn’t have asked for anyone else to spend my rookie semester with.

Now, in my last semester, I’ve had the privilege of co-editing with Mia Debelevich, who is a journalistic force. From our late-night phone calls, constant texting and laughing too loud in the office, Mia has made my last semester truly special.

To Kahlie Wray, Mercedes Hamilton, Sammy Oropeza and Teresa Phelan, thank you for filling my worst days with laughter and friendship.

What a privilege it is to be surrounded by accomplished, talented women.

Bridgette, Kaitlin, Gabby and Elle you all have watched my journey from the first year. There has never been a day when I doubted how much you all mean to me and how much you support me. Thank you for making my college experience, I love you all.

Mia B., Audrey, Carly, Austin and Ciara, there are not enough words in the English language to convey how much you all mean to me. Since day one, you all have been my family. I love you so much.

Lastly, there are two people who deserve the biggest thank you from me: my parents.

Dad, if it wasn’t for you, I would have never attended Penn State. You have spent my whole life pushing me to be the best I can, you’ve supported my path since day one and have always been one of my biggest supporters. I don’t know who I would be without you in my corner.

Mom, thank you for filling my life with love, laughter and music. You often tell me that you were “meant” to be my mom. I disagree. I have no doubt I’m the one who was meant to have the privilege of being your daughter.

We did it, everyone. The “baby” of every group is graduating!

MORE COLUMNS

title: Tie-dye, moonlight boating | This week at State College
author: Aly Cromar | For the Collegian
description:
As the Penn State Community reaches the final week of classes, preparing for final tests and projects may fill many students with stress. Here are some free events this week that could calm the worry of finals.

Monday, April 26

Charleston Lesson and Social Dance

The Penn State Swing Dance club welcomes students of all skill levels to join them on its weekly Monday night Charleston Dancing lessons. No partner or prior experience is necessary.

This lesson will be held from 7 to 8 p.m., followed by a dance from 8 to 9 p.m. More information and those interested can register here.

Tuesday, April 27

Plan Your Pomo with Peer Success Coaches!

Students struggling or wanting to get ahead in their studies are invited to a guided study session by the Chaiken Center for Student Success from 5 to 7 p.m. in Room 322 of Boucke Building.

The session is hosted by Peer Success Coaches and will focus on the Pomodoro study technique. Light snacks will be provided, with other sessions available the following Wednesday.

Wednesday, April 28

Tie Die & Destress

Counseling and Psychological Services (CAPS) invites students to visit them on the HUB Lawn from 12 to 2 p.m. to take a step back from the stress of finals and tie-dye a canvas tote bag.

All materials will be provided at no cost, and no RSVP is necessary.

Baseball Dollar Dog Night

Head down to Medlar Field at 6 p.m. to watch the Nittany Lions Baseball team as they take on the West Virginia Mountaineers for their annual Military Appreciation Night.

Hot dogs will also be available for $1 throughout the game, and entrance is completely free for all attendees.

Thursday, April 29

Moderated Conversation with Tarana Burke

Students are encouraged to attend a moderated conversation with Tarana Burke from 6 to 7 p.m. in Freeman Auditorium.

Burke, founder of the “me too.” movement will discuss her work addressing sexual violence and racial justice, as well as her role in supporting survivors. The event is hosted by the R-VOICE Center as part of Sexual Assault Awareness and Prevention Month.

Students can RSVP here.

Friday, May 1

Softball Military Appreciation

At 5 p.m., cheer on the Penn State Softball team at Beard Field as they compete against the Nebraska Huskers in their Military Appreciation game. Entrance is no cost for all attendees, and students are encouraged to come out and recognize Military service members.

Movin’ On

Students are invited to attend Movin’ On, Penn State’s annual end-of-year music festival at the IM Fields. Gates open at 3:30 p.m, with performances running throughout the afternoon and evening until 10:30 p.m.

The event will feature artists like Zara Larson and The Dare along with student performers, food vendors, and activities. Movin’ On is free, but attendees must be 18 or older and present a valid Penn State or government-issued ID for entry.

Saturday, May 2

Moonlight Boating

Students are welcome to join Campus Recreation and Nature & Art RX at Stone Valley Recreation Area from 8 to 10 p.m. for a moonlit boating trip. All equipment is provided, and participants can choose between a canoe and a kayak.

Registration is per boat, so only one person per group needs to sign up. Transportation is not provided. Those interested should register here.

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball closes out West Coast series with a scoreless loss to Oregon
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Despite holding a lead in the last two games over No. 19 Oregon, Penn State couldn’t close out the wins late and lost two backbreaking contests to the Ducks. However, the Nittany Lions had a chance to close out the series strong and avoid the sweep on Sunday afternoon.

Oregon took the lead first and never looked back, securing the series sweep as the Ducks shutout Penn State in a 5-0 win.

Unlike Friday and Saturday’s games, the blue and white’s offense was shut down. Despite left fielder Jayden Davis and designated hitter Michael Anderson delivering back-to-back leadoff singles, left-hander Miles Gosztola recorded two straight strikeouts and a groundball out to end the early threat.

Oregon’s offense also started slowly as right-hander Isaiah Shayter cruised through the Ducks’ lineup. The freshman walked Ryan Cooney with two outs and Drew Smith poked a perfect bunt hit up the third base line, but Shayter struck out Angel Laya to close out the scoreless first frame.

Both squads produced hits in the second, yet neither could open the scoring by plating them. Center fielder Maddox McDonald knocked a one-out single to left center, but was caught stealing for the third out.

Shayter faced some pressure when Burke-Lee Mabeus tugged a one-out double down the left field line. However, the young pitcher stayed poised on the mound and powered a pitch past Naulivou Lauaki Jr. for his second strikeout. Shayter threw a wild pitch and Mabeus advanced to third, but he struck out Elijah Cook for the huge third out.

There was not much offense until the fourth inning when Oregon finally cracked open the scoring and scored three runs. Smith opened the frame with a bang and smothered a solo dinger to left field to put the Ducks ahead 1-0.

Laya followed with a base knock through the left side and Shayter started to lose his control. Maddox Molony drew a four-pitch walk and Mabeus drilled an RBI double to left and extended Oregon’s lead to 2-0. The bleeding didn’t stop there as Lauaki lifted a sacrifice fly to right and the Nittany Lions found themselves in a 3-0 hole.

Coach Mike Gambino brought in right-hander Kyle Emmons after Shayter recorded a pivotal second out. The junior stopped the damage and swiftly retired the next Duck, Oregon pounced on him in the fifth.

Smith sparked a two-out rally by powering a double that tugged the right field line, and Laya drove Smith in off an RBI single shot up the middle to make Oregon’s lead 4-0. While Gosztola continued shutting down the Nittany Lions, the Ducks’ bats went right back to work in the sixth and added onto their lead.

Following Gambino’s ejection, Mabeus stayed hot and smashed a leadoff homer to right and pulled Oregon ahead further, 5-0. Though Mason Horwat and Dimond Loosli strung together a few scoreless innings, Penn State’s offense never woke up.

Gosztola surrendered just three hits and three walks, while totaling seven strikeouts over 6.1 innings. The Nittany Lions finished with just five hits and four walks as the Ducks’ pitching staff had their way all game.

Up Next

Penn State returns home to Happy Valley and hosts No. 15 West Virginia for its fourth Dollar Dog Night at 6 p.m. Wednesday. The game will be broadcast on the Big Ten Network.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: What’s next for Penn State after spring ball? Matt Campbell, others offer insight
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
With spring ball coming to a close at Beaver Stadium on Saturday, Penn State will now have some time off before picking back up in June.

Matt Campbell said Tuesday that he and the entire staff were planning to meet ahead of Blue-White Practice to assess each player on the roster and their strengths and weaknesses. Campbell confirmed Saturday that process has been completed and also offered some more insight as to what’s next for Penn State operationally.

“Knowing that the storm of a college football season's coming, how are we all aligned, everybody in our program, to what the communication style is going to be to every one of our players,” Campbell said. “We'll meet with every player Tuesday. Then on Wednesday through the finals, I'll meet with every player. I think the key is everybody being aligned to where is the growth, what do you have to do?”

Campbell said the next four months until Week 1 are “critical for this team to grow themselves forward.” But he added this spring was “positive,” sticking with the notion from last Tuesday that the Nittany Lions haven’t wasted a practice.

With two new coordinators at Penn State, there’s still plenty of work to do with installing the offensive and defensive schemes. It’s a bit easier for the offense, as the players who followed Campbell and Taylor Mouser know the playbook already, but there are still important playmakers who are still learning.

When the Nittany Lions return at the end of May, Campbell said they will put plenty of their effort into the schematics as they continue to grow into what the staff envisions as this year’s Penn State team, even if the scheme isn’t where Campbell thinks the most growth is needed.

“I still think the biggest growth we can make is our discipline and detail, to be honest with you, in everything we do,” Campbell said. “Can we continue to have great discipline and detail in our academics and how we finish in the classroom? Can we have great discipline and detail in humility, myself and all of us, in terms of evaluating what we just did? The end piece of it is probably most important. The storm is coming. We're going to have adversity.”

For the players, there’s still two or so weeks until the long May break they have when coaches are out recruiting. As one of the veteran leaders, Rocco Becht is thrilled with the opportunity they have to grow before heading home.

Specifically, Becht thinks these next few weeks will be “huge in the film room,” and he plans to “rep some stuff” outside if the weather permits.

“But really, right now, until this big may break, we'll be in the film room, watching back over spring ball, and as soon as we get back from May, that's when we'll hit out on the field,” Becht said. “We'll start doing routes, we'll start doing different types of throws and different types of coverages. And so I'm excited for that to progress with those receivers.”

But soon, everyone will go their separate ways for a bit. Brett Eskildsen and Koby Howard have a trip booked to Los Angeles to do some more work together, while others are set to return home to keep training.

When they return to Happy Valley, it won’t be “standoffish” as Cooper Cousins described it when they first were in the building together — it will be a family reuniting.

“We're just one big family. Everybody in that building is so close, and everybody in that building is so together,” Cousins said. “This has probably been one of the closest teams I've been a part of, and it's not just seniors hanging out with seniors, it's seniors hanging out with freshmen or early enrollees. We're all one big family.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball to face UC Irvine in opening round of NCAA Tournament
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
After winning its 38th EIVA Championship in program history, Penn State earned an automatic bid to the newly expanded NCAA Tournament.

On Selection Sunday, the Nittany Lions heard their name called early as it was announced they would face UC Irvine in the opening round of the NCAA Tournament, which is played on campus sites.

The blue and white will square off against the Anteaters in sunny California as UCLA hosts the two programs, using its first-round bye to wait and see which opponent it will face on its home court.

Penn State and UC Irvine go head-to-head on Friday at 9 p.m. inside the Bruins’ Pauley Pavilion. UCLA is also hosting the Championship match, which will be played at the same arena on May 11.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball secures series sweep over Illinois with high-scoring road win
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
After a shutout win against Illinois yesterday, Penn State clinched a playoff spot for the Big Ten Tournament in Maryland. They hope to win again today against the Fighting Illini for a strong close to the Big Ten regular season.

Penn State dominated and closed out the series with a 10-3 win against Illinois.

Sophomore right-hander Danika Frazier started in the circle for the Fighting Illini and immediately caused Kaylie Walters to strike out. But once Breanna Hanik took to the plate, she chipped it to left field and made it to first.

Allison Oneacre took to the plate and hit the ball to first, but got out, which allowed Hanik to make it to second. To keep the momentum going, Brooke Klosowicz chipped it to the infield for an RBI single, leading Hanik home for a 1-0 lead.

Bridget Nemeth got the nod to start for the blue and white and was dominant in the first. Allowing no hits in the first inning for the Fighting Illini.

The second inning was quiet for both offenses, with neither team scoring.

The blue and white went back to the top of the lineup to start the third, as Walters led off with a hit to right field, but got out. Oneacre got a walk, but nothing came out of it as the Nittany Lions got another scoreless inning.

It was another scoreless frame, as neither team could get anything going in the third.

Bats came alive for the Nittany Lions in the fourth as Macy Chamberlin made it to first with an error. Gradishar kept the energy going with a hit to midfield, allowing Chamberlin to make it to third.

Natalie Lieto drew a walk, and while the play unfolded, Gradishar successfully stole second, and Chamberlin came home on a pitcher’s error to make it 2-0.

Frankee Flesher hit a fly ball to left field for the out, but Lieto stole second, her seventh of the season. Barbanente then delivered with a deep shot to the outfield, bringing Gradishar home to make it 3-0.

Jordan George stepped to the plate and got a 3-run home run, marking her first home run of the season and raising the score to 6-0. While simultaneously tying the team's single-season home run record with 74.

The Fighting Illini Coach Tyra Perry called a timeout to switch pitchers, putting Junior right-hander Megan Nuechterlein in the circle. Nuechterlein was able to shut down the Nittany Lions' batters for the rest of the fourth.

Illinois came out swinging in the fourth with a solo home run by Eileen Donahue, making the score 6-1.

Nittany Lions coach Clarisa Crowell gives Nemeth a break and swaps to Mackenzie Duncan to start the bottom of the fourth. This left the Fighting Illini scoreless for the rest of the inning.

Another scoreless inning for both teams in the fifth as the Fighting Illini hope to step it up in the sixth on both sides of the ball.

The blue and white had other plans as Barbanente skipped it left field, causing Moore to make an error and made it to first. Walters followed up with a similar hit to left field, also making it to first.

Hanik stepped to the plate and instantly got a three-run home run, making it her third of the series and third day in a row. The score is 9-1, as Hanik’s home run broke the team's single-season home run record.

With this came another pitcher swap for the Fighting Illini, with left-hander Abby Sabalaskey coming in.

Oneacre got a walk to first, and Klosowicz then hit a line drive to first but got out. A call was made that said Oneacre was also out. This caused coach Crowell to challenge the play, but she lost the challenge.

Chamberlin came in swinging and got a solo home run. The score stood at 10-1, with four runs in the sixth inning.

The Fighting Illini were not giving up, as Ellie Haggard hit it to the outfield and, with an error by Walters, made it to first. Keirys Click hit it deep into center field for a triple and an RBI. Haggard made it home, making the score 10-2.

Gabrielle Debevec hit it to right field, sending Click home.

Coach Crowell made another pitcher swap in the game to McKenna Young. The Nittany Lions were able to hold off the Illini for the rest of the inning.

Illinois was able to hold off the Nittany Lions in the seventh with no runs.

Abigail Britton was put in during the seventh and closed out the game for the Nittany Lions. Allowing for a flawless sweep against Illinois.

Up next

Penn State returns home for a three game series against Nebraska. First pitch for Game 1 of the series is set for Friday at 1 p.m.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Annual Lavender Recognition celebrates LGBTQ+ graduates
author: Abena Kedjanyi | For the Collegian
description:
The Center for Sexual and Gender Diversity (CSGD) at Penn State celebrated the 25th annual Lavender Recognition on Saturday at Alumni Hall in the Hub-Robeson Center.

The ceremony honors graduating students and allies from the LGBTQ+ community for their leadership, resilience and impact on campus. Decorated in purple and white, the Hall had rows of chairs facing the stage for the ceremony, while dinner tables were arranged behind them for the reception that followed.

Among the scholarship awards was the LGBTA Alumni Interest Group Award, which was awarded to Alex Massa.

Massa, a third-year PhD student studying meteorology and atmospheric science, said the community’s work is deeply rooted in collaboration, relying on the efforts of a wider network rather than individual action alone.

“I have such a great passion for what I do and really when I do it, it's really to support those who come after me,” Massa said. “As I navigated my academic journey, I faced a lot of barriers and struggle in order to succeed and because of that I wanted to pursue work outside my degree that would help to bring some of those barriers down for those who come after me.”

For him, this recognition affirms that the collective work being done alongside peers and community members carries real significance and contributes to something larger. He said it makes him feel like the work he’s been doing is genuinely helpful.

The event opened with remarks from Sonya Wilmoth, director of CSGD, who welcomed guests and students and provided a brief overview of the event and its purpose. Graduating students paid tribute to their significant supporters with appreciation notes and pink roses, creating emotional moments throughout the ceremony.

Ryan Jones, interim leader of the Penn State Alumni Association, followed with brief remarks welcoming the graduates into the alumni community. The ceremony concluded with final speeches, after which attendees moved to a reception to eat, socialize and take photos.

“I think Penn State is a place everyone can find their people, where everyone can find their community,” Jones said. “It is a place that is going to encourage and support fellow Penn staters no matter their identity, their interests, or their experiences.”

Jones said while most interactions with students occur as they approach graduation, Alumni Association staff also collaborate with colleagues across campus through various programs, which provides a broader understanding of student life and the overall student experience.

When students are part of a supportive community, they’re able to build meaningful connections, he added. And, as they graduate, he said students can recognize this community continues as part of a wider alumni network.

Jones said the alumni network has long supported what’s called “affinity graduation recognition ceremonies and events,” and Lavender Recognition is one of many the network supports.

“We strive to be a really inclusive organization and we want to represent all alumni no matter what their experience, identity or community is so it's really important that we are able to show that kind of support through events like this,” Jones said.

Bob DiFrancesco attended the event to support his son, Dominic DiFrancesco, a fourth-year studying marketing and communications. Bob said he appreciated the university’s continued commitment to diversity amid today’s social climate.

“I think it's nice to see him being himself and not having to hide his identity, to be with his peers and have people that are also in the queer community, that he has their support, and just seeing him smile, seeing him happy,” Bob said. “In his high school years he wasn't out, so to speak, so it is just nice to see him be himself and be happy.”

He also said it’s meaningful for Penn State to maintain programs that recognize and support LGBTQ+ students.

John Romero Mora said he attended the event to support his roommate.

They met after Mora posted on Facebook looking for a place to stay, and his roommate reached out upon realizing they were both from Colombia. He added that he wanted to be there to show his support, as his roommate had recently completed his PhD and had also been a strong source of support for him.

“What I like about Penn State specifically is that you see a little bit of diversity around, and that is beautiful,” Mora, a third-year PhD student studying applied linguistics, said.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Former Penn State CB Cam Miller signs UDFA deal with Carolina Panthers
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
A former Penn State transfer has his first NFL contract.

Cornerback Cam Miller has signed an undrafted free agent deal with the Carolina Panthers following the conclusion of the NFL draft, he announced on Instagram.

During his time at Penn State, Miller was a rotational cornerback, only starting in five games across three seasons. The former Nittany Lion found the most success at Penn State in his junior season, starting in four games while tallying 27 total tackles and breaking up five passes.

However, he was still seen as more of a rotational player as the team utilized numerous defensive backs.

After his junior season, Miller transferred to Rutgers for his final year. In Piscataway, Miller started every game, amounting 47 tackles and an interception. He was a mainstay in the Scarlet Knights’ secondary, and that play earned him the contract.

Miller will have a chance to earn a spot on the Panthers, potentially as a depth piece or the practice squad. He will also reunite with Zakee Wheatley, who was drafted by Carolina in the fifth round.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Pair of Penn State men’s volleyball players step up for win in EIVA Championship
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Sean Harvey and Owen Rose dominated the court for Penn State in the 3-0 EIVA Championship win over Princeton.
