Former Rutgers assistant coach Danielle Edwards was hired as Penn State's newest assistant coach and director of recruiting on Friday.

Welcome to Happy Valley, @edwards__dani! 🦁#WeAre pic.twitter.com/EI45Qw5siu— Penn State Women's Basketball (@PennStateWBB) April 24, 2026

Edwards spent last season with the Scarlet Knights as an assistant coach and director of recruiting operations.

Prior to her time in Piscataway, New Jersey, she spent four years at SMU. Edwards' time there began as director of player personnel before becoming an assistant coach and recruiting coordinator.

During the 2022-23 season, Edwards helped the Mustangs to her best season with a 17-13 record.

Before her time with SMU, Edwards was the director of player personnel at Michigan for two seasons for her first coaching opportunity.

Edwards graduated from Clemson in 2019 following one of the best playing careers in Tigers' history. The Perry Hall, Maryland, native made 123 starts, the most in program history, finished top-five career steals with 291 steals and finished 14th in all-time scoring with 1,274 career points.

Edwards was a two-time All-ACC Defensive Team selection and helped her team to an NCAA Tournament second-round appearance in her senior year.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State announces time change for Blue-White Practice
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Impending weather is shaking up the start times for Penn State's Blue-White Practice festivities on Saturday.

Penn State announced the Blue-White Practice will now begin at 12:15 p.m., as opposed to the previous 1 p.m. start time.

Also affected by the weather is the autograph session, which has been moved to after the practice as opposed to before.

Team arrival has been pushed back from 10:45 a.m. to 11:45 a.m., but gates will still be opening at 11 a.m.

The Beaver Stadium Block Party will continue to run as scheduled from 9 a.m. to 1 p.m. with food trucks, face painting, live music and more.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball offensive players star in EIVA Tournament Semifinal
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s young offensive players made all the difference in its critical 3-2 win against Charleston in the EIVA Tournament Semifinal on Thursday.

The Nittany Lions went five sets in the must-win match, gritting out a win to advance them to the EIVA Championship on Saturday.

The first set wasn't the best outing for the blue and white for both the attack and the defensive game. With only a few kills and several block errors, the first set was handed to the Golden Eagles.

“I’m just so proud of these guys,” coach Mark Pavlik said. “We know it’s playoff time, we know that teams are ready for this. We know that they are going to leave everything they can on the floor, and we have to respond to that.”

While it took the Nittany Lions a full set to adjust to the action against the Golden Eagles, once they gave that response, it was all offense.

While several members of the Penn State roster had strong performances on Thursday, the young stars on the roster stood out.

To no one’s shock, Sean Harvey played yet another big game in the semifinal.

The redshirt sophomore had 19 kills against the Golden Eagles, which was the most of any Nittany Lion on Thursday. Harvey also hit a massive .567

And beyond his offensive performance, he had a well-rounded game. With seven digs and two blocks, he played a big part in the win against Charleston.

This isn’t anything new for the outside hitter. In fact, on Tuesday, Harvey was named EIVA Player of the Year because of the season he’s had with the blue and white.

“That award is a reflection on everyone on this team,” Harvey said. “It’s something more than just my name on that. That’s every one of these guys in this program.”

Harvey is among a few young, rising stars on the Penn State roster.

Right there with him on Thursday was Tristan Hassell. As just a freshman, Hassell has shown immense improvement over the course of the season

In Saturday's match against George Mason, the freshman put up a 26-kill game to mark his career high. And Thursday was just another example.

The second he hit the court, he made an impact. The opposite hitter had 16 kills against Charleston. Beyond that, he also put up a massive service ace and hit .448.

But Hassell is more than just a playmaker. He’s loud on the court, getting in the heads of his opponents and creating an unmistakable energy.

“He’s a firework,” Harvey said. “It could be 0-0, second set, we just won the first, and that dude’s screaming his head off when he gets a kill.

“The team gels around that,” Harvey continued. “When we see him come in, we’re going to go with him. He’s not going to be alone on that.”

“I knew from the beginning of the year he had something special to him,” Jaxon Herr said. “For him to jump up and give us some momentum while playing great at the same time, it just keeps us going that much more.”

The Nittany Lions will now look to face Princeton in the EIVA Championship on Saturday back in Rec Hall.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Love Story on FX | America’s obsession with the Kennedy family
author: Amelia Houck | The Daily Collegian
description:
FX’s “Love Story,” which premiered in February, was largely successful with younger Gen Z audiences. The nine-episode limited series is available for streaming on Hulu and Disney+.

“Love Story” introduced many new viewers to the tragic fate met by John F. Kennedy Jr. and Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy in July 1999, as their private plane, piloted by Kennedy Jr., crashed and landed in the Atlantic Ocean near Martha’s Vineyard.

Tragically, Bessette’s older sister, Lauren Bessette, was also killed in the 1999 plane crash.

Kennedy Jr. was a lawyer and a co-owner of a lifestyle and political adjacent magazine called George. He is the son of former U.S. President John F. Kennedy and Jacqueline Kennedy. Bessette-Kennedy was a fashion publicist who infamously worked for Calvin Klein and curated elevated looks for many celebrity clientele.

The series explained the couple was introduced by Calvin Klein himself, and thus began their whirlwind romance, from their secluded marriage on Cumberland Island to their tragic and untimely fate.

With the primary ongoing theme of the show focusing on how relentlessly and horrifically the paparazzi treated Kennedy and Bessette-Kennedy, through releasing scathing and false information about the couple, it begs the question: Is it ethical to stir all of this publicity up again with such an attention-grabbing limited series?

Needless to say, the Kennedy family has dealt with a lot of loss and heartbreak, and it sadly seems to persist. The family suffered after the tragic losses of John F. Kennedy and Senator Robert F. Kennedy in 1963 and 1968, respectively.

Yet, Americans still have a strong interest in following the Kennedy family even today.

Perhaps it’s a nostalgia for a more prolific time in American history, a more amicable political climate, or, on the contrary, whisperings of the “Kennedy Curse” as a means to try to understand the detrimental amount of loss this family has experienced.

Regardless, “Love Story” introduced a younger audience to a time where, instead of facing internet backlash, criticism or even cancellation, your name, your identity and your morals were tarnished on the front page of every gossip magazine or newspaper stand for all of the country to see.

Seeing conspiracies or false information about yourself in print objectively appears so much harsher, concrete and believable to those unbeknownst to you.

Whereas on social media and the internet, you can easily make a video or a statement about false information regarding yourself, and convey emotion regarding the circumstances to your followers.

However, it can be an uphill battle to come across as authentic over solely print, especially in Bessette-Kennedy’s case when the multitude of “bad” information printed and published about her severely overwhelmed the “good.”

An unfortunate underlying theme for Kennedy's tragic death is the importance of having a sense of personal privacy and the right to be a private citizen, different from a public one. (The series heavily addresses how Bessette lost these rights the second she married and became a Kennedy.)

So although “Love Story” seeks to tell the romantic yet calamitous tale of a young couple, it simultaneously opens a wound and proves the point that the media can be hostile and violate an individual's sense of personal privacy and how they live their everyday lives.

MORE BLOGS

title: From backyards to the big stage | Local Mojo hosts first ‘Mojofest’ in Happy Valley
author: Brennan Kramp I The Daily Collegian
description:
On a crisp spring day with clouds scattering the sky and a breeze coming down from the top of Tussey Mountain, the bands of the Mojofest lineup began their soundchecks for their big stage performances.

Local Mojo’s, Mojofest, held its inaugural opening on April 18 and featured several Penn State and Amherst based bands, along with local DJs. For a lot of these bands such as Penn State's 3:46 Sunrise and Sarasota and the Amherst locals, Morrissey Blvd, Mojofest represented a huge step in their careers.

Sarasota guitarist Mark Robertson said the event offered an opportunity to engage with the community that helped put on the festival, and those in attendance.

“We get to be up on stage, playing good music for people, then we get to go be with the people, listening to good music. It’s the best,” Robertson, a fourth-year studying Spanish and finance, said.

As 3:46 Sunrise listened to other bands' soundcheck, 3:46 Sunrise guitarist Matt Colannino said he used the moment to reflect on being a headlining band at Penn State’s first Mojofest after getting their start playing gigs for frats and bars.

“It’s been a fun, crazy, funny year,” Colannino, a third-year studying accounting, said. “We never thought it would come to this, playing for this many people.”

For pianist Jake Almstead, not only was this his biggest show as a member of the band, it was only his third. Almstead is the band’s newest member and they said his induction was a no-brainer. After watching a few videos, they invited him to play a practice and were “blown away.”

Almstead’s, a first-year studying business administration, first show with the band was for a packed fraternity house during parents weekend, followed by the “biggest” show the band ever played at Doggie’s Pub, and now he gets to jam out at Mojofest.

A show of this magnitude represents not just a great achievement for the band, but acts as part of a greater culmination of moments in their musical careers. Members like drummer Andrew Gille, bassist Sonny Vitureira and guitarist Trevor Brown have been surrounded by music their whole lives.

Not only was Penn State talent on display at Mojofest, but other bands like Morrissey Blvd and Philadelphia-based singer, Noah Richardson, came out to Happy Valley to spread their sound and join the State College music scene. Being an Amherst based band, Morrissey Blvd has been involved with Local Mojo from the beginning.

The three-brother core of drummer Wilson Morrissey, bassist Henri Morrissey and lead guitarist/singer Zan Morrissey have been hitting their musical stride since 2024, when they started taking their band seriously. After solidifying their place in the Amherst scene, the band has continued to spread their love of spirited performance and communal vibes.

As one of the original bands in the early rotations of Local Mojo shows, members of Morrissey Blvd said they’ve been able to deliver the band’s name and message to even more passionate fans, and fuse the crowd’s energy for live music with their unfettered ambition to create music that helps indulge “the fullness of life.”

“There is a desire for community around live music,” Zan said. “Musicians feeling connected to people and for that whole experience to bring people together and have a good time, that’s a desire that, it seems, a lot of young people are sharing right now.”

The festival commenced at 2 p.m., and the crowd was enthralled from the first note that left the speakers. As bands and DJs continued to captivate the audience, dark clouds began to circle over the mountain during Morrissey Blvd’s set — blocking out the sun but never dulling the zeal of the crowd.

Soon enough, rain began to pour down, but Branden Zajaczkowski said it only seemed to fuel the force of both the band and the audience.

“Dancing in the rain, listening to music, was easily the highlight of my year,” Zajaczkowski, a third-year studying aerospace engineering, said.

Local Mojo founder, Charley Blacker, came into State College three days prior to help finalize the details for the festival, making sure that everything was in order for a good day of music.

This was the first Mojofest that would be held at another location outside of Amherst, so it was essentially a prototype for expansion. Throughout the festival, Blacker stood at left-stage, taking in the scenery.

“For a first year festival to get around 4,000 attendees to dance throughout the day in the pouring rain is a feat of its own,” Blacker said. “The local bands and DJs were incredible, and the production value was honestly jaw-dropping.”

With a combined effort from the bands, DJs and crew members, the festival came and went with a spectacular bang. Most left the venue with a smile on their face as they hung their arms around the shoulders of their friends. The pure joy emanating from the crowd made you wish you could be there all over again. Fortunately, Blacker said that’s a nearing possibility.

“We will be bringing Mojofest back to Happy Valley very soon,” Blacker said. “This upcoming festival is going to be something Happy Valley’s never seen before.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Senior Column | Finding my ‘Happy Place’
author: Nadya Solometo | Lifestyle Editor
description:
One of my favorite authors recently is Emily Henry. Known for her rom-com plots, witty dialogue and heartfelt moments, she writes books that feel like they can appeal to anyone.

My personal top pick of hers is the 2023 novel “Happy Place.” The book follows a woman named Harriet, who takes an annual trip to Maine with her group of friends. When the yearly vacation is announced to be ending, Harriet panics, thinking that if there’s no trip, there’s no reason for her friends to get together anymore.

I first read this book in the middle of my freshman year at Penn State, when I wasn’t sure what the next four years of my life would look like.

Now, as the clock runs down on my time here, I can confidently say I’ve garnered a love for every aspect of it.

Growing up, my “happy place” was always with my family — even if I didn’t always show it. My parents made it a habit to make every free night a family night, whether that meant going out for dinner, watching a new movie or just lounging around telling jokes.

I always had grandiose plans to leave home and go far away for school, so when choosing where to attend, it made sense for me to pick somewhere three hours away.

However, driving up to move in with my dad, not-so-expertly hiding tears behind his sunglasses, my mom’s voice wavering in every sentence and my sister squeezed too close to me in the back seat, I was a ball of nerves.

What if I chose the wrong school on a whim? How could I handle being in the middle of nowhere? All of it swirled in my mind until we pulled up to Martin Hall.

As the year went on and I found my footing on campus through friends and classes, I wanted to try my hand at writing. Journalism was something I knew I wanted to pursue in college, so at the Involvement Fair, I went in search of the right club for me.

Which is where I found the Collegian.

I’ve spent all four years of my undergraduate studies on lifestyle staff. First as a candidate, then a reporter and eventually, an editor.

Throughout my time on staff, all of my editors — Sarah and Victoria, Fernanda and Milan, Cearra, Ritamarie and Kaitlyn Murphy — were some of the best people I’ve had the privilege of working for.

Even though each year brought new challenges and changes, the Collegian was always a constant bright spot. Bringing in an article never felt like a chore, but a fun task each week.

During my time as a reporter, I learned firsthand about other people’s experiences and perspectives in college. Without the Collegian, where else could I explore clubs on fungi, archery or paleontology for a day?

When I got the opportunity to be an editor, I was more than thrilled. The position was a mini-goal I had set for myself as a freshman, so being able to hold the role for three semesters has been incredibly rewarding.

However, while the Collegian was certainly a large part of my time at Penn State, there’s also been so much more that encompasses it.

Sophomore year, I had the opportunity to study abroad in London, and it was one of the greatest times of my life. I’ll forever remember going to the Wetherspoon’s in Hammersmith near my flat, or taking the Piccadilly line on the tube to class every day.

Above all, it was there, and at Penn State, that I met some of the best people.

In “Happy Place,” there’s a quote from Harriet where she describes meeting her friends, saying, “The most important friendships in my life all came down to a decision made by strangers, chance.” This idea’s stayed with me.

All of the amazing friends I’ve made here were a matter of luck. Who could’ve imagined that just because a bunch of high school seniors from different places chose the same college, they'd become lifelong friends?

I’ve loved every single tailgate, trip, movie night and random weekend spent at Doggie’s we’ve had together. You’re what made college what it was — an unforgettable time.

I know we’ll be forever friends and, though this chapter is ending, our friendships aren’t.

To my fellow members of BBG (big back girl) Ave., these messages get a little bit sappier.

Michael, I always say I’ll never meet someone who matches my humor as much as you, and now I’m sad I won’t have you to laugh with every day. You’ve been such a bright, loud, infectious light (just like the ones in the arcade you call a room). It’ll feel dim in my life the minute we don’t live together anymore.

Thank you for always being in my corner, even though “respectfully,” I know at times I didn’t deserve it. I don’t think I’ll have more inside jokes with a person than you, and I don’t ever want to.

Kaitlyn, it’s hard to encompass just how much you mean to me. If it weren’t for you four years ago, pitching the idea of us living together at Penn State when all we did was share a lunch table, I would’ve missed out on one of the most important people in my life.

These past four years, we’ve shared a brain cell, a stomach and a home. Now, every other place I live will feel a bit emptier with you not in it.

I’ve loved being your roommate, formal date, DINK and will always call you my best friend. When I tell other people “I love my girl down,” I mean it every time, and always will.

I also want to shout out my family, who showed me what it means to miss people so much. You need to picture a “happy place” with them there.

To my Mom and Dad (who might be teary-eyed reading this), thank you. I wouldn’t be anywhere near able to accomplish all I have today without either of you. I’m grateful for the endless support, love and FaceTime calls you’ve given me throughout my time here.

Moving on to the next chapter with law school, I know you’ll be in my corner every step of the way again, with nothing but adoration and encouragement.

Anthony, I don’t think I’ve ever had a better guest all four years than you. Every tailgate or lunch we’ve had was always fun, and I’m glad to have a big brother like you who will always send an Instagram Reel at just the right time to make me feel better.

To Bella, we used to always quote that somewhat silly TV show, where the two sisters would say, “Sisters by chance, friends by choice.” However, it couldn’t ring truer for us.

If someone asks me the biggest achievement I’ve had in life, I’d easily say it’s been being your big sister. You’re becoming a brilliant, outspoken individual, and all the snarky comments and jokes we’ve shared throughout the journey, I’m hoping to continue.

In “Happy Place” by Emily Henry, as the book nears its close, Harriet comes to realize her “happy place” of a yearly group trip is less the location and more the people on it.

My time with Penn State has shown me my “happy place,” while many parts of it can be found here, was never meant to be something physical. It’s the people you love and make memories with that become what you cherish.

The Collegian has been one of those aspects, and something I’ll hold onto long after I leave State College for the last time.

To Rachael, my first editing partner over the summer, thank you for making the transition smooth and fun. There’s no one else I’d want to read six different articles about the same borough meeting with.

Kaitlyn Murphy, thank you for showing me how Lifestyle staff should be run, full of inventive ideas and silly icebreakers.

Katie, this past spring has been one of the best times I’ve had on campus, and it’s been largely due to you and our dynamic. I’ve been so proud of all the progress you’ve made as an editor, and I’m glad to be leaving lifestyle in your hands next year. Besides your questionable hear-me-out picks (bad boy Number Five), I know the staff will thrive with you as editor again.

Mercedes, Kahlie and Avery, it’s been such a pleasure working with you since the beginning of your time as editor-in-chief, managing editor and digital managing editor, respectively. I’ll always appreciate the hard work and dedication you’ve put into making the Collegian the best it can be.

To the lifestyle staff, thank you for the stories, blogs and food reviews you’ve brought in this past year. They’ve always been my favorite things to read in the week, and I’ve loved how you guys have been down for any article idea or insane icebreaker I’ve brought to budget.

Everyone else I’ve had the pleasure of meeting and befriending in the Collegian, thank you for always being kind and open to having a random conversation at the table, whether it be about reality TV or my obsessive love for coffee.

A few years ago my best friend from home, CC, and I were talking on the phone (like we do every day) and discussing Henry’s book. While rehashing the plot, she asked me what my “happy place” was.

At the time, I answered with a real location — our other best friend from home, Cara's house — and quickly rattled off the names of all our friends in our hometown.

Now, my answer has changed. Like Harriet, my ideal retreat isn’t a specific place, rather particular people. The backdrop doesn’t matter, but the bodies and memories that fill it do.

While the time in one of my physical “happy places” might be over, I know the people who’ve made it one will stay with me long after I leave.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Players to watch | Defensive line, playmakers headline new-look Penn State at Blue-White Practice
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is coming out of a monumental offseason with a coaching change and heavy roster turnover, bringing over 40 new faces to the 2026 roster.

Saturday’s Blue-White Practice is the first look at those new players in front of fans at Beaver Stadium, as well as the returners, under new coach Matt Campbell.

While the practice will run differently from previous Blue-White Games with an opening stretching period followed by drills and 7-on-7 and 11-on-11 action, there’s still some key players to watch out for with the regular season 135 days away.

Koby Howard, wide receiver

Amidst a struggling wide receiver room at Penn State, then-true freshman Koby Howard made four starts in 2025, posting seven receptions for 133 yards.

Howard is currently projected to be one of the three starting wide receivers this season alongside Iowa State transfers Brett Eskildsen and Chase Sowell. During the offseason, Howard earned praise from quarterback Rocco Becht and offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser, who noted his work ethic throughout training.

While the Florida native didn’t record more than two catches in any game last season, he excelled as a downfield receiver. Five of Howard’s seven catches went for over 10 yards, with three of his receptions earning over 20 yards.

After fans begged to see Howard on the field more last season, they’ll get the chance to see him working with the first team on Saturday.

Alex McPherson, defensive end

With Penn State’s entire starting defensive line departing ahead of the 2026 season, defensive line coach Ikaika Malloe and edge rushers coach Christian Smith have been working to establish their starting group for this year.

Among the names are transfer Alexander McPherson, who came to Penn State after one year at Colorado. The rising sophomore played in 10 games with the Buffalos as a true freshman, recording 10 tackles, half a sack and a blocked punt.

Despite his young age and relatively minimal collegiate experience, McPherson has stood out to Smith with his quick transition to Penn State and willingness to learn the new system.

Standing at 6-foot-6, the Florida native is the tallest defensive end on the Nittany Lions’ squad and looks to use that big size to earn a big role this season.

Malachi Goodman, offensive line

The Nittany Lions have historically had a veteran starting offensive line. With four of last year’s five starters departing for the NFL, that could change with redshirt freshman Malachi Goodman looking to earn a starting role this year.

Coming to Penn State as a 5-star recruit, Goodman didn’t see any playing time as a true freshman. However, he’s taken first-team reps at left tackle throughout spring ball and made a big name for himself throughout training.

Goodman has drawn the attention of offensive line coach Ryan Clanton, who said he plays confident, aggressive and does a good job retaining information.

As he’s transitioned to collegiate football, Goodman has put on another 10-15 pounds, currently weighing in around 330 pounds. In addition to his weight gain, Goodman worked to improve his athleticism as he hopes to earn a starting role this year.

Armstrong Nnodim, defensive tackle

Another defensive lineman who’s looking to earn a starting role is Oklahoma State transfer Armstrong Nnodim as he looks to make the most of his first time in front of fans at Beaver Stadium on Saturday.

Unlike McPherson, Nnodim has seen the starting lineup before, starting four games in his 12 appearances last season. The Texas native recorded 11 tackles, two tackles for loss and a sack with the Cowboys.

While he might not be a starter in Week 1, Nnodim’s performance at the Blue-White Practice might push him up the depth chart and close to a starting spot at some point during the year.

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: Blue-White Practice preview | Weather, format and team preview
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is taking to the grass at Beaver Stadium in front of fans for the first time since the Nittany Lions defeated Nebraska on Senior Night, giving interim head coach Terry Smith his lone home win.

But rather than Smith leading Penn State on the sidelines, it’s Matt Campbell’s turn to lead the Nittany Lions for the annual spring game, marking Campbell’s first time coaching in front of the Happy Valley faithful.

The Blue-White Game is set to look different this year, though, with a different name and format. Here’s what to know ahead of Saturday’s Blue-White Practice.

Format

Penn State recently posted the schedule for Blue-White Practice, and it begins with a team stretch when it kicks off at 12:15 p.m. before special teams and individual drills.

Things get a little more interesting afterwards, with the Nittany Lions set to play some 7-on-7 and 11-on-11. It’s unclear if any score will be kept, but fans should get some insight of where the depth chart currently stands at the end of spring practice.

Gates open for the day at 11 a.m. while the parking lots open at 8 a.m. Team arrival is slated for 11:45 a.m.

The practice will not be streamed or broadcasted.

Forecast

According to AccuWeather, Saturday will have a high 53 degrees with a real feel of 49 degrees during the afternoon. There are set to be 13 mph wind gusts and a cloud cover of 99%.

Occasional rain and drizzle is in the forecast, with a 61% chance of participation. AccuWeather is projecting around 0.17 inches of rain.

About Penn State

Penn State will look very different than it did the last time fans were in Beaver Stadium, as 50 players from last year departed via the transfer portal and over 40 new faces came in, many of which followed Campbell from Iowa State.

An almost entirely new quarterback room will be taking snaps, with projected starter Rocco Becht still limited after offseason surgeries in each of his shoulders. Becht will likely participate in 7-on-7 drills, but whether he plays during 11-on-11 is unclear.

The running back room also looks vastly different, with Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen off to the NFL after being the Nittany Lions’ starting running backs for the last four seasons. Carson Hansen, James Peoples and Quinton Martin Jr. are competing for the bulk of reps this year, but Hansen is seemingly the current front runner.

Saturday will give fans their first look at projected starting receivers Brett Eskildsen and Chase Sowell, although they’ll both likely be limited alongside Becht as they continue to recover from injury. Expect Koby Howard, Karon Brookins, Zay Robinson and Amarion Jackson to get significant burn in 11-on-11.

As for the defense, the defensive backfield won’t feature many new faces, although Iowa State transfer safety Marcus Neal Jr. will certainly be a standout alongside returning cornerbacks Daryus Dixson, Audavion Collins, Zion Tracy and Jahmir Joseph.

Kooper Ebel, Cael Brezina and Caleb Bacon are the new additions to a very deep linebacker room with all three transferring over from Iowa State. They’ll likely see the bulk of reps on Saturday alongside returnee Cam Smith.

The offensive line will be missing shoe-in starter Anthony Donkoh, but former 5-star Malachi Goodman is set to make his Beaver Stadium debut alongside center Brock Riker, who transferred from Texas State this offseason and has received glowing remarks from Campbell and offensive line coach Ryan Clanton.

Players likely out with injury on Saturday include Dom Rulli, Tony Rojas, Max Granville, Andrew Rappleyea, Gabe Burkle, Tyshon Huff, Jeremiah Cooper, Alex Tatsch and Alex Manske.

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: Senior Column | Better late than never, I guess
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
Shelley is my infamous car with nearly 100,000 miles on her. She got keyed outside of my frat house. She has no hubcaps, because they were taken by the Secret Service. (Yes, this actually happened. No, I don’t know why.) But more than Shelley just being a car I’m desperately trying to replace with a Tesla, Shelley has been the vehicle for a ton of college memories and the reason I can leave Penn State feeling so fulfilled.

So let’s pretend Shelley’s a Delorian and travel back in time.

2023

I know what you're thinking, of course, Evan is starting his senior column after his sophomore year, he just has to be weird all the time. Well, yes, but for me, this is kind of part one of graduating, as I’m heading out of Altoona and up to State College.

My first two years of college certainly happened. If you had told anyone from high school I became a frat boy, they would’ve laughed at you, but Altoona was a weird place. I mean, its two most famous exports are Luigi Mangione and Altoona Style Pizza. I once tried Altoona Style Pizza. Remember the scene from Ratatouille where the critic takes a bite of his meal and has a flashback? The same thing happened when I took a bite, except it was traumatic. ToonTahn shot me out of my comfort zone.

If you want some of the full Altoona stories, just find me and say one of these words: Harrisburg, Firefly, UFC, Elton, Blonde, Magic, Harrisburg 2, Drake, Newman, Pitt, Wawa, Halls.

I know I sound like I hated my time at Altoona, but I didn’t. It was fun at the moment, but it wasn’t fulfilling nor what I wanted out of my college experience. I found that when I joined CommRadio.

I was sick of wasting time, then I remembered I have free will and a Shelley, so I drove up to State College twice a week and got into the club before telling them, "Surprise, I don’t go here!” I then would DoorDash around downtown until I had enough money to pay for the gas to get home and repeat the process multiple times a week. If you really want something, don’t let minuscule setbacks get in your way.

It was all worth the hassle because working gave me a taste of my dream job. It drove me to do more, driving Shelley all across the East Coast for opportunities. You can’t regret not doing something if you just do everything.

Now I have the chance to live out my dream college experience, hopefully I can make it happen.

Better late than never, I guess.

2025

The first decision I made at University Park shaped the course of my next two weeks: I joined Onward State. Yep, didn’t see that one coming, did ya? But after meeting with Nick Stone(Cold)sifer, it was clear the Collegian was the best decision for me, only having two* years up here at Penn State.

For what it’s worth, two Onward photographers agreed with this sentiment after finding out my major was, quote, “journalism.” I only chose journalism as a step into broadcasting. I never liked writing; in fact, I hated it.

However, it didn’t take long for the Daily Collegian to make me fall in love with real journalism.

My candidate class was loaded. Alongside me were talents including Andrew Deal, Greg Finberg, Maggie Hardy, Chase Mitchell, Lexie Linderman and more. It’s hard not to get better while reading their works. My first true beat was men’s volleyball. To be honest, it was a sport I never liked or cared about at the time, but it taught me an open mind. I grew to love my time on the beat and one story in particular that changed my future trajectory.

While I still didn’t quite care about writing, I was tasked with writing a story about the late volleyball coach Tom Tait. I figured it would be a boring memorandum piece, but after a few phone calls, I learned the story of a man I now admire. I learned that journalism isn’t just writing, it’s telling important stories that people can’t tell themselves. It’s an outlet for creativity, more than just facts and to share emotions. I was hooked.

Writing brought me to places I could’ve never imagined. I’ll save the details for when it’s actually time for me to write this column next year, but when it comes to my goal of living my dream college experience, mission accomplished.

But I’m not ready to graduate. Everything I’ve done, I viewed as an investment into my future rather than living in the moment, but a tongue full of only tomorrows will never quench your thirst for today. Now I get to enjoy my second senior year. Hopefully, I can make it happen.

Better late than never, I guess.

2026

Welcome back, did you enjoy your ride? Yo Andrew, AJ, any bounce this time?

Welp, here we are at the end of the road. All of my experiences at Penn State have led me to right now, laying in my bed reminiscing. Picture me as Justin Bieber sitting at the laptop pulling up the classics and let’s look back.

2025 was my favorite year. On top of my radio duties for Penn State football, which gave me a front row seat to a playoff season, I got the luckiest possible beat draw. Volleyball, hockey and wrestling.

It started with volleyball, where Andrew, Will Horstman, Isaiah Maldonado and I covered one of the greatest stories in sports history. I’ll never forget the times “rooting for the sider” on press row in Rec Hall. And speaking of Rec Hall, those media chairs, I’m still planning on stea…. “borrowing” one of those before I leave.

Then there’s the hockey beat alongside Greg, Maggie, Chase Fisher, Adam Waxman and Andrew Mercer. Personally, covering hockey got my name on national television, got me quoted by international news sources and provided some of my best college stories.

To hear my UP stories, just find me and say one of these words: Redacted, SVH, Blicky, Chicago, Detroit, DC, Bridge, Camden, Duquesne, Hershey.

But the best part of the beat came with the rest of you. The Collegian beat taught me that people from Philadelphia and Boston can be tolerable after all. Greg is simply a talent. He and Zach Karpf helped me hone my writing skills after I developed a love for journalism. Adam provided me with memories I’ll never forget, even if his trunk door split my head open, and nearly wiped my memory entirely.

Alongside him, Chase and Maggie stood with me for countless adventures from Washington to St. Louis. Y’all are great writers and great company, even when a hobo starts pressing us in a Wawa parking lot. Prime is one of the most genuine people I’ve had the pleasure of meeting. Keep up the great work and the great vibes, and remember bro, more puns. Never forget the moment when John Seifarth was playing like “a ‘Prime’ Andrew Mercer.” If only we knew just how badly that quote would age.

I owe shoutouts to a few guys I never shared a beat with. Sam Darnold … I mean, Myles Hannak, who has been a great friend and editor for the last semester and George Keene for the great times at the table. Sam Simonic, my runaway son who apparently is addicted to being hazed, you’ll never beat me in Word Hunt. I also promised to mention Shaler’s best alumnus, Elliott Inscho. Lastly, thanks to Dan Studer for making everyone forget that I’m actually the oldest one here.

Senior year, I used my second-grade wrestling career to land a spot on the wrestling beat alongside Andrew Deal, AJ Piazza and Lyle Alenstein. Little did I know it would change my life.

Before covering wrestling, I was basically a student pretending to be a news source. Penn State wrestling gave me a real platform. Thanks to the Joel Haas school of engagement farming, I took advantage of it to become a notable, turned noteworthy name in a wrestling community that is a joy and headache to be a part of.

Wrestling fans are extremely passionate and meticulous. On my first day, “dual” autocorrected to “duel” on a tweet and I received six DMs about it. I, sometimes, can be the reason there are directions on a shampoo bottle, so this was a crash course on keeping details. Until I decided to just pay $10 a month to edit tweets instead.

Wrestling has some of the best stories that need to get uncovered due to the characters involved in a heavily individual sport. I remember being nervous about being at a press conference with Cael Sanderson for the first time until he started talking about Fortnite and I settled in.

And now let’s address the elephant in the room (NILephant?). My biggest story. One of the most gratifying things that has happened since I came to college is the amount of trust placed in me by my friends and other people. That trust, combined with a surprise phone call in the U.S. Open media room at Oakmont Country Club (way to bury the lead), let me put together a year of research, patience and work into a story that broke the internet for a while.

It was a telling experience. After the story went up, I turned off my phone, went for a walk along an Aruba beach, and hopped on a plane home before seeing the reaction. All the students who saw it were gracious, all the journalists who saw it were supportive, but the fanbase I had grown into turned on a dime.

It was weird having the same people I enjoyed having comment convos with who were, in some cases, literally threatening me, but I knew it was coming and, in a weird way, enjoyed it. I also took pride in seeing some of them come back around as this season went along.

Wrestling has allowed me to do everything except meet Daniel Cormier and do it alongside great people. AJ, you were my introduction to the Collegian through the Lion and you are one of my best friends I’ll ever find. Andrew is in the same boat, being a mix of great talent, a great person and hard worker. The vibe between the three of us has been the kind of unconditional fun I craved.

I’ll always cherish the legendary moments with the three of us. Be it the trip to Michigan, where we learned of Andrew’s addiction to Mallo Cups, or the trip to Philly last year that had everything from the run club to being swarmed by the Secret Service. God forbid a guy wants a glass of wine.

Lyle joined the crew this season and fit perfectly. Although he never successfully spladled Andrew, he helped improve my writing tenfold, be it by teaching me, or by me purposely writing technically about wrestling just to confuse him.

I’ve been able to use the platform of Collegian Wrestling to amass a Twitter with over 20,000,000 impressions and content that has been featured by Ian Rapoport, Conor McGregor, TSN, ESPN and more.

When I was growing up, I wished to just be in the crowd at a Beaver Stadium football game. Penn State gave me the chance to be on the field, behind the scenes and everywhere in between. It’s been an honor to serve as a Segway between the teams I grew up idolizing and the fan base I longed to just be a part of.

To the rest of my Penn State family, I’ll never forget the Cafe Mondays, Gaffaoke Wednesdays, DVSN Thursdays, Doggies Fridays and, most importantly, not Den Tuesdays. The memories make me feel nicer than Andrew Deal in a Cleveland parking lot.

Enough history lessons, welcome back to now. I’ll go park, Shelley and we’ll have one final chat.

If you had asked me in high school where I’d be out of college, my delusional self would’ve said famous on my way to broadcasting the Super Bowl. Luckily, now I’m more realistic; I’m just not that guy.

I’m probably done with play-by-play, but I’m not sad about it because I’ve worked alongside amazing talents like Michael Siroty and Josh Bartosik and dedicated workers like Jack Rachinsky and Ben Hoyt who will get there. That’s enough of an honor in itself.

I don’t have any aspirations of a future in TV news, but I traveled to Argentina with a collection of future stars of the industry, which is enough for me to take pride in. I don’t yet have a job in print journalism, but I shared beats with some of the best writers I’ve had the pleasure of reading, stars like Lyle and Greg, who are certainly going to make it.

I see a lot of people come into Bellisario treating it like a competition. The best thing you can do is enjoy the room you're in. More gratifying than the opportunities I’ve earned is being in a position to share that feeling with others. Seeing the gratitude in people’s faces as they thanked me for the opportunities felt even better knowing I didn’t give them anything; they earned it.

Who you are is just as important as what you do. I hope I left the people and organizations I met better than I found them.

I hope that if any underclassmen from Penn State are reading this, you take my advice to enjoy this as you go and to never turn down an opportunity. Some of the things you’ll get offered in college you will never get offered again. Don’t reject it on account of it’ll make you lose some sleep. Always take the leap if it’s worth the fall.

My whole college experience has taught me to relax and take every opportunity. So I don’t know where I’m going to end up after college yet, but I know I’m going to succeed and be happy. If my words haven’t said the quiet part out loud yet, thank you, Penn State and everyone I’ve met here. I’ll hate the goodbyes, but I won’t forget the good times.

The Daily Collegian helped me discover who I truly want to be. Now, I get to go out, hop inside Shelley and find the place where I can actually be that guy.

Better late than never, I guess.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Senior column | I don’t know where I’ll go after this, but I’ll never stop
author: Avery Hill | Football Editor
description:
My life is straight out of Dr. Seuss’ book, “Oh, The Places You’ll Go!”

I mean, literally. I owned two copies of that book as a kid. Why? I have no explanation other than the fact that I was a weird kid.

But at the time, I had no clue the places I’d go would be the Super Bowl, the Paris Paralympics and the College Football Playoff. I had no clue I’d achieve what I’d achieve by 21 years of age. But “Oh, The Places You’ll Go!” is not just about the achievements. It’s the ups and downs. The life lessons learned.

It’s why I’ll never stop.

You see, I entered college without a clue in the world of what to expect. I didn’t know there was a student newspaper. I didn’t know I would actually be considered to go to Hallmark Sports Events. And I certainly didn’t know I’d cover Penn State football, especially for a coach not named James Franklin.

But what I did know is that I wanted to leave an impact, and I can humbly say I did that. Through it all, I can’t complain.

As a candidate, I joined the men’s hockey beat with no experience writing period and casual knowledge of collegiate hockey.

Enter Joe Tuman, my candidate buddy and friend, who helped me through the process. I was immediately intrigued by his work ethic, along with the rest of the incredible beat I worked with: Jim Hartnett and Gracie Carella.

Their care for the sport was the standard — or shall I say, the Culture.

It clearly rubbed off.

As a first-year journalist, I spent my nights in the HUB cranking out story after story, staying late enough to let the cleaning crew disrupt my peace as they shuffled chairs and tables across the floor.

I’ll never forget the dopamine rush I’d get as I waited for my editors to read my stories. It was a true feeling of pride in myself and what I’d got to do. It was just the beginning, and I didn’t take it for granted.

It’s why I’ll never stop.

But my two semesters of experience covering men’s hockey are just a small part of my story.

Nearing the end of the spring semester covering softball, my editors, Joel Haas and Sam Woloson, approached me about becoming a summer editor.

I had no interest prior. I was young and a bit milquetoast.

I remember when they first mentioned it to the entire staff, and I thought there was no way I could’ve seen myself as a leader.

But once they personally advised me, I figured I’d take the shot.

That opportunity became a transformational experience in my journalistic ability, and it was one of many examples of how those around me have helped me get to this point.

But I can’t mention those who’ve supported me without mentioning my very first editing duo. Joel’s one of them, but alongside him is the Collegian Hall of Famer himself: Andrew Buckman.

Andrew held me to a standard and made sure I knew when I brought in a good story. He also wasn’t shy to text me about being biased, or about its versus their.

It was my absolute pleasure to return the favor and edit for his “Buckman semester.”

Joel has grown to be one of my closest friends in the Collegian. His leadership by example, ridiculous engagement-farming tactics and continuous support for me throughout my time have meant the world.

But as I progressed through beats, I met the man, the Meatball Monday, the Lyle Alenstein, who I started working with on the women’s basketball beat during our sophomore year.

His drive to crank out three stories a day and impeccable sports memorization skills, also known as “ball knowledge,” have made me push myself more.

He’s also been my favorite person to run into at Champ’s.

And then there’s Lexie Linderman, whom I’ve seen blossom since she was a candidate on men’s hockey my sophomore year.

She’s one of the best pure writers I’ve come across at this level, and I’ve learned a ton from her writing. Her support for my stories after I felt I’d turned in the worst piece imaginable made a difference.

Oh and, she’s going to be an incredible football editor.

And though I’d worked with or under Joel, Lexie and Lyle previously, I had no clue they’d become a part of my Saturdays for the last two years.

The 16-hour drives. The budget hotels. The podcast. The random NBA pulls that Lyle and I would rattle off at random times, like Shabazz Muhammad, or even the Lupita's run with Joel after we’d all skipped out on the senior picnic (sorry, Joel!).

They quickly became some of the best friends I’ve ever made.

It’s part of what made Nov. 22, 2025, one of the most emotional nights of my life.

It was Senior Day for Penn State against Nebraska, and I’d just finished editing my final story. After taking my goodbye picture, I made my way down the endless flight of stairs connected to the press box and exited through the side door of the will-call entrance.

I then caught my breath and let it out.

For the sake of my ego, I’ll stop the description there. But just know it was a long walk to Lot 44.

Anyway, to Footblog, thank you for the memories.

But I couldn’t thank some of my closest friends at the Collegian without thanking my best friend: Mercedes Hamilton.

Mercedes and I worked closely while I was sports editor. We didn’t know each other prior, but our relationship accelerated as soon as we got to campus for the fall, taking and struggling through Math 33 together.

She then encouraged me to join news staff, taking her spot on the culture and identity beat as she returned to her role as news editor for the spring. I asked her to challenge me, and she did, helping me produce pieces I’m proud of, including a listicle that featured the words, “it’s Barbie time.”

More importantly, I was blessed with the opportunity of being her boyfriend, something that’s allowed me to grow both personally and professionally.

Her relentless effort, intellect, and passion for so many different elements of life have motivated me every single day.

Mercedes, thank you.

She and my esteemed beat partners are why I’ll never stop.

And I don’t get into this position without my parents. Mom, Dad, along with my entire village at home, thank you for being my biggest cheerleaders.

Whether it was simply sharing my story to your Facebook feed or reading a story about a sport you may know nothing about, you all have been in my corner, and works can’t express how thankful I am for that.

But they aren’t the only ones who’ve influenced me along the way.

Seth Engle, if you’re reading this, thank you for setting an example for me early on. I’ll never forget when you yelled at me about posting a gamer late during my time as a summer editor. It was the tough love I needed.

To my other former editors, Daniel Mader, Daniel Perez, Liam Wichser, Ella Castronuovo and Sam, thank you for always being transparent in my edits; you all truly made me a better journalist.

To each of my former editor-in-chiefs, Megan Swift, Nick Stonesifer and Amy Schafer, thank you for believing in me and helping me feel welcomed.

Will Horstman, thank you for allowing me to work with you this year. I look forward to watching your journey. I also look forward to watching St. Mary’s Ryken bring the belt to Bishop O’Connell for another year.

While I may miss some names, I also want to thank AJ Piazza, Greg Finberg, Evan Smith, Chris Eutsler, Daniel Mader, Dwight “Popeyes” Wu and Zach Allen. I may not have left a butt-print legacy like Joel, but you all certainly helped me enjoy the time I did spend at the table, and around the staff.

As you see, I’ve gotten the privilege to be around so many talented journalists over my four years, and I look forward to seeing the Collegian’s growth.

They’re why I’ll never stop.

That brings me to the new version of Footblog.

To that trio, Andrew “Prime” Mercer, Andrew “The Real Deal” Deal and Noah “Do-It-All” Aberegg, I wish you all of the best.

I’m excited to see you guys take advantage of the moment. It’s truly an incredible opportunity and you all deserve it. You’re not just surrounded by a great reporter in Lexie, but also a talented group of reporters on the entire Penn State beat.

Be a sponge, take notes and go be great.

I’ll be watching.

I don’t know where I’ll be watching from, I don’t know where I’ll go after this, but what I do know is I’ll never get the experience of being in The Daily Collegian again.

I’m going to miss it. It’s given me moments I could’ve never expected, and a support system up there with the best.

To the best student newspaper in the country, thank you and goodbye.

You all are why I’ll never stop.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Senior Column | 'Queen's Queens' - State College Edition
author: Joseph Klein | The Daily Collegian
description:
Millions of years ago, dinosaurs ruled the Earth.

But without social media and video games, they were eventually replaced by humans who, with said inventions, messed up the planet worse than dinosaurs ever could.

I bring this up because many things in life don’t seem to go so well, including my decision freshman year to be a food science major, only to later be met with the Grim Reaper that is CHEM 110, which forced my decision to change majors.

The switch from food science to photojournalism was obvious; I spent my entire childhood pestering my parents about how I wanted to be a journalist like my uncle.

Uncle Joey wrote a column, “Queen’s Queens,” that had become regular reading fodder from my Uncle Alan growing up. Photography had been an ongoing hobby, whether in my free time on my phone, shooting film back home in New York City or away in London. I had gotten an eye for it in high school, thanks to my baseball coach and first photography mentor, Bryan Ciminelli.

Plus, entering the major junior year wasn't all that bad, even though I was in a completely new environment and now two years behind the other photojournalism classmates, skill-wise.

I centered myself on one objective: catch up. With the help of everyone around me, I can confidently say I did, now having one of the top student awards in the topic.

It’s now time to leave and enter the “real world,” but these past two years were some of the best I've lived and I would never trade them.

Through my experiences, I've realized that I do have some mantras I use to push through even the most grueling insanity in college. Here are some of the ones that helped me, whether it be with a press pass or not.

Tip 1: Love what you do. If your heart truly is not intimate with it, it may not be for you.

Tip 2: Always push for access, even if you don’t think you will get it. Growing up, my parents always told me to ask again or ask someone else after being rejected, because you never know what’ll happen the second time.

My junior year, I got turned down by every equipment desk on campus for renting a camera, until I asked Professor John Pendygraft after my first photo night, an event the photojournalism community has to get together, bond and learn. After hearing my story, he helped me in many ways to create my first photojournalistic portfolio, which allowed me to join the Collegian.

Tip 3: Always ask questions; too many are better than none. My first photo editors, Kate (Photo Goat) Hildebrand and Chris (Big Dawg) Eutsler, know this better than anyone. Even if it’s a small question that you think is inconsequential, such as reviewing a caption that you’ve written a million times before. In the long run, ensuring you follow the proper procedure is better than getting fired.

Tip 4: Don’t stay in your own comfort zone; get out of your lane. At the end of my junior year, I had decided to create a cooking column, which was insanely hard for someone who sucks at writing. Luckily, thanks to fate, I was able to meet Professor Walter Middlebrook, previously my uncle’s editor at Newsday, who ended up becoming what I think my uncle would have been had he been alive.

I say this with utmost honesty, but since my initial meeting with him, I have never seen so much red pen ink on paper. But thanks to him, “Klein’s Kitchen” turned out to be something I sincerely enjoyed creating both as a photographer and now as a writer.

Tip 5: Play and have fun! This is a lesson I learned in a high school film class, but it has shaped the way I approach everything. There’s no improvement without experimentation, but there is no experimentation without playing. The chocolate lava cake was created by a chef who was playing with chocolate sponge cakes, and both of the awards I have won in the past four years have been the result of projects that I played with nonstop.

Tip 6: The only person you need to compete with is yourself. I spent the past two years looking at photographers like Alex (The Wizard) Antoniono, Nia (Master Class) Meyers and Esteban (The Goat) Marenco, whom I had only dreamed of being as good as. After spending time with the three of them during the Mountain Workshops in Kentucky, we had created friendly nicknames for one another. I realized that there’s no need for competition with others. The only individual who holds you back from being as good as they are is the past version of you.

Tip 7: Don’t let others shape you. Many people over the years have argued that I may not be cut out for photojournalism and that I should consider different careers. I took the criticisms and threw them back in their faces. People hate because they don’t want you to succeed, but when you do succeed, they don’t have room to hate.

Final Tip: Put your head down and run. My favorite comic superhero was Barry Allen’s Flash. I loved him specifically for one reason: He puts his head down and gives everything he has into whatever he is doing. I tend to come off as very vibrant and fast-paced, especially since I speak with a New Yorker’s pace. But, if you put 100% into everything you do, there’s nothing you can’t do, even if you tear your labrum!

Anyways, the actual point of making my way through college after switching majors went really well, and there were no real hassles in making my way from Point A to Point B because my siblings, Bubby and Alexis, as well as my parents, had my back through the entire ride.

To Zach Karpf, Caleb Craig, Sammy Oropeza, Aayan Hamdani, Alex Achmiz, Dustin Danckwerth, Madelyn Collelo, Rylee Weaver, Craig Dixon, William Yeh, Young Nam, Caleb Soi, everyone I have mentioned previously, as well as the many staffers I’ve had the pleasure to sit with at “The Table.”

And finally, to The Daily Collegian.

Thank you for allowing me to realize my end-of-sophomore-year switch to photojournalism was the best thing that’s ever happened to me.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Review | Sora Teriyaki & Sushi is on a ‘roll’ with its menu
author: Veronica Figg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Sora Teriyaki & Sushi opened March 27 at 110 Hetzel St.The spot offers teriyaki bowls, sushi rolls and various appetizers. I’m a big fan of Japanese cuisine and was excited to try this new spot after seeing the menu.

Fried Gyoza

The first thing I tried was an appetizer of fried gyoza. Gyoza tends to be my favorite dish at a Japanese restaurant, so I was very excited to try theirs and compare. Unfortunately, I was let down.

The fried gyoza was a little greasy, which is to be expected with fried food. However, the bread had an aftertaste that was so overpowering that I struggled to discern the type of meat inside the dumpling, making for an unpleasant eating experience.

The sauce that came with it was a nice addition and had a nice kick, which lessened the unsavory aftertaste a bit.

Rating 5/10

Beef Terikayi Bowl

At Sora, you can make your own teriyaki bowl with your choice of base (rice or vegetables), protein and additional toppings. I typically enjoy beef teriyaki and like most things, it tastes better in a bowl. The rice alone was flavorful and not too chewy, which meant it soaked up the flavors well.

The rice on its own was good, but in my opinion, it needed more teriyaki sauce. I added mushrooms to my bowl, but they were uninspiring.

The beef, however, was lacking in seasoning. While the searing of the beef gave some flavor, it still wasn’t enough to remove the blandness. When I got a bite with the combination of beef, rice and mushrooms, the bowl fared better.

The rice and beef together were good, but the mushrooms had a distinct taste that slightly overpowered the rest of the dish. Overall, the dish didn’t taste like teriyaki, and needed more sauce to help with dryness.

Rating: 5/10

Dragon Roll

In full honesty, sushi is not my favorite dish, but it’s grown on me over the years due to the rest of my family loving it. I decided to try the dragon roll, and was pleasantly surprised.

Sushi is clearly where Sora shines. The dragon roll is a traditional California roll and contains crabsticks and crunchy tempura.

The sushi was made fresh behind the counter and packaged in a container with ginger, wasabi and soy sauce. The sushi was the best thing that I had at the restaurant. The rice was soft and chewy, making for a great roll.

The crab was tender and when combined with the cucumber, made for a great bite. The sauce on top of the roll was tangy and had a small kick to it. If you want to come to Sora, come for the sushi.

Rating: 7/10

Veronica’s Food Sensitivity Corner

As a student who was recently diagnosed with a dairy sensitivity, I’m always on the lookout for foods that fit my new dietary needs. I’m also aware there are other students who may have similar needs as well.

The restaurant doesn’t have any information on their menu about food allergies or sensitivities, so please ask the cashier if an item has gluten, dairy, nuts, etc. or if it’s vegetarian or vegan.

Sora Teriyaki & Sushi is a great place for sushi lovers to try. If interested and wanting to “roll” in, they’re open Mondays, Wednesdays and Thursdays 11:30 a.m. to 9 p.m., Fridays and Saturdays 11:30 a.m. to 10 p.m. and are closed on Tuesdays.

MORE BLOGS

title: Penn State track and field competes at historic Penn Relays
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
The outdoor track and field season is winding down with just one meet left of the regular season. Despite this, Penn State continued to set top marks to close out the season.

The Nittany Lions competed at the historic Penn Relays in Philadelphia from Thursday to Saturday, earning two first-place finishes and two top-10 marks in school history.

Sophomore Gabby Cope opened the event with a personal best in the college women’s hammer throw, recording a mark of 56.31 meters for second place.

While junior Gabi Deglau didn’t earn a personal-best, she threw a season-best 55.30 meters for fifth place, followed by Alex Pancoast’s 54.16 meters for seventh place.

The Nittany Lions also earned a big finish in the college men’s hammer throw championship as graduate thrower Collin Burkhart threw 67.15 meters for third overall.

The first track event for Penn State was the women’s 1,500-meters, where sophomore Ada Rand ran a 4:17.11 to finish 13th overall. Senior Lea Hatcher finished just behind in 4:17.11 for 16th place.

Penn State’s first victory of the day came from junior Nick Sloff, who ran 3:40.85 in the men’s college 1,500-meter championship.

The Nittany Lions continued to wow in the college men’s 3,000-meter steeplechase championship as senior Ryan Watson ran the fastest time in program history since 1988. Watson ran 8:46.52 to finish fifth, marking the fifth-best time in Penn State history. Junior Logan Strawser ran a personal-best 8:54.63 for 18th place.

Four Nittany Lions took the track for the college women’s 5,000-meter championship, with senior Katie Dallas leading the way with a personal-best 16:18.42 for 22nd place.

Friday

Junior Joe White kicked off the Penn Relays on Friday in the college men’s long jump, where he finished eighth with a mark of 6.99 meters.

In the college men’s discus throw, MIT transfer Sam Engebretson earned a top-five finish with a distance of 51.37 meters, a season best. That top-five Penn State finish carried over into the college men’s pole vault, as sophomore Mason Bucks jumped 4.87 meters for fifth place.

The first of Penn State’s top performances came in the college men’s 4x400-meter championship of America prelims, where the relay of Carter Fitzgerald, Peyton Sommers, Matt DeMatteo and Jake Palermo ran 3:05.39 for fifth place, a spot in Saturday’s finals and the 10th-best time in school history.

The Nittany Lions took that a step further in the College Women’s Sprint Medley Championship of America, as Addyson White, Alexandria North, Marie-Eloise Leclair and Hayley Kitching broke the school record with a time of 3:42.35, winning the race.

Saturday

Penn State kicked off the final day of the Penn Relays in the college men’s high jump championship as freshman Trip Campbell earned a third-place finish with a mark of 2.14 meters, followed by Robert Allen in 10th place with 2.01 meters.

In the college men’s pole vault championship, senior Tristan McGarrah improved his No. 5 mark in school history, jumping 5.17 meters for fifth place overall.

Freshman Skye Petrie-Cameron earned a personal best in the college women’s shot put throw championship, recording a fifth-place mark of 15.90 meters. Sasha Garnett threw 15.73 meters for seventh place.

The first of Penn State’s big moments came in the college men’s 4xMile championship of America, where the relay of Ryan Watson, Nick Sloff, Brian DiCola and Justin Healey ran 16:52.08 for eighth place and the school record.

In the college women’s javelin throw championship, Audrey Friedman finished in sixth place with a throw of 45.81 meters.

The Nittany Lions’ record-breaking day continued in the college women’s 4x800-meters championship of America, as the relay of Allison Johnson, Tayissa Buchanan, Boh Ritchie and Hayley Kitching broke the school record with a first-place finish in 8:18.43. Kitching’s final lap of 2:01.96 solidified the victory for Penn State.

While Penn State didn’t win the college men’s 4x800-meters championship of America, it still broke the school record in 7:10.00. Yukichi Ishii, Andrew Regnier, Niko Schultz and Allon Clay finished second, losing by less than one second.

Three Nittany Lions finished in the top 10 of the college men’s shot put championship, led by Dylan Lambrecht in first place with 19.34 meters. Ryan Henry finished second with 18.91 meters, followed by freshman Bryce Thompson in sixth with 18.02 meters.

Penn State wrapped up the day in the college men’s javelin throw championship, where freshman Nolan Carey threw 65.89 meters for fourth place.

Up next

Penn State wraps up the outdoor regular season with the Larry Ellis Invitational on May 1 and 2.

MORE TRACK COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball takes down Charleston to advance to EIVA Championship
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has been patiently waiting to see who it would face in the EIVA Semifinals after earning the top seed in the tournament and the first-round bye.

The Nittany Lions’ mystery opponent was revealed to be Charleston after the Golden Eagles defeated Sacred Heart. Mark Pavlik and the blue and white won both matches against Charleston 3-0 back in the first weekend of April.

In the end, Penn State came out on top, winning 3-2 in dramatic five-set fashion to advance to the EIVA Championship match.

Charleston got on the board first in the opening set due to a Matthew Luoma attack error that was followed by a Sean Harvey kill, which tied it up for the blue and white.

Harvey ended the season with the third-most kills in the EIVA and the best hitting percentage, at .324%.

Penn State stayed on top for the following several minutes, jumping out to a 6-3 lead backed by kills from Luoma, Bennett Wilson and Gaige Gabriel.

Wilson got back in on the action, notching a service ace to give the blue and white a two-point lead after the Golden Eagles went on a 4-0 run that tightened the score.

A flashy series paid off for Charleston with a block assist going to Yotam Briger and Pavlin Ivanov and an attack error going to Wilson as the Golden Eagles got out to a 15-14 lead.

Another 3-0 scoring run for Charleston extended its lead to 18-15 and forced the Nittany Lions into a timeout after back-to-back Lucas Torres kills.

Torres finished the regular season with the eighth-most kills in the conference, with 193 total.

The Golden Eagles captured the first set thanks to a Harvey attack error and moved one set closer to upsetting the blue and white and crushing its EIVA Championship hopes.

Again, Charleston opened up the scoring in the second frame with a Max Nissen kill before Harvey matched it with a kill of his own to tie the action.

Contrary to the opening set, the second was all Charleston in the early running as the Golden Eagles jumped out to a 7-3 lead backed by a pair of Owen Rose errors, another kill for Nissen and a block assist that went down as a Tristan Hassell attack error.

Penn State turned on the jets, using a 6-1 scoring run to even the second set at 9-9 before Charleston used its first timeout of the night.

In their efforts to cut into the deficit, the Nittany Lions saw Hassell, Harvey and Gaige each notch another kill while Wilson recorded two service aces.

The Nittany Lions grabbed what felt like their first lead in a long time after a Rose service error followed by a Hassell and Gaige block assist score, forcing the Golden Eagles into another timeout as the scoreboard read 17-13 in the blue and white’s favor.

Charleston made things interesting as the action was coming to a head, but it wasn’t enough and Penn State took the second set 25-19 on a Golden Eagle service error.

The blue and white got out to an early 5-1 lead in the third set, pushing Charleston into another timeout after two Harvey kills that moved him up to nine on the night.

Not much changed as Penn State jumped up to a monstrous 13-5 lead after another Hassell kill that came out like a missile just before the Golden Eagles took another timeout.

Back-to-back Charleston kills from Torres and Kai Cousins and a Hassell attack error cut Penn State’s lead to 16-10, but momentum was still very much on the Nittany Lions’ side.

Consecutive Samuel Alo attack errors didn’t help his squad’s cause, and the blue and white’s lead grew to 20-12 with the end of the third set in sight.

Penn State cruised to an easy third-set victory at the hands of a Rose kill that was the middle blocker’s sixth of the night.

With just one set to go for the Nittany Lions to advance and compete for an EIVA Championship, it was the Golden Eagles who jumped out to an early 2-0 lead in the fourth frame, but back-to-back kills from Rose and Harvey tied the action at 2-2.

A 6-2 scoring run for Charleston that saw four errors extended the Golden Eagles’ lead to 8-4 in the early running of the fourth set, as Penn State seemingly had no answer.

Harvey’s serve was just off the mark and resulted in a point for Charleston, which made its lead 15-11 and served as the 12th error of the set.

Edging closer to a fourth set victory, the Golden Eagles’ lead was extended to 20-18 thanks to another Torres kill that sucked all the wind out of Penn State’s sails as it used a timeout.

Some points and a timeout later, it was Charleston that captured the fourth set 25-22 after another Torres kill, meaning an extra period of volleyball was needed to determine which program would advance.

In the abbreviated fifth period, Charleston again got on the board first by way of a Wilson attack error, but the mistake was made up for by a Hassell kill – his 15th of the night.

The Golden Eagles had the early momentum, but a 4-1 blue and white scoring run gave Pavlik’s squad the narrow 5-3 advantage.

Things got a little bit chippy, but momentum stuck with the Nittany Lions the rest of the way as they sailed to a 15-8 victory in the ultimate set, with a Rose kill putting a cap on the night to send his squad to the Conference Final.

Up Next

Penn State will face Princeton in the EIVA Championship match inside Rec Hall on Saturday at 7 p.m.

The Tigers finished the regular season with an overall record of 8-14, but snuck into the EIVA Tournament and got hot, taking down three-seed George Mason in the opening round and two-seed NJIT in a semifinals round match that went to the fifth set.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State OL Vega Ioane drafted 14th overall by Baltimore Ravens
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has now produced a first-round pick in three straight years.

Vega Ioane was selected 14th overall by the Baltimore Ravens in the first round of the 2026 NFL Draft. He was the fourth offensive lineman off the board.

Ioane’s draft stock soared after back-to-back stellar seasons as the Nittany Lions’ starting left guard. Across over 1,000 snaps the last two years, Ioane didn’t allow a single sack after allowing three in 2023.

He really caught the nation’s eye, however, against UCLA in 2024 after destroying defensive end Luke Schuermann while lined up out wide. That highlight showed not only Ioane’s strength, but his versatility as an offensive lineman.

“There’s not many guys that are 6-foot-4 and 350 pounds that can move like him,” current Indianapolis Colt and former Nittany Lion Tyler Warren said after that win. “Seeing a big guy that gets to run around and hit people like that is always going to put a little spark in the sideline and our offense.”

Ioane went viral once again a year later, but this time for something he did off the field. After losing to Ohio State amidst a tough season, Ioane saw an equipment manager struggling to push a cart up the tunnel ramp, so he jumped over the railing to help him.

The video, which was originally posted by Mark Brennan of 247Sports, garnered over 111,000 likes on X and was posted by numerous other organizations.

“Simple as one of my teammates, just a different version,” Ioane said of the moment in early November. “A lot of people, who are outside the building, don't know that we don't run without them guys like that. We can't practice without them. We can't go to games without them. Just as simple as helping out our teammate.”

The Ravens surely liked seeing their first round pick display that kind of character, but Ioane’s stats and accolades are what truly made him worthy of the early selection.

Ioane earned All-America honors from numerous outlets after the 2025 season despite playing for a 7-6 Penn State squad and was a First Team All-Big Ten selection by the media. He was also a finalist for the Polynesian Player of the Year Award.

After his stellar redshirt junior season, Ioane decided to forego his final year of eligibility and declare for the NFL draft. He participated in the NFL Scouting Combine and Penn State Pro Day, where he impressed scouts and reaffirmed his draft stock.

“It feels good,” Ioane said at pro day. “It's a feeling that I know I put a lot of work into it, knowing that it's all coming together, all connecting, things like that. But knowing that I put my best foot forward to put myself in a position like that, it definitely feels good.”

Ioane is the second Nittany Lions’ offensive lineman to be taken in the first round in the last three years, joining 2024 No. 11 overall pick Olu Fashanu. He is the 58th offensive lineman to be drafted from Penn State.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball infielder Allison Oneacre named NFCA National Freshman of the Year top 25 finalist
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
As the regular season winds down, accolades are starting to creep around the corner for the Nittany Lions.

Standout freshman infielder Allison Oneacre was named as a top-25 finalist for NFCA's Division I National Freshman of the Year award.

all eyes on AO.#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/BEngPkvoUM— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) April 23, 2026

The Alpharetta, Georgia, native has been a big bat for the blue and white this season, leading the team with a .385 batting average, 16 home runs and a new single-season program record of 56 RBI.

Oneacre's RBI count ranks fifth in the Big Ten and 27th nationally.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men's basketball transfer Eli Rice commits to Middle Tennessee
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State transfer Eli Rice is set to reunite with former teammate Jahvin Carter and return to his home state.

Rice has committed to Middle Tennessee, according to Sam Kayser of League Ready.

NEWS: Penn State transfer Eli Rice has committed to Middle Tennessee, source told @LeagueRDY.The 6-foot-8 wing played the last two seasons at Penn State, averaging 6.7PPG and 1.7RPG this season. Shot 39% from 3. Repped by @jshaugn_23 of @SHASportsAgency. https://t.co/l7IdGheUlB pic.twitter.com/PqJCbFpJgj— Sam Kayser (@KayserHoops) April 23, 2026

Rice and Carter were teammates on the 2024-25 Penn State team. Carter transferred to the Blue Raiders last offseason and averaged 10.8 points per game this past season.

Rice, who's a native of Gallatin, Tennessee, will move closer to home as his collegiate career continues. Middle Tennessee is his third team in his career as he played for Nebraska prior to joining Penn State.

The 6-foot-8 wing averaged 6.7 points and 1.7 rebounds per game with the Nittany Lions in 2025-26, shooting 39% from 3-point range. His shooting hit a cold streak toward the end of the season, causing him to be benched in the Big Ten Tournament game against Northwestern.

Rice only made one appearance in the 2024-25 season with Penn State due to injury, so he redshirted that year. He has two seasons of eligibility remaining as he joins the Blue Raiders.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball set to face Charleston for EIVA Semifinal
author: Liana Tiamzon I For the Collegian
description:
Undefeated conference champions Penn State isn’t looking back, with a perfect record behind it. Instead, the Nittany Lions are focused on what comes next as they prepare for the EIVA semifinals, where they face the Charleston Golden Eagles, setting up a high-stakes rematch between familiar opponents and a potential NCAA Tournament berth on the line.

The Nittany Lions enter the postseason with momentum from a consecutive nine-game winning streak against Ohio State, NJIT, Charleston, Sacred Heart and George Mason. They have established themselves as one of the most complete teams in the EIVA. They have shown balanced attacks and a strong defensive presence, led by blockers like Owen Rose, who averaged 1.80 blocks per set and leads the EIVA with 129 blocks on the season.

Sophomore outside hitter Sean Harvey has led the charge offensively, hitting .373 with 3.80 kills per set. He has been a consistent force, recording 20 kills in one match and 18 in the next. Harvey had also been named EIVA’s Player of the Year, solidifying himself as one of the conference's top scoring threats.

Penn State swept Charleston in back-to-back matches earlier this month, controlling nearly every aspect of play. In the first meeting, the Nittany Lions cruised to a 3-0 sweep. They were hitting .338 as a team while holding Charleston to just .062. They followed it up with another straight-set win the following day.

The difference in the net was very clear between Penn State and Charleston.​​ The Nittany Lions outblocked the Golden Eagles 14-4 in the first match, led by Rose’s 10-block performance.
