Allen adds another award to her historic season after earning her sixth Big Ten Freshman of the Week honors on Monday. Her All-Freshman Team mark was the only unanimous honor for the blue and white.

The star freshman finished third on the team with 41 goals and third in points with an additional 22 assists. Allen recorded six hat tricks during the year, including four five-goal games.

𝐁𝐈𝐆 𝐓𝐄𝐍 𝐀𝐋𝐋-𝐅𝐑𝐄𝐒𝐇𝐌𝐀𝐍 𝐓𝐄𝐀𝐌Suz is one of the best freshman in the land 😮‍💨 pic.twitter.com/iuMogP4RqR— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) April 21, 2026

Outside of Allen, O’Connell was the only other freshman to start in all 17 games this season. The Summit, New Jersey, native caused 25 turnovers and picked up 23 ground balls in her first year in Happy Valley.

MORE LACROSSE CONTENT

title: Weekly recap | Penn State baseball had back and forth week with some bright spots
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State continued its underwhelming season last week, dropping three of its last four games.

The week started on Wednesday with a neutral-site game against West Virginia in Maryland, which it lost in a low-scoring game. Then the Nittany Lions returned to State College for a three-game conference series against Rutgers. The blue and white dropped the first two games before getting a win in the third.

Penn State will have to try to build on this last win and keep battling to try to compete for a spot in the Big Ten Tournament.

Bi-polar batting

The Nittany Lions were only able to score one run in two of their games this past week. However, in their other two outings, they scored 11 and 14, the latter tying their season high. Weirdly, their best and worst scoring days often come against the same opponents. It first happened against Purdue last month. The first matchup saw a 2-1 loss, then the next day they won 14-9.

You can also see this against Ohio State. Earlier this season, the blue and white won its opening series game against the Buckeyes 10-6. The next day, it lost 3-2, but in the series finale, it matched the score of the first but still lost 15-10. Penn State needs to stay consistent throughout its series. It shows flashes of good ball, then looks like a different team against the same opponent.

Highlighting an inconsistency in this past week's series was Bryce Molinaro. He’s been a productive player this season, with 11 home runs and 31 RBIs. However, he was hitless going into the final game of the series against Rutgers. But on Sunday, he had three hits, three runs and four RBIs, including a home run that was a part of a back-to-back-to-back triple home run set.

Clear consistencies

Someone who shined through the whole week was Jack Porter. He started his week with the only run scored against the Mountaineers; he also had more hits than the rest of his squad combined, with three.

This continued when Penn State returned to Happy Valley. He began the series with two runs and four RBIs on four hits. Then, in the second low-scoring matchup of the week, he was the only runner to score again. Back to the shootouts, he only had one hit, but managed to score three runs and have two RBIs. Porter is third on the team in RBIs and hits, as well as tied for second in runs; he continues to be a highlight for the blue and white.

Another consistency has been pitcher Dimond Loosli. He saw action in two games this week, earning a win in the series finale against Rutgers. He first pitched against West Virginia, throwing just one inning against West Virginia, allowing no hits with two strikeouts. Then in his win, he allowed no hits again, with one strikeout in the same amount of innings.

Penn State will have to learn to be consistent if it wants to play in the Big Ten Tournament.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Matt Campbell talks Blue-White Practice, injury updates
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
It’s a big week for Penn State, with the NFL draft kicking off on Thursday and Blue-White Practice on Saturday.

Amidst the chaos, Matt Campbell took to the podium in Lasch Building to talk about this weekend’s Blue-White Practice, the upcoming team schedule and injury recovery.

Here’s more of what Campbell had to say in his last weekly press conference of the spring.

Blue-White Practice

The team and fans alike will experience a new event at Beaver Stadium on Saturday, as Penn State hosts its spring game in the form of Blue-White Practice.

At Iowa State, Campbell hosted a scrimmage as the Cyclones’ spring game. However, Campbell explained this year’s event will run more like a typical Penn State practice during the first period.

“I kind of see the first 20-25 minutes like what a normal practice would look like,” Campbell said. “Team stretch and then obviously get ourselves a little bit of individual inside run, a little bit of 7-on-7.”

The rest of the event will operate as a typical spring game, with the Nittany Lions running offense and defense together with drives down the field.

“An opportunity to continue to work the football team and work on driving the football in different areas of the field,” Campbell said.

Summer plans

With spring ball ending with practice No. 15 on Saturday, Campbell and his staff have already made plans to keep the team progressing over the 12 weeks of practice this summer.

“The early part of next week is to individually, go through every player with everybody in our program, and get a synopsis of, ‘Where are these players right now? What’s their strengths, what’s their weaknesses?’” Campbell said.

Next week, Campbell hopes to lay out the entirety of summer training separately with each player, highlighting what each athlete needs to do over the next three months to succeed in the fall.

“If you can really evaluate your team now and have a chance to really go to work and put the right pieces together, I think it’ll give us the best opportunity moving forward,” Campbell said.

Injury status

Heading into spring ball, the Nittany Lions were without its two top quarterbacks and wide receivers, who were all recovering from injury.

As of Tuesday, quarterback Rocco Becht has continued to work 7-on-7 drills and backup Alex Manske has officially returned to campus after an unspecified procedure, according to Campbell.

“Obviously the biggest thing that’s been a positive is Rocco getting healthy,” Campbell said. “You’ll see him Saturday. I want him to throw the football in the stadium with our fans and everybody else.”

While Manske won’t play in the Blue-White Practice, Campbell is happy with his progress and expects him to return this summer.

“It’s huge, I think it’s a monumental win for us,” Campbell said. “He’s certainly trending towards where we would want him to be by the time we get to the summer so he can progress his way back.”

As receivers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen continue to work their way back from injury, they’ve had the opportunity to practice individual reps before making their way into playing with the whole team.

“Honestly, it’s been awesome for so many guys to get the foundation of what we’re doing on both sides of the ball,” Campbell said. “Our coaches have done a great job, and honestly, our kids have done a great job. We haven’t wasted a practice.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men’s basketball forward Saša Ciani enters transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
One of Penn State’s transfer additions from last offseason is headed back into the portal.

Forward Saša Ciani has entered the transfer portal with one year of eligibility remaining, according to Sam Kayser of League Ready.

NEWS: Penn State forward Sasa Ciani is entering the transfer portal, @LeagueRDY has learned.The 6-foot-10 forward out of Slovenia played just one season at Penn State. Has also spent time at UIC and Xavier.Averaged 2.1PPG and 1.9RPG this season. Averaged 9.9PPG, 7.6RPG in… pic.twitter.com/JFg8tO7JGJ— Sam Kayser (@KayserHoops) April 21, 2026

Ciani started the first four games of the season, but he then became a rotational player after 7-footer Ivan Jurić took over the starting center job. Ciani appeared in 28 games this year, averaging 2.1 points and 1.9 rebounds per game with 38.9% shooting from the field.

The Nova Gorica, Slovenia, native was a team captain for the Nittany Lions in 2025-26.

This is Ciani’s third time in the transfer portal, as he’s already made stops at Xavier and UIC throughout his collegiate career.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Weekly recap | Penn State softball goes back and forth, one player shines through
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State ended its seven-game home streak with the Bucknell Bison visiting Happy Valley, then entered into enemy territory, the Buckeye State, this past weekend as it faced off for a Big Ten series.

After getting swept at home by the Northwestern Wildcats, the blue and white had a week of games that would put them back on track after a tough weekend.

The Nittany Lions went up against Ohio State for a three-game series, dropping the series opener and finale but winning the middle game of the series. The blue and white left the week with a 2-2 record, defeating Bucknell in a midweek game at home.

Here are the ups and downs of the Nittany Lions' tenth week into the 2026 season.

Top performers

For the blue and white, it faced in-state rival Bucknell for a nonconference matchup.

Penn State dominated the Bison in five innings, getting their 10th run-rule victory of the season, defeating Bucknell 11-3.

The performance in the batter’s box has been the most impressive aspect of the Nittany Lion 2026 squad. Stars like freshman Breanna Hanik and junior Michela Barbanente led the way at the plate for Tuesday's game. Both student-athletes hit bombs that help close out the game in five innings.

Freshman infielder Allison Oneacre was the offensive star of the weekend. In Saturday's meeting, Oneacre broke the single-season RBI Penn State record. In the top of the sixth inning, the rookie crushed a two-run homer. Ultimately, it won by run rule over Ohio State 15-7.

Finishing the night 3-for-5 with three runs scored, three RBI and two home runs. The Alpharetta, Georgia, native excelled offensively over the series.

Penn State started the weekend in Columbus strong, striking first, but ended its weekend with a scoreless run-rule defeat in the series finale.

Saturday's game had the most momentum at the plate as junior infielder Brooke Klosowicz went 2-for-4 with five RBI, scored two runs, while blasting a three-run homer.

That was pretty much all from the Nittany Lion offense, as they were silenced by the Buckeyes’ defense in the series finale.

Junior infielder Kaylie Walters made her return to the plate in the series finale after a midseason injury. Walters hit a slap single in her first at-bat, finishing the day 1-for-2 at the plate.

Inconsistency in final moments

In their tenth week of the 2026 season the blue and white went 2-2, with an 8-10 record in conference play and a 28-17 overall record. The biggest struggle for Penn State was keeping the lead in the final frames.

Consistency overall throughout the season, especially in Big Ten play, has been tough, especially as the course of their weekend in Columbus showed as they struggled to keep efficiency at the plate.

Credit to the Buckeye pitcher, sophomore right-hander Lorin Boutte, as she shut down the Nittany Lion offense over the weekend. Throughout the series, Boutte struck out 11 Penn State batters. With four of those strikeouts coming in Sunday’s run-rule victory.

Penn State gave junior righty McKenna Young the start for the series opener against Ohio State.

She finished the series opener with a total of 62 pitches, facing 16 batters, giving up a single run, walk and three strikeouts over the course of four innings. Looking strong as the Nittany Lions led until the third inning.

Sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton entered the circle in an effort to close out a win for the blue and white, but was stamped with the loss. Pitching the last two innings, the LaVale, Maryland, native was inconsistent in the final stretch of the game that ultimately gave Penn State the loss.

In the circle for the series finale, the blue and white stretched its bullpen. Using three pitchers, looking for a way out of Ohio State’s offensive momentum. Britton got the start and took the loss.

Friday's meeting, Penn State led until the Buckeyes’ offense took the lead in the final stretch of the game. Starting strong with three runs scored in the top of the first, the Nittany Lions showed out but never came out for more.

To close out the weekend, the blue and white dominated Saturday with a run rule to close out the game in six innings, scoring ten runs in the top of the sixth.

As the Penn State order looked to be strong heading into the series finale, it was silent.

Efforts from Oneacre in the first inning of the series finale helped boost the momentum that allowed the blue and white to pull out a win.

The Buckeye offense tallied runs fast in the first three innings, not giving the Nittany Lions a chance to catch up.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Protesters voice concerns about ICE activity at State College Borough Council meeting
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Several public comments were made to the State College Borough Council during their meeting on Monday about Immigrations and Customs Enforcement (ICE) activity in and around State College.

The comments accompanied chants that could be heard inside the State College Municipal Building from a protest held by the People’s Defence Front and Student Committee for Defense and Solidarity (SCDS) about alleged State College Police Department (SCPD) involvement in an ICE arrest.

Nicholas Dudek, a State College resident, said he would like to see the council resume discussions of a resolution calling for reforms to ICE which was tabled during its Feb. 2 meeting.

While ICE has not been in national headlines to the same degree as during the fatal shootings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti in January, Dudek said, he still found it important to call attention to the mistreatment of people detained in ICE detention facilities. He also called attention to the Moshannon Valley Processing Center in Phillipsburg, where a hunger strike is currently taking place to call attention to the conditions there, according to reports by WJAC and WTAJ.

“Everybody has a responsibility to do what we can to try and push it back on that,” Dudek said during the public comment section. “I know a lot of other people who, frankly, aren't straight white men who are financially safe enough to come here and say this and who don't feel as safe as I do and want to know that their local government is going to do something.”

Dudek said the borough could try to implement solutions from other communities such as sensitive location protections, which would prevent ICE from entering certain locations such as churches and hospitals.

He also said prohibitions on masked law enforcement officers and requiring ICE agents to have clear methods of identification should also be enacted along with requiring a warrant for ICE to access online data.

Tracy Sprowls, a member of the Centre County Rapid Response Network, said while the borough has stated that the SCPD does not play a role in ICE operations, she questions if inaction is as harmful to the community as actively participating in arrests.

Sprowls said while several council members have stated to her that they believe enacting an ordinance aimed against ICE activities would draw attention to State College, she believes doing so would show an active effort to keep people safe in the borough.

“Right now, the people who have been bothered by ICE do not feel safe,” Sprowls said during the public comment section. “The wife of the person who was picked up on March 25th will no longer drive her car which means she can no longer go to the grocery store. She can no longer take her kids to school or go to work because she's afraid of getting picked up by ICE.”

Following the closing of public comments, a group of protestors who claimed to be unaffiliated with the People’s Defense Front or SCDS unfurled a banner reading “SC BOROUGH SCPD AID N ABET ICE TERROR” and made a statement advocating for the protections of immigrants and others living in the borough before leaving.

In his report to council, President Evan Myers said it’s important that the council still be willing to accept comments no matter how they are delivered.

“We may be upset because we were accused of certain things, but it's our job to sit up here and listen to people talk to us, to talk their truth to whatever meager power we might have, and so I do appreciate that,” Myers said.

Myers said he personally would like to see more concrete action be taken in the borough to protect immigrants and reemphasized that SCPD was not openly participating in ICE operations and that anything to the contrary was “not what we stand for.”

He said while he found it important to listen to the protestors, it would be better if they directed their grievances towards politicians who are actively supporting ICE activities.

“Perhaps it would be better served if they spoke in front of G.T. Thompson's office or Senator McCormick's office because those are folks that actually have voted to expend funds to aid ICE,” Myers said. “That was the only recommendation I would make in that regard but, again, I think it is important that we listen and I think we heard what was said by those folks.”

MORE BOROUGH COVERAGE

title: Senior Column | Not everybody gets a do-over
author: Alex Osman | The Daily Collegian
description:
“Life doesn’t give us purpose… We give life purpose.” - Barry Allen

In December 2020, a week after turning 18, I sat on the Old Main steps and accepted my offer of admission to Penn State. I was so proud to put “PSU ‘25” in my Instagram bio.

2025. Boy, if only I knew.

I missed that mark, huh? Still, that year ended up being the most important year of my life.

I’m choosing to make the memory of my college experience what I make of it. I will look back fondly on all five years. 2025, though, that one’s important.

2025 was the worst year of my life.

I reached my lowest point ever. Three years of problems finally caught up to me. I had to face the fact that I was not graduating that May. I had plenty of other stuff going on too, and everything compounded. I was a wreck for most of the spring.

2025 was the best year of my life.

I proved to myself that my issues were real, but I could overcome them. I proved to myself that I could actually succeed. I proved to myself that I was allowed to be happy. I proved to myself that I could control my life. I grew up.

Funny how that works out.

Looking back, I feel strangely lucky. It’s not fun having to do a fifth year (or pay for one), but in a big picture kinda way, it was worth it.

I got a do-over. A re-do. A second chance.

Not everybody has that privilege.

I got a year to re-center myself, appreciate where I am, repair friendships, make new ones and above all, actually be happy.

College isn’t just one year, though. There were three Osmans at Penn State.

The first Osman was that bright-eyed kid early on who thought he had everything figured out.

The second Osman got bitch-slapped by life and pretended everything was fine. This Osman thought he had grown up but wasn’t mature enough to face himself. This Osman became unrecognizable without realizing it.

The third Osman was born after that spring of 2025. This Osman finally saw growth as a forever incomplete but active process instead of just something to reflect on after the fact. This Osman finally took control of his life. This is the Osman I am now. This is the Osman I should’ve been all along.

I finally stopped letting life happen to me. I decided to start giving life purpose.

Now, this Osman can look back on the people who impacted all three versions.

Caleb Robert Craig, you old man, the first new friend I made at Penn State. I didn’t get out much freshman year, so I’m glad you were on that Hamilton Hall ground floor. Sophomore year was odd to say the least, but I’m glad you were there for that too.

Sophia, my Collegian mom, you inspired me to work harder, be a leader and follow in your footsteps. Almost literally.

Jade Campos, thanks for introducing me to the Collegian. I’d say that worked out well.

Avani, Kareena, Josie and Juice, you made those middle years. Thanks for letting me watch movies on your couch and pound ‘ronas on your balcony. Yashu, even though I didn’t get to know you ‘till later, I’m glad you’re my friend.

Andrew Gille, I’ve been pals with you for longer than almost anyone in this column. From McDonalds and the State Theatre to 3:46 Sunrise, thanks for being by my side for so long.

Megan, Liv, Phoebe, Gracie, Nick, Braden, Fernanda and the rest of my sophomore/junior year Collegian friends, you made me comfortable coming to the table and print night.

Porter, Parker and Joe, you didn’t go here, but that South O apartment was like another home, especially when the going got rough.

Brenanne, you made my first leadership experience a breeze and supported me personally and professionally. Multi-editing is still my favorite thing I did at the Collegian. A ton of that was because of you. I’m grateful we’re still close to this day.

Amy and Sam, we went through a lot, didn’t we? Sorry for making our Top 3 experience harder than it had to be, but I’ll still remember those cigs with a smile.

Teagan, Maggie, Jackson and Cearra, thanks for drinking sahrs and $5 pitchers with me.

Nick Gofas, my favorite TDC candidate, you’ve been here more times than I can count. Thanks for housing stews (hold the Griffin) with me for six years now.

Zach, thanks for being a brother. That trip with Gof was one of my favorite experiences lately, and I needed it more than I realized. Love ya, kid.

Kaitlyn Murphy! Ohhhhhhh boy oh boy oh boy. I already said so much when you #expired so I’ll just run through the cliffnotes here. Who woulda thought after the Dark Ages (#mybad) we’d be where we are now. I proudly consider you the best friend I made at Penn State. No person on the planet shares a brain with me like you do. I miss you, but I’m so friggin happy that you’ll be a lifelong friend. Yippee!

The Dark Knight is still overrated, though. Sorry.

Trevor, Matt and Sonny, working with 3:46 was the best thing I did in college. That’s five years of memories and you guys made one helluva impact. This time last year, I never could have imagined I’d be crying on stage at Doggie’s with a band I’m only kinda-sorta-technically-maybe-but-really-very-much a part of.

Doing what I did this year with 3:46 opened my eyes to what I truly love in the media world. If I hadn’t been a 5th year or hadn’t met you guys, I would still be speaking suspiciously vaguely about what I really love to do.

Love you guys and thank you for letting me enter your world. I’m excited to see you make it big next year. Even without such a cool manager.

Camryn, Emilia, Thomas, Chloe, Avery, Cassidi and my other CommAgency friends, it's been great getting to know you all. Sorry it took me so long to hang in the suite, even as a historically long-tenured member.

Of course, after five years as a multimedia candidate, videographer, multi editor, DME, d-teamer and photographer, thank you, The Daily Collegian.

And finally, the three most important people:

Mom, Dad and Anna. Thank you for being the biggest constants in my life through all five years. Thank you for unquestionably supporting me. Thank you for being the people I can always count on to inspire me, talk to and learn from. I love you infinitely.

Thank you, everyone, for five years of memories. Thank you, everyone, for making me the man I am today and the man I have yet to become. Thank you, everyone, for making me glad I’m still here.

Thank you, everyone, for everything.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse opens Big Ten Tournament with Michigan rematch
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coach Kayla Treanor’s first regular season in Happy Valley is officially over, and Penn State is heading to the playoffs.

The Nittany Lions face fourth-seeded Michigan at noon on Wednesday to kick off the quarterfinals of the Big Ten Tournament.

While the blue and white fell to the Wolverines earlier this season, it will look for an early tournament upset to boost its NCAA tournament chances.

All-time matchups

Heading into this year, the series was all tied, 6-6. Since Michigan took the regular season victory in 2026, it took control of the all-time record.

That same dominance has been displayed in recent years, as the Wolverines are riding a 5-1 record against the Nittany Lions into the postseason. When the two squads began facing each other in 2014, the blue and white won four straight before Michigan took over with three straight wins.

That success continued after one win from Penn State, as the Wolverines won another four straight. In 2025, the blue and white earned a one-goal victory in overtime before the Wolverines dominated the Nittany Lions on Senior Day this year.

2026 Wolverines

Michigan's season got off on the wrong foot after being upset by No. 25 Notre Dame to start the year. That script flipped, however, as it then upset No. 5 Florida and No. 6 Boston College in the following weeks.

Besides another upset loss to No. 16 Yale, the Wolverines had 10 straight wins, including ranked victories over Colorado, Penn State, Denver and Ohio State.

Over the last two weeks, however, Michigan has had its share of both underwhelming performances and standout victories.

The Wolverines lost two straight contests to unranked Rutgers and No. 9 Johns Hopkins, where the Blue Jays scored twice as many goals as the maize and blue. Four days later, Michigan dominated No. 3 Maryland in a 15-4 victory before closing the season with a two-goal loss to Northwestern.

Regular season matchup

The Wolverines overcame Penn State’s large crowd at Senior Day and Alumni Day to earn an 11-6 victory on March 22.

Michigan pulled out with an early 3-0 lead courtesy of two goals by graduate midfielder Ava Class, who scored four total against the Nittany Lions. Aiding the midseason All-American was Ceci Stein, Caroline Byrd and Calli Norris, who each had two goals apiece.

While the Nittany Lions had three 2-0 runs with two-goal performances by Payton Wainman and Kelly MacKinney, a 6-0 run by Michigan from the end of the first quarter through the second quarter created an insurmountable lead.

Goalkeeper Sydney Manning played all 60 minutes for the blue and white, recording a 50% save record with 11 saves.

Wednesday’s contest

Michigan has two major advantages heading into Wednesday’s game — the higher seed and home-field advantage.

The 2026 Big Ten Tournament is being played in Ann Arbor, where the Wolverines have a 6-2 record this year. However, Penn State hasn’t struggled much in enemy territory, posting a 7-1 road record.

While Michigan is coming off of a big loss to the Wildcats, the Nittany Lions are coming off of one of their highest-scoring contests in recent weeks, defeating Rutgers 18-15.

Senior Erika Ho put on a career performance on Saturday, scoring six goals with an assist. Wainman, MacKinney and freshman phenom Rocquette Allen each recorded hat tricks amidst their standout seasons and Delaney Radin set the single-season assist record, finishing the regular season with 53 assists.

Both squads have their fair share of weapons, so Wednesday’s matchup will be determined by whichever team can perform best when the pressure is on.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Senior Column | The wisdom of being an Unc
author: Isabella Viteri | The Daily Collegian
description:
“The thing about new beginnings is that they require something else to end.” – Blair Waldorf, Gossip Girl

“But if it weren’t crazy, they wouldn’t call it a leap of faith.” – Eleanor Shellstrop, The Good Place

“Believing in even the possibility of a happy ending is a powerful thing, but living with that kind of belief, that’s the most powerful thing of all. That’s hope.” – Mary Margaret, Once Upon a Time

I’ve watched these shows over and over, but their endings are starting to hit differently now. Leaving hurts, even when it comes with the excitement of what’s next. When we’re younger, we treat every milestone like a finish line. Now it feels more like a checkpoint in a much longer race. We spend so much time looking ahead that we forget to slow down and take in the moments that matter.

And now, whether I like it or not, this is one of those endings.

It’s not just the end of college. It’s the last time living with these friends, the last late-night Taco Bell runs and the last random drives where nothing and everything mattered at the same time. It’s the last time playing with the athletic bands that gave me purpose for four years, the last time marching through the football tunnel and the last time watching Penn State men’s hockey as a student and falling in love with the sport over and over again. As much as that hurts, I know something else, too. If I were able to experience this kind of joy once, I can find it again in a different chapter of life.

Looking back, coming to Penn State was one of my first real leaps of faith. I moved more than 12 hours away from home without ever visiting campus. Someone once mentioned the school, and for some reason, it stuck. No school in Georgia felt right, and I didn’t have a family connection guiding me. So I applied, got in and committed before I even saw it in person.

When my mom and I finally arrived for New Student Orientation, stepping out of that tiny airport and into Pennsylvania felt surreal. It was beautiful, unfamiliar and overwhelming all at once. I got lost more times than I can count, and honestly, I still do. But something about that visit confirmed I had made the right decision.

That didn’t mean it was easy.

I started as an aerospace engineering major in Air Force ROTC while also being in Blue Band. My schedule was packed with early morning training, 8 a.m. classes and constant pressure. I thought I had everything planned out, including a career, a path and a version of success that made sense on paper.

By November, I was overwhelmed and questioning everything. I remember calling my mom in tears, unsure if I could keep going. She told me to finish the semester and that we would figure things out after. That moment, and her belief in me, is a big part of why I’m still here.

Eventually, I stopped trying to force a version of my life that didn’t feel right and started paying attention to what actually made me happy. I changed my major to advertising, found my place in The Daily Collegian and leaned into the communities that made Penn State feel like home.

Somehow, four years later, things worked out. Just not in the way I expected.

To Sarah, my best friend and the best surprise I could have asked for at Penn State, I never would have thought playing sousaphone alongside you would turn into what we have now. I think our first interaction went something like, “Are you a girl? Do you play the sousaphone? Do you want to be friends?” Well, yes, Sarah, to all of those questions. From that moment on, we became a package deal wherever we went. Becoming your friend, your roommate and your partner in bad decisions was one of the best decisions I’ve ever made. Thank you for being there for the laughs, the tears and the complaining. You deserve the absolute world.

To Alexia, my dawg, I never thought we would get to this point. I remember the late nights at ballroom and the crazy travel memories that helped build such a strong friendship. Thank you for all the laughs, the silly side quests and for always letting me crash at your house.

To my sousaphone section Kacie, Hannah, Sarah C., Sarah W., Heidi, Ellie, JJ, Henry, Lucas, Nick, Kurt, Aidan, Ben, Vinnie, Francis, Bryan, Gavin, DiGioa, Dixon, Jake, Jeremiah, Jesse, Justy, Luca, Max Anthony, Jacob, Spencer, Evan, Nev, Tarun and Melvin, thank you for keeping me both sane and a little crazy. A big part of why I was able to truly enjoy college is because I started and am ending my journey with you all, whether it was marching on the field, playing flag football or going to group dinners. Thank you to everyone who was part of it. I love you all.

To my favorite professor, Dr. Elliott, thank you for the way you teach and support your students. I’ve taken your classes since my freshman year and always made it a point to take more whenever I could. Your classes are the reason I fell in love with psychology and began to question ideas like “color isn’t real” or how we each perceive the world differently. As I leave, I can finally say what you end every class with: stay safe and take care.

Para mi familia, a mis abuelitos, gracias por su apoyo en todo lo que hago. Gracias por cada rezo al angelito que me mandan cuando estoy en la escuela, y abuelita, gracias por contestarme el teléfono cuando camino sola. Me estresas un poco, pero te quiero siempre.

A mi tío Juan, gracias por ser un ejemplo en mi vida. Me has impulsado a ver el mundo cuando puedo y a ser fuerte y paciente con la familia.

A mi tía Adriana y a mi tío Pato, finalmente es tiempo de graduarme de la universidad. Gracias por estar ahí cuando los necesito y por los chistes en cada juego de mesa. Sí, tío Pato, te gané esa vez en Tren Mexicano.

A mis primos Felipe y Matías, gracias por invitarme cuando pueden y recordarme que, con tanto trabajo, a veces necesito relajarme también.

To Dodo, it has been a long journey for us, and our choices have shaped how we have remained in each other’s lives. Thank you for choosing to be part of mine. Even through difficult and challenging times, I continue to choose you, because you’re the only dad I want in my life.

Para mi mamá, ya llegamos finalmente. Thank you for every sacrifice you have made so I could be here. You are the reason I work so hard and keep going, even when things feel uncertain. Tú eres mi razón para seguir adelante, como yo soy la tuya. I am so lucky to have you as my mom, and through everything, we continue to stand by each other because that is what makes life worth living.

As I move forward, I know I will keep making mistakes, taking risks and figuring things out as I go. I may not know exactly where I am headed, but I do know this.

Take the leap anyway.

You don’t always need logic or a perfect plan. Sometimes the best things come from the choices that don’t make complete sense at the time.

And if you don’t believe me, that’s fine. I’ve officially reached unc status, and uncs always have a little wisdom to offer.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Weekly recap | Penn State men’s volleyball earns EIVA regular-season title with wins over George Mason
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
After back-to-back games against George Mason last weekend, Penn State officially clinched the EIVA regular-season title.

Penn State played five sets in both matches last weekend and left with a victorious 3-2 outcome in both games, solidifying an undefeated 12-0 record in the EIVA.

Friday’s win against the Patriots gave the Nittany Lions the EIVA regular-season title, guaranteeing them a first-round bye and home-court advantage for the EIVA Tournament.

Now on a nine-game winning streak, the blue and white will look to keep its momentum going as the postseason approaches.

Here’s a look at how last weekend went for the Nittany Lions.

Strong offensive performance

It was a big weekend of kills on the attack for the blue and white. Across both games, it totaled 121 kills.

On Friday, Matthew Luoma led the Nittany Lions in kills with 23. This marked the outside hitter’s season high.

Trailing just behind was Sean Harvey with 20 kills on Friday. But across the entire weekend, the redshirt sophomore tallied 38 kills.

But in terms of kills, the real star of the weekend was Tristan Hassell. As just a freshman, the opposite hitter had 26 kills on Saturday, a stark difference from the one kill he tallied on Friday.

Hassell’s 26-kill match was a new career record. He also set a career-high 31 points in the same match against the Patriots.

Solid defensive game

While the attack was a highlight for the blue and white last weekend, it was equally as strong on the defensive end.

Harvey had a season-high 15 digs on Saturday, with six additional digs coming from Friday’s match.

Harvey also had five blocks in Friday’s match, marking another season-high.

He wasn’t the only member of the blue and white roster who played a good defensive game last weekend.

Owen Rose had eight blocks in Saturday’s match against George Mason, with an additional five on Friday. Hassell also had five blocks on Saturday.

Ryan Merk was another strong piece to the blue and white’s defense over the weekend. The libero totaled 20 digs across the two matches, with 10 in each game.

In total, the Nittany Lions tallied 21 blocks and 90 digs in both games, proving a strong defensive outing that they’ll look to continue ahead of the postseason.

Too many service errors

If there is anything last weekend revealed, it was that the blue and white might need to look to improve its service game.

Friday’s match saw just four service aces, while Saturday’s match had seven.

The number of service errors, however, was noticeably higher. Friday saw 25 service errors, while Saturday had 18, totaling 43 over the course of the weekend.

As the Nittany Lions gear up for postseason play, it will be imperative to avoid giving up easy points to service errors.

But it wasn’t all negative. Michael Schwob had four service aces across both games, with two in each match. Bennett Wilson and Hassell both had two in Saturday’s game, which marked a season-high for Wilson.

As the No. 1 seed in the EIVA Tournament, the blue and white will be set to play on Thursday following a bye. It will begin tournament play in the semifinal round against the winner of the match between No. 4-seed Charleston and No. 5-seed Sacred Heart.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Senior Column | Four Years, One Article
author: Jonathan Zavialov I The Daily Collegian
description:
I spent four years at The Daily Collegian doing a lot of things, and writing was not one of them.

I joined The Daily Collegian halfway through my freshman year for a pretty simple reason: I wanted to learn more technical skills. I was a Computer Science major looking for something practical. It also helped that I lived in Hamilton Hall and Willard was right across the street. If it had been on the other side of campus, who knows.

If I had actually understood what “Business Insights” meant at the time, I probably wouldn’t have joined at all. When I got there, it didn’t exactly feel like I had made a great decision. The department was disorganized. No one really knew what we were working on. Most of what we did felt like busy work. We never interacted with the news side or any other teams. Looking back, I don’t think I could have explained what our role was if you asked me.

Sophomore year, things changed. At that point, it was just me and Marcelo Santos, the first of many good friends I'd make at the Collegian. Two people doesn't leave much room for a leadership race, so naturally we both became directors of the department. This is when I met Luke Vargas, the business manager. Up to that point, I don’t think I had ever met someone my age who acted like a real leader. I always assumed that kind of presence came with decades of experience.

Luke didn’t see it that way. He talked a lot about responsibility and what he called sacrificial leadership — the idea that if you commit yourself fully to something, it gives back more than you expect. At the time, I didn’t think much of it, but his words stuck with me.

That same year, The Daily Collegian lost all its funding from the university.

I still remember the feeling when we found out. It was quiet at first, then a mix of confusion and panic. For a long time, we had operated without much structure or expectation. There was always funding, so there was no real pressure to make the business work. We stayed busy, but not in a way that required anything to actually work. Losing that support changed everything overnight.

For the first time, it felt like the Collegian was fully ours. Not in a symbolic way, but in a real one. If the business didn’t work, it was on us. A group of undergrads with little experience suddenly had to figure out how to run something sustainable.

While everyone was trying to process what this meant, Luke focused on what came next. He treated it like a challenge instead of a setback. He expected more from us, and, over time, we started to expect more from ourselves. I watched as the organization started to change in real time. Through our efforts, it slowly became more focused, more structured, more intentional. It stopped feeling like something we were part of and started feeling like something we were responsible for.

By junior year, I was fully in it. I started working closely with my new co-director, Rohan Badami. He pushed me in ways I wouldn’t have pushed myself and thought much bigger than I did. A lot of what we built came from him asking questions I didn’t think to ask.

Arya Cheeti was another one of those people. He saw things in me that I didn’t see in myself and never hesitated to say it. That kind of confidence, especially early on, goes a long way. It never quite sat right with me how that ended.

That’s when the work started to feel real. I led my team to build out projects like a social media analytics tracker and a DEI content audit tool. We piloted a fully in house games platform for our website. We had to figure out what to build, why it mattered and how to actually make it work.

Over time, I interviewed and brought on a team of people who were all smarter than I was in different ways. That part meant more to me than any project. The work mattered, but the people mattered more. They showed up, figured things out, and made everything better than I could have done on my own.

That's where I learned how to lead. Not from a class, but from trying to make things work with real people and constraints. It was up to me to decide what we worked on, how we approached it and what direction to take. I slowly turned the business insights department into a software department. All our projects were development work, and I used it as my own testing ground for AI tools, for new technologies, for whatever I wanted to learn next. We deployed our projects to a platform with over 1M+ monthly views. Getting to do that while AI was evolving as fast as it was and applying it to workflows that hadn't changed in years, was something I don't think I'll get again anytime soon. That kind of autonomy only exists because the Collegian is student-run. But it also comes with pressure. Especially after we lost funding, there was no one else to fall back on. But that pressure is what made it meaningful.

Now, it’s in a completely different place than when I joined. It’s more organized, more connected and, most importantly, it works. People know what they’re doing and why they’re doing it. There’s a level of ownership that didn’t exist before.

Getting to be part of that transformation, and to see where it is now, is something I’m proud of. It also makes leaving harder than I expected. It took me four years to finally write something for the Collegian, which feels about right, all things considered. But I’m glad I did.

The Collegian also gave me more than I expected outside of the work itself. The people I met here opened doors professionally, and a lot of the opportunities I’ve had since can be traced back to this place. The Alumni Interest Group has also supported me in ways I didn’t expect, including a scholarship that made a real difference for me.

More than anything, I’m grateful for the people who worked with me, who trusted me and who made the work worth doing. Grateful for the responsibility we were given, even when we weren’t ready for it. And grateful that the Collegian is the kind of place where students are allowed to build something real.

I didn’t join for that, but I’m leaving with it.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Penn State football draft profile | DE Dani Dennis-Sutton brings raw physicality
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Expectations were high for defensive end Dani Dennis-Sutton after announcing his return for the 2025 season, saying there was “unfinished business” for him to take care of.

While his stats didn’t necessarily decline, Dennis-Sutton had an up-and-down season. He flashed in some games, but was completely invisible in others.

Despite the disappointment, Dennis-Sutton was still one of the best pass rushers in the Big Ten last year, finishing top 10 in the conference in sacks and tackles for loss.

“It was a tough season, but I think I’ve learned from it, I think I’ve grown from it,” Dennis-Sutton said at Penn State Pro Day. “My leadership skills have grown tremendously this year, and I wanted to finish the season off strong with those guys.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-6

Weight: 261 pounds

Bench press: 26 reps

40-yard dash: 4.63 seconds

Vertical jump: 39.5 inches

Broad jump: 10-foot-11

3-cone drill: 6.9 seconds

Projections

Projected round drafted: Rounds 2-3

Player comparison: Danielle Hunter

Scouting report

Between Penn State Pro Day and the NFL Scouting Combine, Dennis-Sutton had numerous opportunities to showcase his potential to scouts.

The 6-foot-6 defensive end is the ideal size for that position in the NFL, and Dennis-Sutton showcased flashes of speed and physicality throughout the 2025 season. Several times during the year, Dennis-Sutton was among the first defenders to shed their block, earning 8.5 sacks and 12 tackles for loss.

Dennis-Sutton’s 40-yard dash time ranked sixth among defensive ends during the combine, but his speed off the line was on display all season, as he generated 45 pressures across the year.

However, his talents went beyond his work against opposing offenses. On special teams, Dennis-Sutton tied the single-season record for blocked punts in 2025, recording back-to-back blocked punts against UCLA and Northwestern with his third coming against Michigan State.

He finished his collegiate career tied for fifth in program history with seven career forced fumbles and sixth with 23.5 sacks.

Additionally, Dennis-Sutton showcased his leadership this season by being the only draft-eligible player to suit up in the Pinstripe Bowl. It paid dividends for the defensive end, as he tallied two sacks and two tackles for loss.

Best team fit: Dallas Cowboys

Over the past year, the Dallas Cowboys have lost two of its most important defensive linemen in Micah Parsons and Solomon Thomas in trades to the Green Bay Packers and Tennessee Titans.

Parsons’ departure just days before the season left a big hole during 2025 — one that Dennis-Sutton could fill this year.

The Cowboys gave up the third-most yards and the most points per game last season. While Dennis-Sutton may not make an immediate impact like former Nittany Lion and first-round pick Parsons, Dennis-Sutton could learn from several NFL veterans in Jonathan Bullard and Kenny Clark.

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: Mock draft roundup | Where are Penn State players projected days away from NFL draft?
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
It’s officially one of the most impactful weeks of the NFL schedule: draft week. Multiple Penn State players will hear their names called in Pittsburgh, making their dreams come true.

ESPN and NFL Network just released a full, seven-round mock draft on Monday as the draft is just a couple of days away.

Here’s where some former Penn Staters are mocked for the upcoming draft.

Vega Ioane, offensive lineman, 1st round

A perennial first round pick, Vega Ioane continues to get mocked in the middle of the first round.

ESPN’s Jordan Reid has Ioane going to the Baltimore Ravens at No. 14, while NFL Network’s Chad Reuter has the former Nittany Lion going to the Ravens’ AFC North rival, the Pittsburgh Steelers at No. 21.

Both teams have a need inside at guard, with the Steelers losing Isaac Seumalo to free agency and the Ravens’ interior being on the weaker side. The addition of Ioane would be an immediate upgrade for either team.

Dani Dennis-Sutton, defensive end, 2nd round

Dani Dennis-Sutton has taken a noticeable rise in recent mock drafts, with each one having the former Nittany Lion selected in the late second round.

Dennis-Sutton tested well at the NFL Scouting Combine and as a result, rose in mock drafts, making him a probable second-round pick. Reid has the pass rusher going 52nd overall to the Green Bay Packers, while Reuter takes him just five picks later to the Chicago Bears, both toward the end of the second round.

Dennis-Sutton proved his abilities as the lead pass rusher in 2025 and could be a reliable threat on the edge for any team that selects him.

Zakee Wheatley, safety, 3rd round

The consensus in the most recent mock drafts has Zakee Wheatley staying in state and going just one pick apart.

In Reid’s, Wheatley gets selected 98th overall to the Philadelphia Eagles, while Reuter has him going one pick later to the Steelers in the third round.

Both teams have some question marks, as Jaylen Ramsey’s comfortability with safety isn’t confirmed and the other options not quite as strong. For the Eagles, projected strong safety starter Marcus Epps hasn’t played as well the past two seasons, making the safety selection more reasonable.

Either spot would give Wheatley an opportunity to earn a starting role early in his career, joining the recent group of former Nittany Lions to find success at safety in the pros.

Drew Allar, quarterback, Day 3

Despite some questions earlier in the year on where Drew Allar would go, the consensus seems to be on the third day. Reid and Reuter both have Allar going in the 4th round, with Reid’s mock draft having him go 103rd overall to the New York Jets.

Reuter has Allar going just 25 picks later to the Detroit Lions, who have an established starting quarterback in Jared Goff. However, Goff is 31 years old and another few years could allow Allar to start.

Allar wouldn’t have to play right away for either team, as both have veteran quarterbacks who will start to begin the year. This will allow him to sit, develop and work out the kinks of the “project” title he’s been given since the draft process started.

While there was often a high difference in Allar’s selection, his draft stock seems to be sitting at the fourth round just three days away from the draft.

Kaytron Allen, running back, Day 2 or later

Kaytron Allen has the most polarizing selections of the former Nittany Lions. Allen is likely to go late in the second day or early in the third day, but Reid doesn’t have him getting selected at all across the seven-round mock draft.

On the other side, Reuter has Allen going in the third round to the Miami Dolphins at No. 90 overall, which seems to be a more reasonable spot.

If he goes to the Dolphins, he’d be in a deep running back room, one the Dolphins have invested in over the past couple of drafts.

Allen proved he has top running back capabilities and despite the difference in mock draft spots, he’s likely to be in that middle round location.

Nick Singleton, running back, Day 3

After having a down year, Nick Singleton’s draft stock has taken a significant drop. Now fully recovered from his injury, he has under a week to prove himself to raise the stock back up.

Entering the 2025 season, Singleton was seen as one of the top running backs in the class. However, he’s now seen as a mid-round pick as Reid has him going in the 5th round to the Packers. On the other hand, Reuter has him 20 picks higher, in the 4th round, to the Jets.

Both options are interesting selections because neither team has any need for a running back. The Packers have Josh Jacobs and the Jets have Breece Hall, meaning Singleton would have to work for No. 2 running back reps behind the star tailbacks.

No matter where he goes, the team is getting a seemingly high-upside running back, who has breakaway speed that could boost the offense of any team he’s on.

Other late round projections

The rest of the former Penn Staters have a wide variety of locations in the draft. Some are selected in one mock draft and not selected in another.

Reid has a Nittany Lion going in each of the final three rounds with defensive tackle Zane Durant getting taken at No. 157 by the Carolina Panthers, offensive tackle Drew Shelton selected No. 193 in the 6th round to the New York Giants and offensive tackle Nolan Rucci getting taken No. 252 overall to the Los Angeles Rams.

Reuter, however, has just Durant getting drafted, but 16 picks higher in the 4th round to the Eagles. Neither Shelton nor Rucci were taken in the NFL Network mock draft, showing the difference in the drafts and the variability of the two prospects’ fate.
