title: Senior Column | Be Happy
author: Vasco Chen | The Daily Collegian
description:
I was in my very first semester at Penn State as a forensic science student when I first wondered if I had picked the wrong major. The lab work for CHEM 110 had gotten so confusing that I couldn't even follow my classmates' train of thought as they arrived at their answers.

But instead of realizing my major didn't suit me, I blamed myself for not studying hard enough. I thought that if I had put in more effort, I could’ve done better.

I already wasn't doing so great mentally. There was a lot of pressure coming from my mom, who constantly reminded me how I'm behind people my age after spending four years in the Marine Corps. What I really needed after my military service was a long break to recuperate and transition to civilian life, but instead, I chose to return to school immediately for my mom’s sake. She held the typical Chinese mindset, believing that education is everything and that everything is a competition. My high school friends were making banks, working for Google, for Microsoft and here I was just starting college.

With the stress from all my STEM classes piled on top of that, it came as no surprise that I became depressed in my second year. I lacked the motivation to do anything. I struggled to get out of bed, couldn’t maintain personal hygiene and I was afraid to even check messages from my friends. It felt suffocating to think of all the responsibilities I’ve yet to take care of. At the time, I thought I was simply being lazy and my self-hatred continued to fester.

I didn't realize it was depression until my third year, so I never got any professional help either. By then, I had finally begun to consider changing my major, but it wasn’t until I was academically suspended for two semesters that I finally made up my mind.

Cooped up in my room back home with my mom breathing down my neck, I told myself things couldn’t continue like this. No more deprecating myself for being a failure, because that wouldn't fix anything. I must take proactive measures to change, starting from the little things.

While lying about taking online classes, I used this time off to seek self improvement. I tried to fix my sleeping schedule, exercise daily and help around the house more. Gradually, I regained the motivation to do things again. Then, I worked up the courage to log in to LionPATH, avoided looking at the grades section and applied for a change of major to digital and print journalism.

I’ve always loved to write, my passion for which stemmed from fanfiction. For me, writing is a form of therapy, a way to escape from reality and become someone I could never be. But I’ve never considered a writing major because writing fanfics was my guilty pleasure, a secret I was too embarrassed to tell people due to the stigmas associated with it. I was ashamed to even think my amateur writing is good enough to make a career out of.

Now, I’ve realized none of that matters. Whether it’s stigma or other people’s opinions, only my own happiness matters. Writing makes me happy, so I will continue to write.

After returning to school, I applied for an off-campus shared housing called Co.Space, where over twenty people live together to build a network and make changes to this world. I met so many talented people there who inspired me to step out of my comfort zone so I could one day be as amazing as they are.

I became who I was back when I first came to Penn State: a bright-eyed individual curious about everything and eager to try new things. I signed up for all the clubs I was interested in but never had the time for, each door leading me to more doors of opportunity. Eventually, I was even able to study abroad in Argentina, where a story I reported on will be aired on TV. The old me, who was strangled by depression and afraid to reach out for the light, could never imagine doing all these things one day.

So here’s my advice for you. To anyone reading this and considering changing your major, do it. It’s not too late; it’s never too late. Don’t let anyone decide for you. You can consider their opinions, but ultimately it’s your life and it must be you who makes that decision. Instead of getting into debt for a major that makes you miserable, why not choose what makes you happy and pay back student loans with a smile on your face?

To everyone who has helped me along the way, thank you.

To my parents, thank you for having my back despite initially not agreeing with my decision to change my major. I’m glad I can change your mind and I’m beyond grateful for all the love and support you’ve given me.

To my brother, thank you for always sending me memes and funny videos. You’re the only one who sends me videos of our dog regularly and it keeps me motivated to keep trudging forward.

To my dog Honey, thank you for being a fluffball of sunshine. You’re the best in the world and I wouldn't trade you for anything.

To my former Co.Space roommate, Olivia, you’re everything I never thought I could be. Seeing you work tirelessly to juggle your dozens of responsibilities while still keeping a positive attitude inspired me to put myself out there more. I wouldn't have become a speaker consultant for TEDxPSU if I hadn't become roommates with you.

To Elyse, thank you for everything you’ve done to make our dance cover a success. I had such a fun time dancing with you in KPMD. I hope you will realize that what makes you happy is more important than what your parents say. I know you can be a wonderful English teacher one day, and I wish you the best of luck in whatever you choose to do.

To Mariah, thank you for being my friend. I hope you will be able to seek therapy for your depression and reply to my messages one day. I look forward to hanging out with you again.

To Shaheen, thank you for being the best professor I’ve ever had at Penn State. You’re not only an incredible journalist but also someone born to teach. I’ve learned so much from you and I can’t thank you enough for encouraging me to apply for that study abroad class. You make me look forward to getting out of bed and going to class every day. Your class assignments make me want to hide under a cover and cry sometimes, but I’ve also gained valuable experiences from them.

To The Daily Collegian, thank you for giving me a chance to let my writing shine. To my editors, Mia, Rachael, Nadya and Kaitlyn, thank you for providing great feedback on my stories and making me a better writer.

Finally, thank you to everyone who has read this far. Writing this column is taking me back to the days of writing for myself, and it truly is as uplifting as it ever was. I hope my story can help someone who’s going through the same thing I was.

I think life is all about the pursuit of happiness, of finding things that make you look forward to the next day. As Levi Ackerman once said, “Choose whichever decision you'll regret the least, because no one knows what the outcome will be.”

If you’re confused and struggling, just seek happiness and you will be alright.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Penn State football draft profile | QB Drew Allar’s arm strength unmatched among fellow prospects
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Drew Allar’s last moments as Penn State’s starting quarterback occurred on the grass at Beaver Stadium. He sat there under the pitch-black sky with James Franklin and the medical staff surrounding him after he had tried to scramble on a crucial third down late in the fourth quarter against Northwestern.

Just like that, the Allar era was over. Franklin confirmed in his last postgame press conference as the Nittany Lions’ head coach that Allar was out for the season, and the quarterback many believed could finally take Penn State to a national championship victory had officially failed to meet those expectations.

“Obviously, it wasn't the ideal ending to the season, me being out, but I took that as an opportunity to kind of grow and learn from that and make myself a better player, both athletically and obviously, just mechanically and that sort of thing,” Allar said at Penn State Pro Day. “So I just took it as an opportunity to better myself.”

Allar finished his collegiate career with 7,402 passing yards, 61 touchdowns, 13 interceptions and a 63.2% completion percentage, which ranks first in program history. His passing yards rank fourth all-time, while his touchdown passes rank third.

But a poor start to the 2025 season and some serious mistakes in big moments have led to Allar being a confusing prospect. Regardless, a team will surely take a chance on his raw talent and competitive nature.

“I feel like I've shown growth each year I played quarterback, and then I think I showed growth from the end of my senior year to the combine, and in this setting, I feel really good about all the work that I put into it,” Allar said.

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-5

Weight: 232 pounds

Projections

Projected round drafted: Rounds 3-5

Player comparison: Joe Flacco

Scouting report

In the right environment, Allar could carve out a solid NFL career. But he needs to fix some serious flaws before that can come to fruition.

His footwork is the main knock on his game, and he said at pro day he’s been telling teams that’s his main weakness. Allar also struggles with consistency, as one throw might look stellar, but then the next he’s missing a wide-open guy.

Allar’s mental strength has also been considered somewhat of a vulnerability, as he often struggles in big games and moments. The two obvious ones are his late interception in the Orange Bowl and game-losing interception against Oregon in overtime last season.

But even outside of those two moments, Allar underperformed against Ohio State in 2023 and 2024, and he threw for just 70 yards in Penn State’s loss to Michigan in 2023. He infamously cried postgame after the loss to the Buckeyes in his first season as a starter, resulting in concerns about his emotions regularly getting the best of him.

Some of these weaknesses aren’t entirely Allar’s fault, however. His wide receivers were major weaknesses throughout his three years as a starter, even though Penn State attempted to fill that void last offseason.

Allar’s strengths, however, are what have made him such a polarizing prospect. His arm strength is the best among all draft-eligible quarterbacks, and he can make throws others simply can’t.

His size is also ideal for the NFL, as he stands at 6-foot-5, 232 pounds and has nearly 10-inch hands. Similar to his arm strength, Allar’s frame is arguably the best for the big leagues compared to his fellow prospects.

Allar also grew as a runner later in his Penn State career, tallying 8.9 yards per scramble and 4.73 yards after contact in 2025. He finished his four seasons as a Nittany Lion with 732 rushing yards and 12 rushing touchdowns.

Best team fit: Los Angeles Rams

Allar needs a team that has a veteran starter and elite offensive head coach to help him develop and fix his weaknesses. The Los Angeles Rams fit that bill almost perfectly.

Matthew Stafford, who won NFL MVP last season, is nearing the end of his career at 38 years old, but has been one of the top quarterbacks in the league for a long time and would be the perfect mentor for Allar.

On the other hand, Rams’ head coach Sean McVay is one of the best offensive minds in the NFL and offensive coordinator Nate Scheelhaase knows plenty about playing and coaching quarterbacks. Los Angeles and Allar just seem to fit well together as the Rams look toward a future without Stafford.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Senior Column | It's about the journey, not the destination
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
Well, my time at Penn State is coming to a close, which means my time at The Daily Collegian is coming to an end. It’s crazy to think this moment has arrived. Through all the ups and downs, being on the sports staff really helped me put one foot forward in achieving my goals and dreams. Now that the end is near, it’s time to recap this whole experience.

I remember walking into the new side of Willard, walking down the wooden stairs and being like WOAH! There were a lot of clubs and opportunities, which was a lot to take in all at once. I made my way through and found the Collegian and never looked back.

Let's start from the beginning. I joined in the fall of 2022 on the men’s soccer beat; it was the first step to my dream of becoming a sports journalist. I was full of excitement and ready to work with the guidance of my candidate buddy, Liam Wichser. I enjoyed my time on the beat, but what I was really excited about was covering men’s lacrosse.

Lacrosse was a sport that meant a lot to me. It was big in my family, as many of my family members played the game. I never got to play, so being able to be on the beat was a great feeling. My first year on the staff, the team made the Final Four, which made it a really fun season to cover.

Time moved fast and the next fall I decided to take my talents to the women’s soccer beat. I will say this beat was one of my favorites as it was Lyle Alenstein and I, later adding Ryan McKenna and Chase Mitchell as candidates. The beat was electric and full of energy. I also finally learned how to use Twitter correctly. Olivia Estright knows the whole story on this one. (Yes it's still Twitter, not X) and continued with my usual spring beat of men’s lacrosse. Each year on each beat, I could see my writing and overall reporting improve.

Enter my third year. DANANANANANA (for those who don’t know it's “Super Mario Bros.” theme music) This is the biggest and most important one. I really started to focus on not just my writing and reporting as a sports journalist, but also the people I was doing it with.

This was the start of memories and traditions.

I decided to take my talents back to the men’s soccer beat, which ended up being worth it, because I was working toward one goal. Being. On. The. Wrestling. Beat. That was my main focus for my junior year.

I applied to be a part of the wrestling staff and nervously waited for the results, but it was good news; I got the position. This was one of the first moments I knew my work had paid off. Wrestling was another one of those important sports to me, which made it a great feeling to not only cover the sport, but also one of the greatest dynasties of all-time, if not the greatest.

My beatmates Evan Smith and Andrew Deal really made it fun to cover the beat, but also outside the office (we’ll get back to them later). I was able to travel to Philadelphia to cover the NCAA Championships and witness and report on Penn State winning a national championship in person. I also got to meet some pretty cool people along the way, Daniel Cormier and Shane Sparks. Oh, and I had another year on men’s lacrosse as well, which was a continuing trend. This was also my first time in the comfy chairs and they didn’t disappoint.

For my final fall sport, I had to continue to cover national champions, so I decided to report on the women’s volleyball team. This was also one of my favorite beats. Andrew cubed you could call it. Even though people kept getting taken away from the beat, it was a blast. My second beat was with Andrew Deal and Andrew (PRIME) Mercer. With all the changes that happened throughout the season, this was the first time I really felt myself take over an entire beat. Posting on Twitter consistently and writing the most articles I’ve written in a semester, it was another step in the right direction.

Back on the wrestling beat this year, I had the opportunity to travel to Cleveland to report on Penn State’s fifth straight national championship, and have a HOLLYWOOD moment, which is shocking to say in the city of Cleveland, but overall it was another great experience. This was also my last season with the comfy chairs, which was very sad. I ended my career on the men’s lacrosse beat, marking my fourth year on the beat.

Memories

If you know me, you know my favorite sport is hockey. Big shoutout to Chase Fisher and Adam (UNC) Waxman for watching the moment that Matthew Schaefer became a New York Islander and our six-hour journey to watch the Penguins take on the Flyers. It was a pleasure having these moments outside the office with you.

When I turned 21, it became a weekly tradition, either Brother’s Tuesday or Cafe Wednesday. When it comes to Brothers Tuesday, Alex Osman and Zach Karpf way to put me onto it. Talking some football with our burgers was always an exciting part of my week.

Cafe Wednesday was another electric time of the week that featured Cearra O'Hern, Ritamarie Montagna, Joe “the culture builder” Tuman, and Zach. It was great to debrief about life and whatever was going on that week.

Chipotle Wednesday featured Zach and I. Chipotle is one of my favorite places, and Zach we had some interesting moments and conversations during our trips to Chipotle; it was always a great time and a continued trend.

More shoutouts to Kaitlyn Murphy for reminding me to lock in. I wonder where the LOCK IN wrapper went. Dan Studer (MEGA UNC) I’ve never seen someone in head to toe in New York Yankees gear, also can’t believe you made me sit in the third row in COMM 403. Joey Klein, can’t believe I met you before we went to Penn State, but it was great getting to know you the last couple of years. Also, the guy knows where to get a bagel in case anyone is curious.

Nobody matched the dedication that Joel Haas had to the Daily Collegian, and it was interesting to see it daily. Also, never saw someone as ready for a flag football or dodgeball game than this guy. Avery Hill, the only other bowler I know at Penn State. We still need to hit the lanes at some point, need to get you to a 200. Also, shoutout to Lexie Linderman, Greg Finberg and Emma Naysmith, you’ve all helped me along my journey.

Never forget when I almost joined the photo staff. Kate Hildebrand and Chris Eutsler always made being at the table a great time. I couldn’t transfer from sports staff to photo, though, so I had to take myself out of the portal.

To Evan, you made being on the wrestling beat a blast. The jokes that were made on The Mat Return are some I will never forget, mostly because of Evan. The fact that you were also the boss of me at The Lion 90.7 FM was a little concerning, but we made it work. There was nobody else I would rather have a call with on the Lion, so thanks for the big save there. The endless jokes and laughs are some I will never forget.

To Andrew Deal, you also made wrestling fun, but women’s volleyball was also a great time…when you were on the beat. I’ll never forget when I watched the Chicago Bears game and you were there to witness that reaction. I understand that Shelly is a great car to be in, but Andrew, please be careful what you do around the car. It was great writing alongside you for the last year and a half and remember to enjoy your Mallo Cups.

To Lyle, it’s crazy to think I’d become so close with someone on the Collegian. From the first time we met when we were talking about different types of pizza, to being on the same beats sophomore year, to becoming roommates, it was great to watch you grow as a writer and an editor. Puka Nacua is known because of you. Thank you for the moments outside the office and for being someone I could ask questions to all the time. It's been great being able to call you one of my closest friends at Penn State. Long live the Kegway.

To Nick Puglisi, Ian, Breen, Peter Pilko, and Luke Sheluck, I can’t believe we made it all the way through freshman year to now. We had some crazy times through the years and made quite a few friends along the way.

To Mom, thanks for being my go-to person when I needed anything. It’s greatly appreciated and I wouldn’t be where I am without you. Your guidance from when I was younger to where I am now has helped me become who I am today. Thank you for always having my back and being there whenever I need it. I have grown a lot and have challenged myself because of what you’ve taught me.

To Scott, (my bro) to the endless catches on the front lawn, to seeing you in the stands at my games, thank you for the endless support. I really appreciate it. I think you were the only person more excited than me when I committed to Penn State. Thank you for introducing me to the sport of wrestling; it clearly paid off.

To Dad, thank you for the support and for helping me out throughout my journey. Watching lacrosse games and going to your favorite bar, Brothers, during your visits have been great memories for me. You’ve really helped me get to know the sport, the people involved within it and made sure my friends and I always had a good time when we went out. So, thank you for helping me create memories along my journey.

To all of my grandparents, you all have helped me approach life the right way and have been great people to look up to. I really appreciate it. To the rest of the family (including Cocoa), there are way too many cousins, aunts and uncles to name, but you’ve all helped me a lot along the way.

That’s all folks! That’s the end of my Penn State journey.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Penn State women's basketball earns commit from Vanderbilt transfer Ava Black
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Tanisha Wright continues to add to her squad at Penn State.

The Lady Lions added former Vanderbilt guard Ava Black as the second athlete on the 2026-27 team.

What's up Happy Valley 🦁💙🤍 #committed@coachtwright_ pic.twitter.com/4fpVzoEdy3— Ava Black (@Ava_Jaye_Black) April 20, 2026

Black spent her freshman year with the Commodores, where she averaged 2.8 points and 2.3 rebounds per game. The 5-foot-11 guard shot 52.2% from the floor, including a career-best 12 points against High Point in the first round of the NCAA Tournament.

The Auburn, New Hampshire, native played in 30 of Vanderbilt's 34 games during Year 1, averaging 10 minutes per game.

Black joins incoming freshman Kamrah Banks as the only two players on Penn State's squad.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball faces Bucknell on Dollar Dog Night
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State looks toward a familiar face for its first matchup of the week.

Bucknell comes back to town for the second time this season. The Nittany Lions will look to stay in the win column before heading to Eugene for a three-game series versus Oregon.

Coming off a challenging series against Rutgers, Penn State looks to continue its fight to make it to the Big Ten Tournament.

The Nittany Lions will host the Bison at 5:30 p.m. Tuesday for another Dollar Dog Night at Medlar Field at Lubrano Park.

Previous matchup

The blue and white faced off against the Bison less than a month ago. That game, which also took place at Medlar Field, saw Bucknell win 9-0 in a shutout.

Pitching was the main concern in the last matchup, with Penn State going through seven pitchers. The longest any pitcher went was Ethan Bauerschmidt, who threw 2 1/3 innings. In those, he allowed five runs on only three hits with just one strikeout.

Mason Butash and Skip Shenosky also saw action on the mound. Butash had four strikeouts in just 1 2/3 innings pitched, and Shenosky had five in just two.

Penn State was shut out, only having five hits in the game. All five came from different players. Pitcher Dante Pavone got the win for Bucknell, allowing just one hit with four strikeouts in three innings.

Players to watch

Going into the second matchup of the year, Michael Anderson and Bryce Molinaro are likely to be keys to success. Both players showed in their last outing against Rutgers that they’re threats each time they step to the plate — each hitting a homer and combining for five hits, five runs and seven RBIs.

Another player to be watching is Jack Porter, who’s been a consistent hitter since the Nittany Lions’ game against West Virginia. He also had three runs and two RBIs on Sunday.

For the Bison, Michael Trommer is the name to watch, who leads the team in hits and home runs with nine on the season. Garret Mathias will also be a concern for the blue and white as he leads the team with 34 RBIs on 32 hits.

Pitching-wise, for Bucknell, Everett Garber leads in strikeouts with 47.

Overall, history says Penn State has the better chance at this game, holding an overwhelming 132-32-1 record between the two programs. Even with the shutout victory earlier this season, the Bison are 3-7 in their last 10 against the Nittany Lions.

In Bucknell’s last action, it pitched another shutout, this time against Army, winning 8-0. Coach Mike Gambino and his team will have to channel what they did in their first and third games against Rutgers to find success against this solid Bison squad.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball faces Saint Francis for last nonconference game of regular season
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Before hitting the road again, Penn State returns to Happy Valley and faces Saint Francis on Tuesday in its last nonconference game of the regular season. The Nittany Lions dropped two of three against Ohio State over the weekend, but have an opportunity to gain some positive momentum facing a slumping opponent.

The Red Flash are just 4-36-1 on the campaign and take a five-game losing streak into its matchup against the blue and white. While Penn State started the season hot, conference play has been mixed. The Nittany Lions are in the middle of the Big Ten with a 28-17 record with just two conference series left.

The blue and white’s explosive offense is the main reason it won so many games in the beginning of the season, but its bats have been streaky lately. However, opponents have batted an impressive .404 average and drove in 392 runs against Saint Francis this season, so the matchup is favorable for Penn State’s lineup.

Penn State needs solid production throughout its lineup but its power hitters should have solid at-bats. Junior Brooke Klosowicz is providing another outstanding offensive season for the Nittany Lions. The Johnsburg, Illinois, native holds a .368 average and has knocked a team-best 53 hits with 13 home runs and 48 RBI.

Freshman Allison Oneacre has been one of the best freshmen in the nation. The Alpharetta, Georgia, native possesses an efficient .383 average and has totaled 49 hits. Oneacre leads the squad in home runs (16) and RBI (55).

On the mound, Penn State’s pitching staff ranks seventh in the Big Ten with its 4.27 team-ERA. Junior McKenna Young holds the squad's lowest ERA at 3.35, while totaling 54 strikeouts over 62.2 innings. The Red Flash have struggled in the batter’s box with a team-average of .264 and 275 hits.

The Nittany Lions are favored to shut down Saint Francis’ lineup and they could do so by utilizing one starter instead of rotating through their bullpen arms before this weekend’s series against Illinois.

Sophomore Sofia Zielinski spearheads the Red Flash’s lineup. The Phoenix, Arizona, native possesses a team-best in average (.462), hits (49) and home runs (10). If the blue and white shut down Zielinski, it will be in good shape.

Freshman Zoe Kesterson has pitched the most innings for Saint Francis with 84. The Charles Town, West Virginia, native had a solid outing in her last start against FDU. Kesterson surrendered two unearned runs and seven hits over 5.2 innings.

This is a favorable game for the Nittany Lions and if they can click on all cylinders, it would provide them with some confidence heading into their final stretch of conference play.

First pitch is scheduled at 6 p.m. Tuesday and can be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball outside hitter Sean Harvey earns EIVA weekly honors
author: Liana Tiamzon I For the Collegian
description:
Sean Harvey continues to establish himself as Penn State’s primary offensive weapon in the 2026 season, emerging as one of the most reliable scorers in the EIVA. The redshirt sophomore leads the Nittany Lions by a wide margin in total kills.

Harvey’s dominance has translated into recognition, earning four EIVA Offensive Player of the Week Honors. This recent week of play added the most recent.

𝟰𝘅 𝙀𝙄𝙑𝘼 𝙊𝙛𝙛𝙚𝙣𝙨𝙞𝙫𝙚 𝙋𝙡𝙖𝙮𝙚𝙧 𝙤𝙛 𝙩𝙝𝙚 𝙒𝙚𝙚𝙠🦁 Finished 12-0 EIVA with wins over GMU🦁 Hit .373 w/ 3.80 kills/set🦁 20 kills Friday, 18 kills Saturday#WeAre pic.twitter.com/Ihk7Z9Fc5i— Penn State Men's Volleyball (@PennStateMVBALL) April 20, 2026

The sophomore hit for .373 while averaging 3.80 kills per set, combining efficiently with high volume production to be an anchor for the Nittany Lions’ offense.

author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following the final week of the regular season, Rocquette Allen keeps earning honors.

Allen was named the Big Ten Freshman of the Week for the sixth time this season following her four-goal week.

Another week, another award🤷‍♀️Rocquette is your 𝗦𝗜𝗫-𝗧𝗜𝗠𝗘 Big Ten Freshman of the Week 😤 pic.twitter.com/z9q5H0mme6— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) April 20, 2026

The Lonetree, Colorado, native sits third on the Nittany Lions with 41 goals this season, alongside 22 assists.

Against Northwestern, Allen recorded one goal with two assists, followed by a three-assist game against Johns Hopkins.

Allen recorded her sixth hat trick of the season to close out the season at Rutgers, scoring three goals with another two assists.

title: Former Penn State center Gracie Merkle to finish collegiate career at Minnesota
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After spending two seasons at Penn State, Gracie Merkle will wrap up her collegiate career elsewhere.

Merkle committed to Minnesota on Monday, she announced on social media.

Go Gophers! #committed pic.twitter.com/YqNeXoGZcc— Gracie Merkle (@GracieMerkle) April 20, 2026

The 6-foot-6 center became the tallest player in Penn State history after transferring from Bellarmine for the 2024-25 season.

Merkle experienced back-to-back dominant campaigns in Happy Valley, capping off her career with 19.2 points and 8.2 rebounds per game in 2025-26. In addition to her high scoring, Merkle also led the nation with a 72.5% field goal percentage.

After committing to Maryland before withdrawing and returning to Penn State last season, Merkle will head to Minnesota for her final season after its 24-9 record this year.

title: Report: Penn State men’s basketball forward Tibor Mirtič enters transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A member of Penn State’s frontcourt is set to depart the team.

Forward Tibor Mirtič is entering the transfer portal, according to Sam Kayser of League Ready.

NEWS: Penn State forward Tibor Mirtic is entering the transfer portal, @LeagueRDY has learned.The 6-foot-9 forward out of Slovenia played one season for the Nittany Lions and averaged 3.7PPG and 3.3RPG this season. pic.twitter.com/QwHQQkXjoG— Sam Kayser (@KayserHoops) April 20, 2026

While he’s listed as a freshman on the Nittany Lions’ roster, he was only given two years of eligibility by the NCAA as he’s a 2003-born player with professional playing experience in his home country of Slovenia.

This season, he appeared in 15 games before his year was cut short due to injuries. He first endured a knee injury against Michigan on Jan. 6, and while he was rehabbing that injury, he injured his shoulder during his first practice back.

As he played in 48% of Penn State’s games this season, it appears Mirtič won’t be able to get a medical redshirt as that’s above the NCAA requirement of only 30% participation.

While he was on the court, Mirtič was a rotational player in the frontcourt. He averaged 3.7 points and 3.3 rebounds per game with 53.2% shooting from the field.

title: PSU Reproductive Justice Project aims to educate, support students
author: Alli Satterfield | The Daily Collegian
description:
Content warning: This article contains mention of sexual assault. Resources and support are available through Penn State’s Counseling and Psychological Services.

Founded in 2023, Penn State’s Reproductive Justice Project provides students with educational opportunities, resources and support in an aim to foster discussion about sexual health and support the defined tenets of reproductive justice.

The student-led organization exclusively focuses on the four tenets of reproductive justice: the right to have children, right not to have children, the right to raise families in safe and sustainable communities and the right to bodily and sexual autonomy

Olivia Adams, the organization’s president, said the group aims to keep their initiatives fun and engaging. Their recent reproductive advocacy week, which ran from March 23-27, reflected this.

“We had fun things like sex toy bingo,” Adams, a third-year studying integrative science,said. “But we also try to do educational things, and we did a sex- ed panel where we talked to some of the ‘sexperts’ on campus in R-VOICE and UHS.”

During their weekly meetings, the organization has educational sessions for members, but their main goal is reaching and impacting the general student body. They table and host events on campus to help students learn more about reproductive justice, which Adams said is different than, although equally important to, reproductive rights.

“A reproductive right is anything that goes along with reproductive laws,” Adams said. “So, it’s like the right to abortion, which is also part of reproductive justice, where you have bodily autonomy and the right to not have children.”

Adams said having access to condoms is an example of a reproductive right and clarified that while these rights add to our ability to have reproductive justice, the concept of reproductive justice is more complex and expansive. It includes ensuring individuals have access to safe and sustainable communities, food, proper nutrition, education and child care.

The organization aims to help Penn State continue to become a safe and sustainable community. Adams said Penn State resources like the Lion’s Pantry, CATA bus and UHS are already steps toward achieving reproductive justice. But, she thinks available educational resources could expand.

The Reproductive Justice Project is trying to help with this education, while also trying to reduce the social stigmas around sexual health and speaking out against assault.

One of the club members’ favorite ways to do that is through the sex toy bingo events the organization hosts. Isabella Bautista-Santiago said this event is her favorite because it’s super fun but also super effective in reminding people that sexuality isn’t something to be ashamed of.

“We’re all adults here,” Bautista-Santiago, a third-year studying immunology and infectious disease, said.

She joined the organization this semester and said the close-knit community is something that makes the Reproductive Justice Project so special.

“Everyone’s a super close community,” Bautista-Santiago said.

The organization also provides peer support, and the small size of the club helps their efforts be more effective. Adams said the organization can provide comfort for survivors of sexual violence who want to speak to someone who’s not a mandated reporter.

Penn State does have resources through UHS to help survivors, but these employers are required to report incidents, and Adams understands that not everyone wants to report an incident immediately, which is where the Reproductive Justice Project can come in.

“It’s being able to be there for people in a way that is important to them, handling something from an outsider perspective and being able to support them in a way that they need,” Adams said.

There are plenty of different people to speak to in the Reproductive Justice Project, according to Nevaeh Sickles.

Sickles, a third-year studying premedicine, has been a member of the club since its inception and said everyone in the organization has shared morals in spite of their differing perspectives. She values their emphasis on inclusivity and justice.

Members of the organization said they want survivors to know that they always have support at Penn State.

“They should know it’s not their fault and that they are not alone,” Sickles said. “There are resources to help them whenever they feel comfortable to express what they’ve been through.”

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Column | Predicting the 2026 women’s lacrosse Big Ten Tournament
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
The first regular season has concluded under coach Kayla Treanor, and Penn State is postseason bound.

The Nittany Lions earned the fifth seed in the Big Ten Tournament on Saturday after defeating Rutgers. The blue and white open the tournament on Wednesday, facing No. 4 Michigan at noon.

While the Nittany Lions lost to the Wolverines during the regular season, they had an overall stronger regular season compared to recent years, posting their best regular season record since 2017.

Quarterfinals

No. 4 Michigan vs No. 5 Penn State

Michigan has multiple advantages heading into Wednesday afternoon’s contest, including posting a better conference record and having home-field advantage.

However, playing on the road hasn’t been much of an issue for Penn State, as the Nittany Lions have posted a 7-1 away record this year. Their only loss came against No. 3 Maryland, falling by just one goal.

While the Wolverines are coming off a recent upset victory over the Terrapins, I think Penn State gets the win with the help of its high-scoring offense, with Rocquette Allen, Payton Wainman and Kelly MacKinney each scoring multiple goals.

Penn State 13, Michigan 11

No. 2 Maryland vs No. 7 Ohio State

Speaking of Maryland, it should have no issue when it faces Ohio State at 3 p.m. on Wednesday.

The Terrapins are coming off a 13-9 victory over the Buckeyes on Saturday, as well as a historic moment last Wednesday. Midfielder Kori Edmondson and defender Maddy Sterling were taken in the first-ever WLL college draft, showcasing their talents as one of the top-16 draft-eligible players in the country.

Maryland 14, Ohio State 9

No. 3 Johns Hopkins vs No. 6 Rutgers

Johns Hopkins has had a dominant end of the season, winning three major Big Ten games to earn the No. 3 seed over Michigan.

The Blue Jays will continue that stretch with a victory over Rutgers for four straight wins after defeating Oregon by 12 goals and the No. 6 Wolverines and No. 19 Nittany Lions in overtime.

Johns Hopkins 15, Rutgers 6

Semifinals

No. 1 Northwestern vs No. 5 Penn State

Penn State experienced several strong moments when it faced Northwestern earlier this season, including a 2-0 start and four straight goals to end the game. However, the Wildcats had their own times of success, most notably holding a 7-0 run to pull away and ultimately beat the blue and white.

While Northwestern is coming off a quarterfinals bye, the Nittany Lions will be playing their third game in a week.

That will warrant strong chemistry on an already assist-heavy team, but will ultimately lead to their downfall against the Wildcats.

Northwestern 18, Penn State 16

No. 2 Maryland vs No. 3 Johns Hopkins

While Maryland is the higher-ranked team heading into the semifinals, it’s faced back-to-back losses against inferior-ranked teams.

The Terrapins fell in a one-goal nailbiter to Northwestern on April 9, before being dominated by Michigan, 15-4, last Thursday. The recent hot streak by the Blue Jays will propel them to their first Big Ten championship appearance in school history.

Johns Hopkins 13, Maryland 11

Championship

No. 1 Northwestern vs No. 3 Johns Hopkins

The Wildcats have historically dominated the Big Ten with seven championship appearances since its inception in 2015.

That will show next Sunday as Northwestern performs a repeat of its regular-season matchup, where it won 16-12. While the Blue Jays won’t earn the victory, they’ll keep things a little closer as the Wildcats earn their fourth straight conference title.

Northwestern 13, Johns Hopkins 10

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State football draft profile | RB Nick Singleton enters NFL holding three notable records
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Although he might not hear his name called as early as he once thought, Nick Singleton has the opportunity to shine in the NFL.

Singleton had a down year in 2025 compared to the season prior when he helped lead the Nittany Lions to a College Football Playoff semifinals, but his record-breaking career in the blue and white has the potential to carry him into the early-mid rounds of the 2026 NFL Draft.

Singleton broke his foot during Senior Bowl practice and had to undergo a surgery that kept him off the field for much of the offseason, leading to him missing out on running at Penn State Pro Day and the NFL Scouting Combine.

He was recently cleared to run and released a video of his workouts so NFL teams have the chance to see his film and alleviate any injury concerns.

“Obviously it wasn't the season I wanted, such as for the team too, but it finished off strong,” Singleton said at pro day. “End up winning the (Rutgers) game and leave my legacy here at Penn State. It means a lot for me and the reason I came back, too."

Metrics

Height: 6-foot

Weight: 219 pounds

Bench press: 25 reps

Projection

Projected round drafted: Rounds 3-4

Player comparison: Rachaad White

Scouting report

Singleton offers a balance of size and speed, which allows him to explode from the handoff and break free from multiple points of contact.

Stronger on the outside zone, Singleton was able to put his acceleration to good use, but lacked superior vision and tended to require hand-fed openings to make a big play. That lack of vision saw him consistently running directly into contact instead of evading, eliminating potential for explosive runs.

There’s no denying that Singleton is an athletic freak. He’s incredibly fast and possesses elite acceleration once he breaks through a hole, but he’s also a great pass-catching back with 987 receiving yards across his career.

Upon arriving in Happy Valley, Singleton took the high expectations set for him as a 5-star and ran with them in his freshman season with the Nittany Lions, rushing for 1,061 yards and 12 touchdowns, including a career-long 87-yard rushing touchdown in the 2023 Rose Bowl victory over Utah.

After a slight down year as a sophomore, Singleton had his best season in 2024, with 1,099 rushing yards and 12 touchdowns. He played a key role in Penn State’s journey to the College Football Playoff semifinals with 261 yards and five touchdowns throughout the playoffs.

While Singleton scored a career-high 13 rushing touchdowns in 2025, he only rushed for 549 yards, playing a threatening role in the red-zone while Allen took most of the midfield handoffs. He ended his career as the program’s all-time touchdown leader with 55.

In addition, Singleton’s 5,586 career all-purpose yards are an all-time Penn State record as well. He joined Saquon Barkley as the only players in program history to accumulate more than 5,000 all-purpose yards and 50 touchdowns, and was the first to do so nationally since Jonathon Taylor.

Best team fit: Indianapolis Colts

Taylor is no doubt one of the best running backs in the NFL, but the Indianapolis Colts lack depth. Singleton’s track record of reliability on third down may give coach Shane Steichen flexibility on the ground as well as give Taylor rest for big plays earlier in the drive.

Giving Singleton second-string reps behind Taylor may be the push the Colts need to get over the hump and make the NFL playoffs, as they’d have a pair of record-breaking running backs to utilize when the run game needs to shine.

If drafted by Indianapolis, Singleton would reunite with former Penn State tight end Tyler Warren, as the two played together in Happy Valley from 2022-24.

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: Penn State football draft profile | RB Kaytron Allen brings consistent, tough running
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Kaytron Allen took a handoff from Liam Clifford and stumbled his way forward for a four-yard gain, breaking the Penn State all-time rushing yards mark, and capped it off with a touchdown later on in the drive.

The record capped an amazing year from Allen, who had 1,303 yards in 12 games, good for 108.5 yards per game. As a result of his amazing year — and record-breaking career — Allen is just days away from hearing his name called at the 2026 NFL Draft.

The Norfolk, Virginia, native was one half of one of the best running back duos in the country, alongside Nick Singleton, but Allen took the reins in 2025, earning AP All-America third-team honors as a result.

“I don't think people really respect what I've done here,” Allen said at pro day. “I always made progress and stuff like that since I've been here, progress after progress. But no, I'm different, and you get me, I'm a difference maker to the team I'm ready to play right now.”

Metrics

Height: 5-foot-11

Weight: 218 pounds

Projections

Projected round drafted: Round 4

Player comparison: Tyler Allgeier

Scouting report

Allen isn’t going to blow anyone away with his speed. But what he lacks in speed, he brings in everything else.

The running back has the strength and power to bounce off tackles and his downfield running style had a tendency to give defenses fits during his collegiate career.

Entering the 2025 season, Allen wasn’t seen as a three-down running back, or a back that could play consecutive drives, considering how much he split with Singleton. However, that changed down the stretch, as Allen saw his carry numbers take a rise starting against Iowa, where he ran 28 times.

From there, he never had lower than 19 carries in a game as the unofficial lead running back for the team.

His power and short-yard running was never a concern, but he made himself more versatile. Allen proved he could break off big runs despite his lack of breakaway speed and could put an offense on his back when needed, something that could be a difference maker in the NFL.

Allen ended his Penn State career with 42, 50 and 55-yard runs in the final three games.

Best team fit: Jacksonville Jaguars

The Jacksonville Jaguars are sure to draft a running back at some point in the 2026 NFL Draft. Allen’s run style and familiarity with the area could benefit him, and the Jaguars and make it a perfect fit.

Jacksonville lost Travis Etienne Jr. to free agency as he signed with the New Orleans Saints, leaving a rather big hole in the team’s offense. Allen played his high school football for IMG Academy in Bradenton, Florida, which is just under four hours from Jacksonville.

The Jaguars’ running back room now consists of Bhayshul Tuten and Chris Rodriguez Jr. as the likely ball carriers if nothing else changes.

This gives Allen the opportunity to slot right in and get carries in a Jacksonville offense that saw a lot of success in 2025.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Recruiting roundup | Penn State has a former commit visit, picks up 4 new commitments
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State currently has a top-10 recruiting class, per 247Sports composite, after a big weekend for Matt Campbell and company that included four commitments and the addition of a top-100 cornerback.

Here’s a recap of recruiting from Week 4 of spring practice.

New commits

The Nittany Lions added four pledges to their 2027 class this week, with all four coming across a span of three days. Additionally, three of the commitments hail from the East Coast, a contrast from last week’s Midwest recruiting spree.

Top-100 cornerback Kei’Shjuan Telfair was the biggest pick up for Penn State and is a player that Terry Smith has been recruiting for years — Smith initially offered Telfair in October 2024.

Telfair, an Ohio native, is the lone commit that isn’t from the East Coast and had 43 offers on the board from schools like Miami, Clemson, Ohio State and Indiana. But Telfair’s pledge showed why keeping Smith on staff was crucial for Campbell on the recruiting trail, as now four of Penn State’s 11 commitments are cornerbacks.

Offensive linemen Owen Reilly and Jon Sassic alongside safety Jonathan Galette were the other three pledges. Sassic plays at Pittsburgh Central Classic, a high school that has consistently been a pipeline for Penn State.

2027 cornerback commit Zachary Gleason Jr. also comes from Pittsburgh Central Catholic, while current Nittany Lions Peter Gonzalez and Xxavier Thomas played high school football there as well.

Penn State beat out Wisconsin to land Galette, as the safety visited the Badgers on April 12 after seeing the Nittany Lions a few days prior on April 7. Campbell and safeties coach Deon Broomfield will need to keep their feet on the gas to stave off any push from Wisconsin, as Galette still has an official visit scheduled in Madison on May 29.

Layton Von Brandt visits

Former Penn State commit and 4-star offensive tackle Layton Von Brandt was on campus Saturday as the Nittany Lions continued their efforts to get him back on board.

Von Brandt is ranked as the No. 62 player nationally, No. 6 offensive tackle and No. 1 player in Delaware. If Penn State managed to get him on board, it would be a huge recruiting win for Ryan Clanton.

But it’s going to be tough for the Nittany Lions to win this recruiting battle. Von Brandt’s top eight includes Notre Dame, Ohio State, LSU, Tennessee, Auburn, Virginia Tech and Florida — he already has great relationships with former Penn State coaches James Franklin at Virginia Tech and Phil Trautwein at Florida.

With seemingly six schools still in the mix, it could be a bit until Von Brandt makes his decision, but getting him on campus this spring was huge for Penn State. He doesn’t have any official visits scheduled yet, though, which will be an important indicator of if the Nittany Lions are a serious player for Von Brandt.

Other notable visitors

Priority defensive line target Stanley Montgomery visited Penn State this weekend as the Nittany Lions continue to push for the 4-star Philadelphian. Montgomery has an official visit scheduled for June 5, but will also take trips to Northwestern, Kentucky and Virginia this summer.

Montgomery ranked by 247Sports composite as the No. 7 player in Pennsylvania, which would make him a massive in-state recruiting win for Penn State.

4-star safety Malakai Taufoou was also on campus this weekend, a player Broomfield began recruiting at Iowa State over a year ago. Taufoou is ranked as the No. 35 player from California and following his visit, included the Nittany Lions in his top five alongside Washington, Oregon, Cal and BYU.

Edge rusher Aidan O’Neil, who has the Nittany Lions in his top-seven, took a trip to Penn State this weekend. O’Neil was in Happy Valley on March 29 as well, and the Nittany Lions made the cut for an official visit this summer as they look to land the 4-star from New Jersey.

Looking ahead, Penn State had five players from the Class of 2028 visiting. 4-star cornerback Keaton Fields and 4-star offensive tackle Carter Barrett are the only two visitors currently ranked by 247Sports composite.

title: ‘We work really well together’ | Penn State Army ROTC prepare for Sandhurst Military Skills Competition
author: Kyla Jones I The Daily Collegian
description:
As members of the Penn State Army Reserve Officers’ Training Corps (AROTC) prepare for the Sandhurst Military Skills Competition, early morning and rigorous training days have become the norm.

First introduced in 1967, the Sandhurst Military Skills Competition is a two-day international contest held each spring at the United States Military Academy at West Point. It features 48 cadet AROTC teams and tests physical endurance, military skills, teamwork and leadership.

David Hayes, team captain, said during rainy days he’s motivated by the chance to discover what exists outside his comfort zone.

“You really have to do hard things,” Hayes, a fourth-year studying criminology, said. “It’s really rewarding to understand the result of waking up at 4 a.m. … will never, most of the time, be negative and more than likely positive.”

AROTC has been training since August. In order to be selected for Sandhurst, AROTC has a test week where cadets are assessed on individual physical fitness, teamwork, miscellaneous skills and ruck marches — hiking with a weight on your back.

After observations and peer reviews, cadets are then selected for a team that competes in October’s Brigade Ranger Challenge at Fort Dix, a smaller competition. Based on that performance, cadets are then chosen for Sandhurst, which will take place from May 1 to May 2.

Gia Castagna, one of two women in the group, said she’s been a part of the team since freshman year and is excited to take on a new challenge and make Penn State proud.

“A lot of what we're all doing are hard things together,” Castagna, a third-year student studying cybersecurity, said. “You kind of form that bond, and I'm extremely lucky to be surrounded by, not only a team that pushes each other, but also respects one another. And it's a really positive environment to be in, even though there's not a ton of girls. I feel welcomed and involved.”

Castagna said teambonding has helped her learn a lot about herself and develop confidence.

“You're around people who could support you,” Castagna said. “That helps you kind of develop yourself and your self esteem.”

Nathan Musser is competing for the first time and said he’s been getting butterflies, while hearing upperclassmen’s stories of the competition. As May approaches, he’s been getting more accustomed to the training schedule.

“What we're doing here, it's very unpredictable, so you get to see a lot of raw emotion and character,” Musser, a first-year studying finance, said.

Musser described the competition as the group’s Super Bowl, and said it gives him balance.

“Definitely adds a little bit of pressure, but it's a really cool feeling just to know that you represent something bigger than yourself,” Musser said.

Sergeant Nicholas Piatkowski, head coach of the Ranger Challenge and Sandhurst teams, said he wants the cadets to remember their service goes beyond the team and sport. Cadets all wear black metal wristbands that bear the name of fallen soldiers.

“We wear the bracelets as a way to remember the sacrifice of those who came before us,” Piatkowski said. “That’s what we have to do, let their legacies live on.”

As a coach, he said the most challenging part is being “ in the dark.” He doesn’t know what events will look like until the team actually goes into them.

Piatkowski was a cadet during his time at Penn State. As such, he said he recognizes the importance of the competition and training as representative of the university.

Brienna Blackwood, co-captain, said the group has had a long training period. In order to prevent burnout, they have limited their days and plan to incorporate meal rotation.

“We switched from five days a week (physical training) to four days in-person and one virtual run on Fridays so that people didn't have to wake up too early,” Blackwood, a third-year energy engineering major, said. “We wanted to keep cardio strong, and also start honing in on the technical skills that we would be seeing at Sandhurst.”

Each event is categorized differently, according to Blackwood. After the 48 hour competition, each team is awarded by specific event then as a team — first, second or third place.

“One of the main changes that we've made from last year to this year is really trying to emphasize cross-training so that anyone can exceed in any event,” Blackwood said. “We notice that Sandhurst is very physically demanding.”

Blackwood said she wants everyone on the team to remember how to support one another.

“As a team we work really well together, and that's the one thing that I want to take with us and come away stronger,” Blackwood said.

title: This week in State College
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
With only two weeks left in the spring semester, there are many things in State College left to enjoy.

As classes start to wind down, the weather warms up and finals seasons approaches, here are some events that might interest you.

Monday, April 20

Charleston Lesson and Social Dance

From 7 to 9 p.m., the Swing Dance Club at University Park will be teaching attendees the Charleston dance, a 1920s jazz club staple in the State College Municipal Building.

Attendees are not required to come with a partner and no experience is needed. From 7 to 8 p.m. there will be dance lessons, and from 8 to 9 p.m. there will be a dance party.

Registration for this event is required. It is recommended to join the GroupMe for updates.

Tuesday, April 21

Intro to: Sowing and Growing

At Headhouse 1 classroom from 5 to 6 p.m., the Sustainable Food Systems Program will be hosting an active Sow and Grow workshop to teach students the essential for planting and caring for seeds.

People who attend the workshop will be able to take home their own seeds to grow. Attendees should register here.

Wednesday, April 22

The 2026 Undergraduate Exhibition

From 10:30 a.m. to 2:00 p.m., in the Alumni Hall in the HUB-Robeson Center undergraduate students across Penn State campuses will be showcasing their research and creative work.

There will be traditional research presentations, five minute oral presentations highlighting original pieces of visual art, five minute performances in the area of music, writing, poetry, dance and theater.

Yoga and Meditation

From noon to 1 p.m., at The Palmer Museum of Art there will be a yoga and mediation lesson.

Students and faculty can join a refreshing yoga and meditation practice, with all sessions led by certified instructors. Everyone is welcome to join, regardless of experience level.

Supplies are limited, so it’s recommended to bring your own. Registration for this session is not required.

2026 Men Against Violence Walk

From 5 to 7:30 p.m., on the Old Main Steps the R-VOICE: Relationship Violence Outreach, Intervention, and Community Education Center and Interfraternity Council at University Park will be holding its annual Men Against Violence Walk.

The walk unites men and allies to speak out against all violence, showing their dedication to sexual violence prevention and awareness. Participants are encouraged to arrive at 5 p.m. to make signs. At 5:30 p.m., there will be a few words followed by a mile-long walk around campus.

Following the walk, there will be free food. Registration for the walk is required.

Thursday, April 23

Build Your Career Skills

At the Bank of America Career Services Building from 11 a.m. to noon, the center will be hosting a workshop to help students prepare for interviews for both internships and jobs.

Registration for this workshop is not required.

Plan Your Promo with Peer Success Coaches

From 5 to 7 p.m., in Westgate Room E125 the Chaikin Center for Student Success will be hosting a workshop led by peer success coaches to teach students the Pomodoro Study Technique. During the workshop students will be able to learn techniques to help fight off procrastination as finals approach.

Free food will be provided.

Friday, April 24

A Boogie Wit Da Hoodie

At 8 p.m., A Boogie Wit Da Hoodie will be making a stop at the Bryce Jordan Center (BJC). Doors for the concert will open at 7 p.m.

Tickets can be found on Ticketmaster starting at $44.

Saturday, April 25

Penn State Football Blue-White Practice

At 1 p.m. at West Shore Home Field at Beaver Stadium the Penn State football team will hold their spring practice with an open-practice format of the 2026-27 team. There is no fee to watch the spring practice.

Seating will be available for fans in the north, south, and east lower bowls. Before the event there will be The Beaver Stadium Block Party on Curtain Road from 9 a.m. to 1 p.m., with free food, live music and face painting.

title: ‘It takes courage’ | First Gen Advocates support ‘trailblazers’
author: Kelsey Bonifer I For the Collegian
description:
First-Gen Advocates (FGA) is a student-run club that works to assist first-generation college students in navigating what can be a strange, overwhelming and occasionally lonely experience.

Through weekly meetings, events for the entire campus and community building, the group has developed into a place where students may give and receive advice.

Domenic Contrino said the group brings a sense of community for him and other fellow first-generation college students, as they are all "trailblazers" for their families.

“There’s a lot of pressure and uncertainty that comes with that,” Contrino, a graduate student studying environmental engineering, said. “Your family supports you, but they don’t always know what you’re going through.”

He said FGA has filled the gap between comprehension and support.

The club organizes a variety of events all year long, with the goal of meeting other first-generation students. In November, the group hosted a First-Generation College Celebration in the HUB-Robeson Center, which featured a week of programs and workshops.

Other events are usually intimate and smaller, including study sessions, Halloween pumpkin painting or informal get-togethers where students can unwind.

“FGA gives people a place to be heard,” Contrino said. “To share their experiences and realize that what they’re going through is normal.”

Delany Moreno has been a part of FGA since her freshman year when she found the club at the involvement fair. She said not only did the helpful resources offered by the group stand out, but also the overall atmosphere.

“It was welcoming right away,” Moreno, a fourth-year studying criminology, said. “I met people who understood what I was going through.”

FGA President Milan Dukes sees the group as more than just a club, as it’s been a support system that’s helped shape her college years.

As the second person in her family to go to college, Dukes remembers feeling unsure at times, especially when things still seemed new, but she’s grateful for what she learned from these scary experiences.

“You don’t always know where to go or what to do,” Dukes, a fourth-year studying psychology, said. “But being in this group, you realize you’re not alone. I hope other people feel the same way.”

Since becoming the group’s president, Dukes’ weeks are now filled with planning meetings, talking to members and finding ways to make the club more beneficial. Her only goal is simple: progress.

“You never know if you’re doing enough,” she said. “But if people believe in what you’re building, that's what matters.”

The idea goes beyond the executive board, as FGA members said confidence can be seen in each other, from those who take on leadership roles to others who get the nerve to ask questions they didn’t know how to ask before.

“Use your voice,” Dukes said. “That’s something we really try to emphasize.”

Donna Hercules said her confidence comes from the community in FGA and a new perspective on college. At Penn State, she had a hard time finding her place at first, but through her experience with the group, Hercules has felt more comfortable on campus.

“I didn’t realize how many resources were out there and how many people were in the same situation as me,” Hercules, a fourth-year studying biobehavioral health, said.

Now, Hercules helps plan events for the club, such as the upcoming spring Carnival on Wednesday from noon to 4p.m. on the HUB lawn, as well as workshops for internships and job search strategies. She said these opportunities are especially important for first-gen students who are entering the professional world for the first time.

According to Contrino, many first-gen students face problems that extend beyond school, like figuring out how to get financial help or how to do well in job interviews without a family member to guide them.

“You can’t always go to your parents for advice on things like that,” Contrino said. “But that also builds a kind of resilience.”

He also said that these struggles aren’t the only thing that defines first-gen students.

“We’re no different from anyone else,” Contrino said. “We’re all dealing with pressures, but there’s pride in being first-gen. It takes courage.”

FGA tries to build a sense of pride, not just among its own members, but all over campus. The group is trying to change how people think about first-generation students by holding events, sharing stories and making spaces where they can be seen.

“Unity comes from understanding that we are different but not drastically different,” Hercules said.

title: Penn State baseball generates powerful team-wide offense in series finale against Rutgers
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
The blue and white found a way to stir up an offense in the final meetup of their three-game series with Rutgers, winning 14-13, despite a late-game comeback from the Scarlet Knights.

Penn State has struggled this season to get an early lead and had fallen behind 2-0 in the series against Rutgers before this game.

Within the second inning, the blue and white were already on the scoreboard, with junior Spencer Barnett’s double to centerfield starting the run. While Barnett didn’t score, his hit was the start of a productive inning.

Jack Porter and Kevin Karstetter got on base following the hit, and Preston Yaucher’s single to left field sent them both home to get an early lead of 2-0.

The hitting continued in the third inning, with a Bryce Molinaro double and Jack Porter's home run. This home run added to a steady offense led by slugger Michael Anderson and Porter for the weekend.

It was his second home run of the series. Additionally, he got a hit on Saturday and a total of four RBIs on Friday.

“Big Mike is doing Big Mike things, and Moli’s been great, but to create that length behind those guys and give those guys some protection,” coach Mike Gambino said. “You got Porter-Barnett in that group right there, which is tremendously important.”

The fourth inning saw great capitalization on the blue and white’s behalf, scoring five runs on five hits. Three of those runs were scored off three back-to-back home runs, by Anderson, Molinaro and Barnett.

Anderson’s homerun brought in a total of three runs, sending Yaucher and Davis home, after they each singled to center field.

Molinaro made history with his additions to the Nittany Lions’ forward drive, putting up two home runs and four RBI’s in his four at-bats.

These two home runs added to his 11 on the season and made this season with the blue and white his third with 10+ home runs. He is one of two Nittany Lions in program history to do so.

“As you ebb and flow, you want to keep those valleys not as low and not as long,” Gambino said, “and that’s a really good job by him coming out of it.”

While Penn State, at one point, held a 12-run lead, Rutgers quickly climbed back into the game in the seventh inning. The Scarlet Knights scored seven runs at the top of the seventh, and these were left unanswered by the blue and white. This run by Rutgers closed the run margin to five in the eighth inning.

“The weird thing about the 10-run rule is that not ending a game on a 10-run rule can feel almost like a mid-game loss,” Gambino said. “We tend to react more than we respond, and we react negatively.”

In the eighth, Rutgers continued their success at the plate, scoring two more runs to close the Nittany Lion lead to two runs.

The Scarlet Knights added one more run to their tally in the ninth inning, but couldn't extend their scoring past thirteen runs, allowing the Nittany Lions to escape with a win to close out the series.

“We hate where we are, but we are still in the playoff hunt,” Gambino said.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's volleyball earns commitment from 2027 outside hitter Kate Kalczynski
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Coach Katie Schumacher-Cawley has landed another 2027 recruit.

After initially committing to Ohio State, outside hitter Kate Kalczynski has officially committed to Penn State.

The Troy, Michigan, native played at Legacy Volleyball Club as an outside hitter, despite standing at just 6-foot.

Kalzynski averaged a .411 kill percentage throughout her three years on varsity at Mercy High School.

Along with that, she averaged a hit percentage of .269 and a total of 517 points and 117 aces throughout her career.

She has 971 kills, 521 digs and 63 total blocks through her 241 sets played through three seasons.

Kalczynski joins a strong 2027 class, including Olivia Henry, Taylor Harrington, Nejari Crooks and Molly Robertson.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball suffers run-rule defeat in series finale against Ohio State
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a convincing 15-7 win over Ohio State in game two of the series, Penn State faced the Buckeyes in the rubber match on Sunday afternoon. Unlike Saturday’s game, the Buckeyes poured it on early while the Nittany Lions struggled in the batter’s box.

Penn State fell in the series finale as Ohio State routed the Nittany Lions 9-0 behind stellar pitching and hot bats.

Right-hander Lorin Boutte delivered a solid start on the mound for Ohio State. Third baseman Allison Oneacre smashed a one-out double to center, but the blue and white left her stranded in scoring position in the opening frame.

However, the scoreless tie wouldn’t last long as the Buckeyes jumped on right-hander Abigail Britton early and plated two runs in the bottom of the first. Hadley Parisien drew a leadoff walk and cleanup hitter Sami Bewick came through with two outs. The junior knocked a double to right center and put Ohio State up 1-0.

Kiki Estrada continued the two-out rally with an RBI double of her own down the right field line to extend the Buckeyes’ early lead 2-0. Britton escaped the inning without any further damage, but Penn State went down quietly in its half of the second.

Ohio State continued putting the game out of reach later in the inning. Lottie Landmesser rocketed a solo home run to left field and made the Buckeyes' lead 3-0. Izzy Neal followed with a single to right field, which led to coach Clarisa Crowell turning to left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris.

However, the freshman couldn’t stop the bleeding. Parisien powered a one-out triple to right-center and plated Neal. A few batters later, Taylor Cruse launched a two-run home run to left center and ballooned the Buckeyes'lead to 6-0.

Boutte successfully retired the blue and white again in the third and Ohio State blew the game open even more. For the second straight inning, the Buckeyes led off with a solo homer. Estrada lifted her solo dinger to right center and added another run to the lopsided lead, 7-0.

Reese Cullen then shot a single to left field and Landmesser showed off her power once again. She blasted her two-run home run to center and the Nittany Lions found themselves in a 9-0 hole.

Right-hander McKenna Young successfully retired the Buckeyes in order in the fourth, but the contest was already well out of reach.

Ultimately, Penn State couldn't break Boutte on the mound as the squad was held scoreless all five innings. The sophomore surrendered just two hits and two walks while she struck out four batters.

Up Next

Penn State returns home for a nonconference matchup against Saint Francis at 6 p.m. on Tuesday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball closes out series with high-scoring victory over Rutgers
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
The sun was shining down on Medlar Field at Lubrano Park today, after Penn State managed to just get by and clinch the win against the Scarlet Knights, and leave the series on a high note.

The blue and white won 14-13, after a huge comeback by Rutgers in the seventh inning nearly led to a sweep in the series.

After a scoreless first inning, the Nittany Lions made a statement in the bottom of the second. It began with a double from Spencer Barnett, before a walk and a single for Jack Porter and Kevin Karstetter, respectively, left the bases loaded.

With two outs on the board, Preston Yaucher’s single into left field sent in both Karstetter and Porter to put the score up 2-0 in favor of the blue and white.

Freshman pitcher Isaiah Shayter started out the game for Penn State, keeping the Scarlet Knights scoreless through the third, giving the blue and white a chance to close out this series on a high note. Penn State’s top power hitter, Michael Anderson, shot a ball down the left field line for a double before third baseman Bryce Molinaro doubled into right center field, expanding Penn State’s lead to 3-0.

Porter was next up in the lineup of power hits for the Penn State offense, launching a two-run homer into left field, to put the score at 5-0 with only one out on the board. McDonald, working overtime, was able to steal both second and third base off throwing errors by the Rutgers defense, before a strikeout from DeMucci closed out the third.

The Scarlet Knights put themselves in a better position after a two-run homer by Trey Wells put Rutgers on the board, 5-2, after another nearly scoreless inning, with two outs already recorded.

With its bats on fire, the blue and white dominated in the bottom of the fourth, hitting three straight home runs. Anderson started the streak, launching his 16th home run of the season into center field before Molinaro and Barnett followed with homers into center and left field, leaving with a 10-2 score and no outs on the board.

After a fantastic run by Shayter, only letting in two runs during his four-inning pitching stretch, Dimond Loosli came in for relief, once again keeping Rutgers off the board and turning the game back over to Penn State. Molinaro, coming in hot for the Nittany Lions, hit his second home run of the game, this time a two-run homer, bringing in Davis along with himself. This put the team in range for a mercy-rule victory, leading by 10 in the bottom of the fifth.

With two outs on the board, Penn State continued to stay alive, as Barnett doubled up the middle before scoring yet another run after a launched ball into left field by Porter missed the glove of a sun-blinded Rutgers outfielder. Porter made his way around the bases after a single into right field by McDonald. Penn State closed out the fifth with a 12-run lead.

Another scoreless inning past for Rutgers, as Mason Butash came in to relieve Loosli, marking the third pitching change for Penn State, compared to Rutgers’ seven.

Rutgers more than managed to get things going in the seventh inning, dominating to come back and close the gap by five, just in time to avoid a mercy-rule loss.

The inning closely resembled Penn State’s close attempt at a comeback in the first game of the series, as Rutgers put seven runs on the board. Sweeney, a star hitter for Rutgers, hit a single into left field with the bases loaded, allowing Chase Krewson to score, before Julius Rosado slammed the ball into right center field for Sweeney, Tristan Salinas and Ryan Jaros to put up three more runs.

Another pitching change saw senior Ben DeMell take the mound in an attempt to put an end to the inning, but he came up short as Yomar Carreras grounded out to Yaucher for Rosado to score, giving Rutgers its fifth run. Trey Wills showed no mercy, tripling down the right-field line, leaving Penn State scrambling for the ball as Matt Chatelle and Gabrielle Rivera sprinted home.

Penn State failed to respond before another nightmare turnover began. Harrison Lollin came up to the mound but was immediately met with a homerun by Jaros, before two more runs were scored, putting the game only at a two-run toss up, 14-12.

A glimpse of hope in the bottom of the eighth for Penn State to get away from Rutgers was quickly crushed after a double play took both Anderson and Davis off the field with the bases loaded, putting an end to the eighth inning.

The top of the ninth saw everyone holding their breath as Rutgers once again started out the inning with a home run, leaving only a one-run gap, but the Nittany Lions managed to skate by with a win, officially putting an end to their five-game losing streak.

Up Next

Penn State will remain home to host Bucknell at 5:30 p.m. Tuesday for another Dollar Dog Night.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE
