Goaltender Arsenii Sergeev leading the stretch after morning skate. Looks like he’ll make his NHL debut tonight, getting the start in Game #82. pic.twitter.com/Dm6n0UNjf9— Brendan Parker (@BParkerTV) April 16, 2026

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s basketball transfer Kayden Mingo commits to Baylor
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
The highest-rated recruit in Penn State history has found his next school.

After just one season with the Nittany Lions, guard Kayden Mingo has committed to transfer to Baylor, per DraftExpress.

NEWS: Penn State transfer Kayden Mingo has committed to Baylor, Excel Sports tells DraftExpress.Mingo started every game in the Big Ten last season, averaging 13.7 points, 4.3 assists and 2.1 steals per game.Scott Drew reloading in the backcourt. pic.twitter.com/yx9BAlu6mI— Jonathan Givony (@DraftExpress) April 16, 2026

Mingo also reportedly visited Oklahoma during his portal recruitment. He has three years of eligibility remaining.

The Farmingdale, New York, native averaged 13.7 points, 3.5 rebounds, 4.3 assists and 2.1 steals per game across 28 appearances as a freshman in 2025-26. Despite alluding to a return during a postgame press conference after the Big Ten Tournament loss to Northwestern, Mingo entered the transfer portal on April 8.

Mingo was a 247Sports composite 4-star and was the crown jewel of Penn State’s 2025 recruiting class.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey forward JJ Wiebusch transfers to Wisconsin
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
JJ Wiebusch is returning home.

The former Penn State winger has transferred to Wisconsin after two seasons in Happy Valley, per Brad Elliott Schlossman of the Grand Forks Herald.

Updating the board with J.J. Wiebusch to Wisconsin: https://t.co/CT4S5Ps7ry— Brad Elliott Schlossman (@SchlossmanGF) April 16, 2026

The Verona, Wisconsin, native tallied 77 points in 69 games with the Nittany Lions, including a 36-point sophomore campaign. Wiebusch was named to the Allentown Regional All-Tournament Team during the team's 2025 Frozen Four run.

The offensive-minded forward transfers within the Big Ten as he heads to the Badgers, who recently suffered a 2-1 loss to Denver in the Frozen Four final.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball looks to clinch EIVA regular season title in series against George Mason
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 15 Penn State looks to close out the regular season with a few more wins ahead of its last two-game home series. And the results of this weekend will determine the fate of the Nittany Lions ahead of the postseason conference tournament.

The blue and white gear up to play the in-conference George Mason squad at 6 p.m. Friday and 3 p.m. Saturday from Rec Hall, with Saturday’s match being Senior Day.

At 10-0 in conference play this year, the Nittany Lions will look to finish the regular season with an undefeated record in the EIVA.

If the Nittany Lions take at least one win against George Mason, they will clinch the EIVA regular-season title ahead of the EIVA Tournament. This will also give them hosting rights for the tournament.

However, it’s also a high-stakes game for George Mason. Currently second in the EIVA, two wins this weekend would allow the Patriots to clinch that same title the blue and white are gunning for.

But it won’t be an easy feat.

George Mason enters the contest at 8-2 in conference play. At 20-7 on the year, while also on a 10-game winning streak, the Patriots will surely give the blue and white a run for its money.

Owen Rose has been a massive piece of the blue and white’s defense.

Monday, the middle blocker earned the EIVA Defensive Player of the Week title for the third time this season, marking his seventh time earning the honor in his career.

He currently ranks No. 1 in not only the EIVA, but also the NCAA in blocks per set. The redshirt senior has 129 blocks on the year, with an average of 1.48 blocks per set.

But where there’s Rose for the Nittany Lions, there’s Alexander Lillie for the Patriots.

The middle blocker currently leads the EIVA in hitting percentage at .391.

Lillie has 86 blocks and 141 kills on the year. He poses a major threat to the blue and white, both offensively and defensively.

Aidan Weltin also brings a set of offensive skills to the match. The sophomore currently ranks No. 1 in the EIVA in kills per set. He totals 327 kills on the year and averages 3.94 kills per set.

But not too far behind Weltin is Penn State’s Sean Harvey.

Harvey has been a force to be reckoned with this season. He currently ranks No. 2 in hitting percentage and No. 3 in kills per set in the EIVA.

The redshirt sophomore has earned the EIVA Offensive Player of the Week title three times this season. He currently tallies 301 kills and an average of 3.54 kills per set.

However, Georgi Zahariev of George Mason earned the EIVA Offensive Player of the Week title heading into this match up. This was his second time receiving the honor this season.

Over the weekend, Zahariev marked 2,000 career assists while also totaling 11 digs, nine kills and 14.5 points.

He currently ranks No. 1 in assists per set in the EIVA with an average of 10.03 per set.

With talented rosters on both ends of the court, and both teams looking to clinch the EIVA regular season title, the in-conference matchup will be the perfect close to the schedule for the two teams.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State football draft profile | C Nick Dawkins brings elite leadership, consistency
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Nick Dawkins comes from a lineage of athletes in his family, with him having the chance to become the third football player in his family to make the NFL.

Dawkins is the cousin of NFL Hall of Famer Brian Dawkins and current Buffalo Bills offensive lineman Dion Dawkins. His father, Darryl Dawkins, was a 14-year NBA player and his other cousin, Karl-Anthony Towns is the current starting center with the New York Knicks.

At Penn State, Dawkins was a player who stayed true to the Nittany Lions and waited three years before getting the starting role in 2024.

Dawkins entered Penn State as a long-term development player, coming in as a 3-star recruit and the No. 7 player in Pennsylvania in the 2020 class. In his freshman season, he didn’t see any playing time but was named the Developmental Squad Offensive Player of the Year.

That honor helped him see the field consistently in two of his next three seasons before becoming the starter. Dawkins started and played in every game besides the Pinstripe Bowl from 2024-25 despite finishing his final campaign with some lingering injuries.

“I really wish I was able to show more athleticism,” Dawkins said at pro day. “Like some lingering issues and injuries that I wasn't able to finish out a lot of the change of direction stuff.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-4

Weight: 305 pounds

40-yard dash: 5.16 seconds

Vertical jump: 32 inches

Broad jump: 9-foot-4 inches

Projections

Projected round drafted: Undrafted

Player comparison: Javon Patterson

Scouting report

Dawkins played a key role in the success of the running game over the past two seasons as the center of the trenches.

His tape will showcase a lot of run plays, but Dawkins held his own in pass protection. However, he was often overshadowed by other linemen at Penn State, including expected first-round pick offensive guard Vega Ioane and the initial likely center predecessor, Cooper Cousins.

But Dawkins made the most of his opportunity. The familiarity with Drew Allar was key to some long inside runs for likely draftees Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen.

Dawkins’ leadership was also a major key to his role on the team, commanding not only the offense with Allar, but also the team as a two-year captain.

Dawkins, who’s sometimes known as the “mayor of State College,” has also had a big impact on the Happy Valley community. He was named the 2024 Allstate Wuerffel Trophy recipient and Allstate Good Works Team Captain after founding the Dawkins Family Foundation to champion academic and athletic excellence for young students.

As a center, despite entering as a rookie, his leadership and goodwill could play a big factor to his potential success on an NFL roster.

Best team fit: Philadelphia Eagles

Dawkins wouldn’t hold a starting job with the Philadelphia Eagles, but could play a key role as a backup offensive lineman.

The Allentown, Pennsylvania, native would be able to grow as a lineman behind one of the consistently strong offensive lines in the NFL. Landon Dickerson, Cam Jurgens and Tyler Steen have the interior of the line locked down, but the depth behind the inside is very thin.

The Eagles don’t currently have a backup guard, and if Dawkins wants a chance at the NFL, he could show his versatility as a potential guard. The longtime Nittany Lion had a visit with the Eagles on Tuesday and could remain in state for the beginning of his professional career.

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: Preview | No. 14 Penn State men’s lacrosse set to close regular season against Rutgers at SHI Stadium
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 14 Penn State has had an up-and-down season, failing to meet the high expectations placed on the team from its previous Final Four berth at Gillette Stadium last year. Currently .500 in Big Ten play, with a recent one-goal defeat to an unranked Michigan.

The Nittany Lions are set to hit the road for their regular-season finale, facing Rutgers in Piscataway, New Jersey, for an 8 p.m. meeting under the SHI Stadium lights on Friday.

Meanwhile, Rutgers entered the 2026 season with similarly high hopes, ranking at No. 18 in the preseason USILA poll. However, the Scarlet Knights have fluctuated among the upper top-20 throughout their schedule, winning only a single ranked match-up, a double-overtime thriller over then-No. 11 Johns Hopkins.

For Penn State, this is a must-win game to secure a first-round home game at Panzer Stadium. For Rutgers, the floor of the standings may be nigh-unavoidable, but a ranking is within sight as the Scarlet Knights tallied 17 votes in the most recent USILA poll, the most among unranked teams.

Tale of the tape

Penn State enters SHI Stadium with a 2-2 record in the Big Ten, having beaten No. 7 Maryland, 10-6, and No. 9 Ohio State, 13-6. But the Nittany Lions fell to No. 12 Johns Hopkins and Michigan in back-to-back games that went down to the wire.

The blue and white have struggled all season long with unranked foes, dropping a pair of games to Villanova and Navy early in the season—two teams that failed to receive any votes in the recent poll.

Despite the upset losses, Penn State performed a pair of upsets outside of the Big Ten. Just a week after losing to the Wildcats in overtime, the Nittany Lions cruised past No. 2 Princeton on the road, winning 13-7. Later, the blue and white dominated No. 6 Cornell with a convincing 19-7 victory.

Rutgers, on the other hand, has seen the complete opposite results, winning all but one of its games against unranked opponents. The one defeat was to Michigan, where the Scarlet Knights fell 13-10. They currently post an 8-5 overall record.

In the Big Ten, Rutgers is at the bottom with a 1-3 conference record, beating the Blue Jays in double overtime, whom the Nittany Lions lost to, 11-10.

Both teams are looking to dig themselves out of two-game losing streaks. While Penn State’s track record against ranked opponents paints the picture of an elite squad, Rutgers’ status as an unranked underdog may prove crucial if the Nittany Lions enter with the wrong mindset, a sentiment that coach Jeff Tambroni stressed postgame on Saturday afternoon.

Players to watch

Colin Kurdyla leads the Scarlet Knights with 28 goals, 24 assists and consequently, 52 total points, far and away the biggest contributor on offense. He tallied a career-high 11 points on four goals and seven assists against St. John's on March 10, spearheading a 19-4 victory.

On defense, Conor Udovich — a Media, Pennsylvania, native — leads Rutgers’ defensive efforts, with a team-high 22 caused turnovers. At 6-foot-3, Udovich stands tall in front of goaltender Cardin Stoller, who posts a .542 save percentage and 9.68 goals-against average through 13 games.

For Penn State, Hunter Aquino returned to action against Michigan and quickly resumed offensive production, scoring a pair of goals in limited recovery minutes. Freshman Jack Iannantuono currently leads the Nittany Lions with 29 points on 15 goals and 14 assists. Kyle Lehman trails closely behind with 28 points, tallying a team-high 18 goals coupled with 10 assists.

Alex Ross, recently selected by the New York Atlas as the eighth pick in the 2026 PLL College Draft, is Penn State’s top pole. The captain tallied 18 caused turnovers and 25 ground ball pickups so far this season. Ross also has a goal and an assist on the year, presenting a dual-threat on the fast break.

Freshman Preston Hawkins guards the pipes for the Nittany Lions, holding a 5-5 record in his true-freshman season with a .497 save percentage and 9.26 goals-against average.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Senior Column | Looking back on my long journey
author: Samantha Lee | The Daily Collegian
description:
Life is all about the journey. And for a girl from the opposite side of the planet Earth, you’ll never know what you are stepping into before even noticing it.

My journey began in Seoul, South Korea, where I was born and raised, and continued living until I came to the United States as a timid 15-year-old. Growing up, I knew I wanted to step outside of my comfort zone and explore what was waiting for me. The biggest influence on my decision to come to the United States to study was my sister, who also attended high school and college there. She came to this country when she was about my age, and even though she went back after completing her studies, her journey still inspired me to step out of my comfort zone.

As a 15-year-old, coming to a different country without my parents was still a tough decision. However, when I saw that my sister was enjoying her time as an international student, I decided to give this journey a try.

My first month at a new high school was really hard. I was a new girl from Korea, and no one knew who I was. I didn’t really have many friends, and I barely knew anyone I could talk to. However, as the month passed by, I slowly started making new friends. One thing I learned was that it is faster to connect with someone who shares my interests.

During my freshman year of high school, I took choir and theatre classes and made many friends through them. I was more on the artistic side and wanted to join a club where I could sing, so I chose choir and theatre. A few months later, I went on a school trip, where I even got to know more people, and it was the first time I talked to more than 10 people outside my circle.

Since then, my life at a new school has gotten a lot easier. I made more friends and even took the courage to start a conversation with them. One thing I realized was that they genuinely wanted to get to know me, and they were excited when I started having conversations with them.

Even then, the feeling of homesickness never went away. Luckily, I was able to go home every summer and winter break and spend time with people that I know. However, that changed when the pandemic hit.

During the pandemic, I was feeling uncertain. When the school closed for the remainder of the spring semester of my sophomore year, I had no choice but to go home. I took all the classes online while quarantined, and I missed all of my friends terribly. I was living through the craziness of COVID-19 like no other and patiently waited for school to resume.

Luckily enough, my school decided to move back to in-person classes with social distancing. I was overjoyed to be able to go back to Virginia and see all my friends. However, one thing has changed. I wasn’t able to go back to Korea twice a year, as I always did before, because of Korea's rigorous quarantine rules. I felt disheartened that I couldn’t see my family as usual and spend Christmas with them. However, in the midst of everything, I never felt alone. I realized that I had friends and teachers who always had my back.

Fast forward to my senior year of high school, I never thought I would be taking the next step in my life, which led to some amazing memories over the next four years. As I was making decisions for college, Penn State was one of my top choices. As a girl who spent four years in a small town in Virginia, I wanted to venture outside my comfort zone again, just as I did when I decided to come to the United States as a ninth grader.

What drew me to Penn State were the various opportunities the school offered me. I was able not only to explore my academic options but also enjoy various extracurricular activities..

When I first stepped foot on Penn State’s grand campus, I was mind-blown. I was surprised to learn there are over 900 student organizations at Penn State to choose from. There, I always knew I wanted to continue my passion for singing. I auditioned for Oriana Singers, a treble chorus at Penn State, and Blue in the Face, an a cappella group, which I can proudly say became my college family.

Throughout my time at Blue in the Face, I had many tremendous opportunities to perform in unexpected places, such as singing the national anthem during sporting events, performing at THON as a group and even singing at the new student convocation in 2023. Whenever I sing with this wonderful group of people, all my worries and stress go away, and I get to have a lot of fun during every rehearsal and performance.

I initially came in as a student in the division of undergraduate studies so I could take classes across a myriad of departments and discover my true passion. Ultimately, I chose to major in advertising/public relations with a focus on public relations and minor in photography.

There, I was able to be creative and use visuals to tell stories to others. I don’t know what led me to choose these two disciplines, but I do know that my passion for storytelling and social media was sparked the moment I started taking classes in advertising. Then, I actually wanted to put all the skills I learned in classes into practice.

During my junior year, I served as a social media manager for Oriana Singers, and I was extremely grateful to have been chosen for this role. I always knew I wanted to come up with fresh ideas for Oriana Singers’ Instagram and raise awareness of the choir. I’ve been in Oriana Singers ever since the beginning of my freshman year, so serving as a social media manager was a way for me to give back to the choir.

Even after my time as a social media manager ended, I always wanted to expand my opportunities to work on social media and gain various experiences on campus.

I joined the Daily Collegian during my senior year with a passion for storytelling using visuals and social media. Even though it was a bit late, I never thought of it as late. For me, the Daily Collegian was just a beginning. I initially joined as a social media candidate, but I wanted to expand my passion for news, so I joined the photography staff in my final semester.

Coming in as a senior was rough, as I had to manage academics, extracurricular activities and now being in Collegian. However, all of the editors that I had, Kippi, Izzy, Geraldine, Matt and Sammy, helped a lot, so I was able to grow as a member of The Daily Collegian’s staff.

I am extremely thankful for the Collegian for allowing me to have unforgettable experiences, and trusting me so I can hone my skills in social media using analytical tools, and even getting to use a new software, Meta Business Suite, so I can prepare myself for the next step of my life, going into the workforce.

Even though my time at Penn State is coming to an end, I believe it’s still the beginning of my next journey.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Senior Column | Check the doc
author: Emma Lessick | The Daily Collegian
description:
If I’m going to be completely honest, when I stepped onto the HUB-Robeson Center’s lawn during the involvement fair as a freshman, I had no idea what The Daily Collegian was. I didn’t know that it was a news organization or a school paper. I didn’t know what opportunities it could offer me, or the person it would help me become.

And I certainly couldn’t have imagined just how much joy, purpose and how many amazing people it would bring into my life over the last few years.

In the midst of all the uncertainty that came with freshman year, there was one thing I held onto — Teresa. My closest and best friend, my roommate, my sister from another mister and now my editor too.

Trusting her felt easier than facing anything alone. So when she joined the Collegian, so did I.

Fast forward three years.

Ugh. Now, if I’m still going to be completely honest here, I never really wanted to write a senior column. I didn’t feel like I had much worth saying. But Teresa insisted.

So here I am. Writing my senior column.

I have spent my college years on the photo staff, capturing moments from a variety of lifestyle events, news and sports. Don’t get me wrong, I had already been doing some photography before college — it wasn’t my first time picking up a camera, but it was more for the fun of it.

Back then, it was just something I enjoyed without thinking too much about where it could take me.

The Collegian changed that.

It pushed me, challenged me and ultimately took my photography further than I ever thought possible. It opened doors I didn’t even know existed and gave me opportunities I never imagined for myself.

And for that, I will always be grateful.

“Check the doc” has been one of the most consistent parts of my college routine — even more reliable than my sleep schedule.

Every day, like clockwork, I would get the message “Check the doc” and have to log in to the photo assignment sheet and see where I needed to be and what events I was covering.

Now, as I am getting ready to graduate, I keep thinking I forgot something, until I realize there’s no more doc to check.

Among the vast opportunities gifted to me, the best one of all … the people. Sienna, Sammy, Andrew, George, Mercedes and of course Teresa.

Sienna — You were the first person to introduce me to the world of professional photography. I would never have been able to use Adobe if you hadn’t taken the time to teach me how to use it, tips and tricks and best editing practices. You were always more than willing to answer any questions I had and played such a key role in my transition to Penn State.

Sammy — MY QUEEN. Girl, I will miss your witty, funny and most of the time inappropriate jokes. You can always make me laugh and I always looked forward to seeing you. I only really came to the photo budget because of you. But I swear if I get one more “check the doc” notification…

Andrew — You are pretty much always at the table, so at this point, I just expect you to be there. Thank you for being dependable. You always add a smile to my day.

George — King George. Although I haven’t seen you in months, I hope this semester is treating you well. From introducing me to movies to the Grinch costume you wore over Christmas, watching you coach basketball, your energy and positive attitude always made my day. Also, every time I’m at the bar, I'm playing cards — more specifically, playing High and Low. I hope you can join us for one last My Hero Zero night.

Mercedes — Thank you for trusting me with all my creative endeavors this year, allowing me to explore written staffs within the Collegian, and stretch some writing muscles I didn’t know I had.

Teresa — you already know.

Anyway, here’s to all the memories, late nights and tears shed laughing.

Signing off,

Emma Lessick

MORE COLUMNS

title: Senior Column | From ‘Stars Hollow’ to State College
author: Emma Naysmith I The Daily Collegian
description:
I’ll never forget the night before my college freshman year move-in re-watching “Gilmore Girls,” “The Lorelais’ First Day at Yale” alongside my family, wondering what my next four years would hold.

While Rory, one of the show’s protagonists, undoubtedly has her flaws, I can’t ignore some of the parallels in my life to hers.

I consider this my absolute comfort show, and my first time watching will forever be a sacred memory of my childhood. Consequently, I can’t deny how my perspective of the show has contributed to my thought process at times to reality.

I’ll start at the beginning, I’m blessed to have the best parents and best friends in them just as Rory has her mom, Lorelai, and her father-figure Luke.

My parents have always been my biggest inspiration and supporters, I’m so grateful for their guidance, support and most of all, friendship.

Just as the show journeys through Rory’s upbringing in the idyllic Stars Hollow, memories of my small town childhood hold that same comfort and glow.

Rory spent much of her upbringing with a coffee in hand and book in the other. Similarly, I often found myself enjoying that same combination, and still do.

She wouldn't be herself without her love of reading, which she states in her high school graduation, as one of the “two worlds” she lives in. Similarly, I define my second world as one dominated by a fascination with storytelling.

Some of my fondest childhood memories are of being read bedtime stories by my parents and my dad recounting his day at work while at the dinner table (the stories involving dogs whose names and photos were readily available were always my favorite).

In high school, just as Rory met a bold blonde, who grew into her best friend, I’m blessed to have had mine since before preschool.

Kiersten McNeely, I can’t remember a time without you and I wouldn’t want it any other way. Our friendship, shared memories and above all, sisterhood, means more to me than you will ever know.

Also in high school, Rory and I both joined our school newspaper. She and I both furthered our journalism careers to our college publications.

Joining The Daily Collegian changed so many aspects of my life both personally and professionally.

For that, I thank all of the leadership before me, and everyone I’ve ever had the opportunity to work with on staff.

Additionally, to those I’ve had the pleasure of interviewing, thank you for trusting me to share your stories.

Being news editor the fall semester during an immensely politically polarized time and presidential election pushed me more than I could’ve ever imagined.

I’m very thankful for my wonderful co-editor Kaitlyn Murphy, without you and our trauma bond, I wouldn’t have survived the semester.

Just as Rory dated a boy on the paper, so did I. I’ll always believe Rory and Logan were meant to be in so many ways.

To Abby Shanley (former business manager, woohoo!), I'm grateful to the Collegian and an off-campus location we spent way too many hours at for crossing our paths.

Meeting you and Jess Davidson (shoutout Facebook and Irving’s) was a highlight of my college experience. I’m blessed to have made such wonderful, lifelong friendships deriving from all of our shared “OG” experiences.

To my beloved roommates of the “Three One Six (7),” thank you for your constant love, support and friendship throughout my highs (Cafe grilled cheese) and lows (a certain February mimosa tower).

Hailey Koeppel, your sense of knowing oneself, defending your boundaries and undying loyalty has taught me so much about what it means to be a good friend and maintain healthy relationships. Thank you “Pitch Perfect!”

Bridget Hogan, your kindness, humor and the fun you bring to all of life’s events have brought me infinite smiles and so much light to my life.

Astha Amin, our friendship has made me feel so seen and understood. I’m grateful for our conversations, shared laughs, TikToks and of course, “honeymoon” nights out.

Jess, four years later and we made it through college! Being able to go through this chapter of our lives together has been the ultimate blessing. Your kind heart, thoughtfulness, unwavering support and friendship will always be special to me.

And to The Pennsylvania State University, never in a million years did I ever envision myself enrolling here, let alone loving my experience beyond measure.

As all good things must come to an end, I’ll forever cherish memories of my time and experiences in State College.

And as I plan to watch Rory’s graduation episode and her entrance to post-graduate life the night before my own graduation, I feel excitement toward the future and a thankfulness for everything that has brought me to this point.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Penn State football draft profile | Technique, kick return key for WR Trebor Peña
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
With 13 minutes remaining in the fourth quarter of the 2025 Pinstripe Bowl, Trebor Peña caught a pass up the middle around Penn State’s 44-yard line then immediately crashed into a pair of Clemson defenders.

However, the veteran receiver slipped both tackles and sprinted down the field for a career-long 73-yard receiving touchdown.

It was Peña’s second touchdown of the season, a noticeable drop from a nine-touchdown campaign in his previous breakout year at Syracuse where he was quarterback Kyle McCord’s primary target.

Despite having a down year in his single season at Penn State, Peña enters the 2026 NFL Draft with versatility as a wideout and as a return man.

His 2024 tape, and standout performances against Indiana and Clemson this year, offer teams a choice: take a chance at a receiver who’s had an up-and-down career, or pass him on to the next.

“We've been through a lot, but I feel like it was all worth it,” Peña said at pro day. “You learn a lot of lessons going through adversity and stuff that doesn't always go your way, but you have to find a way to bounce back. I feel like we all persevered and pushed through and showed the type of people we are.”

Metrics

Height: 5-foot-10

Weight: 187 pounds

Bench press: 19 reps

Vertical jump: 35.5 inches

Broad jump: 10-foot-3

40-yard dash: 4.52 seconds

Pro shuttle: 4.11 seconds

3-cone drill: 6.90 seconds

Projections

Projected round drafted: 7th round-undrafted

Player comparison: Greg Dortch

Scouting report

Although Peña’s smaller build creates some challenges when fighting through press coverage, he’s been known as a smooth receiver, catching in stride and selling cuts while maintaining the same speed.

However, while Peña is a technician, he lacks the explosive acceleration to create substantial gaps between him and the opposing cornerback, leading to many 50-50 balls where he is at a size disadvantage.

At the beginning of his collegiate career, Peña originally cemented himself as an elite kick returner in the ACC at Syracuse. His first collegiate touchdown came his freshman year in the form of a 98-yard kick return against NC State, the Orange’s first since 2011.

The following season, Peña kicked it up a notch as Syracuse’s go-to kick returner before a season-ending injury in Week 8 against Virginia Tech. Peña ranked second nationally and first in the ACC with 552 combined kick return yards on 34 returns.

In 2022, Peña transitioned into an option receiver, completing his first full season at Syracuse with 22 receptions for 203 yards. He maintained consistency as a return man as well, posting 508 combined kick return yards.

After redshirting in 2023 due to injury in Week 1 against Army, Peña had a career-defining redshirt junior campaign, tallying 84 receptions for 941 yards and nine touchdowns. He was named to the All-ACC second team after leading the conference in receptions.

Peña went from the No. 1 wide receiver with the Orange to an option alongside Devonte Ross and Kyron Hudson. Although he tallied 552 receiving yards compared to Ross’ 501, Peña only scored a pair of touchdowns while Ross scored five. Peña also saw less reps in the punt return but averaged 8.9 yards on eight attempts.

Peña faced many ups and downs throughout his time playing college football, but his versatility as a receiver and return man could make him an appetizing late-round pick.

Best team fit: New York Jets

Garrett Wilson, New York’s primary receiver, struggled with knee injuries in 2025, leading to his season being cut short in Week 10 against the Cleveland Browns.

Despite not playing the full schedule, Wilson still led the Jets with 395 receiving yards as the team relied on tight end Mason Taylor and running back Breece Hall for the remainder of the season.

Essentially, the Jets need the depth that Peña can provide. New York doesn’t have a solidified slot receiver, and although the Jets could select a higher-ranked player for that spot, depth remains necessary.

While Wilson is projected to be healthy upon his return to the NFL, the Jets are deep in a lengthy rebuild, and Peña could be a healthy inclusion to a wide receiver room that acquires a second or third-round pick as well.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: 30 years of  LGBTQ+ history in Penn State Athletics through Collegian reporting
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State is one of the most historic sporting institutions in the country. Its athletics program has produced gold-medal winning Olympians, star NFL players, six Dan Hodge Trophy winners on the mat and dozens of LGBTQ+ athletes who, while charting impressive athletic careers, doubled as activists for equality at Penn State and across the nation.

Take a look at some of the most pivotal moments for LGBTQ+ athletes in Penn State history.

March 1991:

In the late 1990s, Rene Portland, Penn State women’s basketball’s winningest coach of all time, emerged as a polarizing figure. In the midst of her 11th season, she became the figurehead of one of the campus’s first Pride-related debates.

In a Philadelphia Inquirer profile, Portland claimed she didn’t allow players who identified as lesbians on her team. At the time, the Collegian spoke with the Lesbian, Gay and Bisexual Student Alliance (LGBSA), who said this illustrated “the need for a sexual orientation clause,” to protect the rights of queer athletes.

Shortly after the article's publication, more than 50 students gathered outside Rec Hall to protest Portland’s alleged anti-LBGTQ+ biases.

“It's evident that not only do the students need to be educated on homosexuality but, embarrassingly enough, so does the faculty," said protester Angela Mikula, then-co-director of the USG Department of Women's Concerns.

Penn State’s athletic department eventually announced it would conduct a formal investigation of Portland’s alleged anti-LGBTQ+ stance. That investigation unearthed a 1986 Chicago Sun-Times article which detailed similar anti-LGBTQ+ sentiments from Portland.

"I think that any of us who have been here recognize that there is a long way to go in the whole realm of tolerance," associate athletic director Ellen Perry said. "In Intercollegiate Athletics, as the rest of us, we are just trying to find ways in which we can effectively educate and deal with these issues."

During the investigations, athletic director Jim Tarman released a comment, leading many to believe that action wouldn’t be taken against Portland.

"There is no University or athletic department policy which precludes or otherwise discriminates against the participation of student-athletes on the basis of sexual orientation,” Tarman said. “We have complied and will continue to comply with the University's policies of non-discrimination."

The LGBSA continued to protest the administration’s inaction and advocated for the addition of a sexual orientation clause to anti-discrimination policies.

“I think we'll just have to keep on doing this until we get what we deserve," LGBSA social/educational co-director Jeff Kemp said. "So that might mean a very busy week or two weeks – whatever it takes.

Amid ongoing protests, Lt. Gov. Mark Single met with students and expressed support for the addition of a sexual orientation clause. Joab Thomas, university president, said he would propose the idea at the next Board of Trustees’ meeting.

April 1991:

In early April, the Collegian published an article authored by two anonymous student-athletes, detailing the challenges queer athletes face.

“We are two lesbian student-athletes representing separate Penn State Athletic teams. We feel forced to conceal our identity as lesbian athletes due to the possible repercussions which may accompany revealing our sexual orientation. We also feel that there are many other gay men and lesbian athletes who, like us, are closeted in fear of discrimination. In our case, losing the opportunity to continue our education at Penn State.”

Collegian archives report LGBSA continued to spearhead the fight to add a LGBTQ+ non- discrimination clause and for the university to fire Portland.

Summer 1991:

In June the Board of Trustees voted to add a sexual orientation clause to the university’s list of protected categories.

“The Pennsylvania State University does not discriminate against any person because of age, ancestry, color, disability or handicap, national origin, race, religious creed, sex, sexual orientation or veteran status.”

The clause received widespread support from athletes and coaches alike.

“The new policy makes it much easier to look into these complaints. Before there was nothing that gave the affirmative action office the authority for investigating a complaint of discrimination based on sexual orientation," Bonnie Ortiz, director of the office of Affirmative Action, said.

Importantly, Ortiz said the policy is not retroactive, a crucial caveat in the case of Portland.

1992:

Despite the clause’s addition, there was still no action from the university on Portland’s allegations. This boiled over when members of Temple’s Gay Student Union decided to protest Portland when the Lady Lions played the Owls.

"We're protesting because she's taking the easy way out. She just bans everyone and we're supposed to accept it," Julie Davids, a Temple alumna, said. “That statement is ridiculous. It's a slap in the face.”

Protests continued at away women’s basketball games, notably Rutgers students wore purple shirts in support of queer athletes when the Lady Lions faced them.

1996

In 1996 Sue Rankin, one of Penn State’s longest-serving allies, departed her post for new ventures. Rankin coached Penn State softball for 16 years, during which she was one of the first openly lesbian DI coaches and led the program to numerous successes.

"I coached for about 16 years," Rankin said. "And during that time I think the program was successful. At the same time I was involved in promoting diversity.”

Rankin took a new role in the administration as a senior diversity planning analyst in the Office of the Vice Provost for Educational Equity, focused on LGBTQ+ issues at Penn State. Rankin later revealed in an op-ed that her decision to leave stemmed from pressure from the athletic department to cease her activism.

Although Penn State lost Rankin, students and faculty continued to advocate for queer athletes.

In November 1996, the Coalition of Lesbian, Gay and Bisexual Graduate Students organized again wearing t-shirts at a women’s basketball game supporting LGBTQ+ athletes.

1997:

T-shirt displays continued at women’s basketball games. Portland, however, claimed not to see the show of support for LGBTQ+ athletes.

"I have enough problems coaching my kids on the court," Portland said. “Anytime I look up, it's to see my mother."

Portland continued to express no formal support from Portland for LGBTQ+ athletes. In March, the Collegian reported Julie Jarosz, a women’s basketball center, left the team for personal reasons. Jarosz's father claimed it was due to abusive leadership from Portland, reenergizing the old scandal and foreshadowing more issues to come.

Rankin held a group discussion to help gain awareness, support and propose potential changes at Penn State to help boost support for queer athletes. The discussion focused on readings of anonymous statements from Penn State athletes on LGBTQ+ relations on campus.

"You're at a game and you've got all these other people, you know, not wanting to room with you or eat with you," Rankin read from an anonymous athlete's statement. "I wouldn't say the gay issue was the biggest problem our team had, but I think it lost games for us."

2005

With help from the National Center for Lesbian Rights, former Penn State women’s basketball player Jen Harris sent a letter to then-university President Graham Spanier claiming coach Portland discriminates against players she believes to be lesbian.

Harris claimed Portland made routine comments on her sexuality and pressured her to change the way she dressed.

"The climate in Penn State intercollegiate athletics is representative of systematic homophobia and heterosexism experienced by university community members on a daily basis," a statement by the campus queer community said, at the time.

The next day, the administration officially reviewed Harris's claims of bias, and the former Lady Lion continued to detail the mistreatment at the hands of Portland.

“(Portland) wanted me to change," Harris said. "She said my appearance came off, I guess, as being a lesbian. She didn't use those words exactly, but she kind of implied it."

In October, legal representatives for Harris and Penn State met, and Portland officially gave its reason for her release of the player. The coach claimed inadequate academic standing and improper conduct and denied anti-discrimination claims.

“It is unfortunate that she has chosen to attack me for her lack of success at Penn State," Portland said. "However, her lack of success had everything to do with her lack of commitment to basketball."

Those comments only further exacerbated matters, as Harris threatened to sue Portland the following day for defamation.

"Our original intent was to work with and cooperate with the university and assist them in their investigation," Karen Doering, Harris’ lawyer, said. "However, now that the university has put out statements making false and defamatory remarks about our client, we can no longer sit back and wait to begin the legal process that will enable us to produce evidence to prove that the allegations Jen Harris made are true."

Doering threatened a suit if the comments from Portland were not retracted. The renewed scandal in Portland led to more protests. The NCLR eventually moved forward with its lawsuit, after the administration claimed Harris’ side had not submitted sufficient evidence of Portland’s biases and that it couldn't investigate the matter without it.

Tension relating to the Portland scandal and support of LGBTQ+ athletes was at an all-time high. Nearly 70 protestors gathered outside Old Main to call for the firing of the coach, with a group of advocates even entering the building to chant their support.

The Office of Affirmative Action officially began an investigation of Portland and offered an update that same day, but no concrete action had yet begun.

2006:

In January, Portland had her first public appearance and refused to comment. Toward the end of the month, Portland and Penn State’s legal teams began filing motions to dismiss the suit and the coach again began to see protesters at away games, this time in Minnesota.

Earlier that same season, an estimated 500 protesters gathered at Michigan State against the coach’s practices.

By mid February, both sides had filed their claims in court and the case continued. At the end of the month protestors gathered for the last home game of the season and draped rainbow flags over sections of bleachers.

In April, the university completed its internal investigation and found Portland violated the university's non-discrimination policies. She would return to coach the Lady Lions; her punishment would be a $10,000 fine and a warning that if she were in violation again, she would be fired.

2007:

Portland continued to face public pressure, and in February 2007, the Collegian reported she and Harris reached a confidential settlement on the discrimination case.

“This has been a really important case, and this has had an impact on coaches and universities across the country," said Doering. "This case has generated an unprecedented public dialogue."

In the same year former Penn State basketball player John Amaechi detailed his coming-out in his 2007 book “Man in the Middle.” The center spent two seasons with the blue and white before five seasons in the NBA, and with his announcement, he was the first openly gay professional basketball player.

“Having the courage to say ‘I am gay,’ you can't get somebody better to stand in front of the camera and talk about it," ESPN books editor Chris Raymond said. "Now people have to have an enlightened discussion about [homosexuality in athletics]."

In an interview with the Collegian, Amaechi opened up on how his identity shouldn’t be defined by those two mere aspects.

"My definition is not wrapped by, such as my being is not wrapped up, in how I put a ball in a hole," Amaechi said. "It's also not wrapped in being a gay person. It's just a part of it."

In March 2007, the Collegian reported Portland officially resigned as head coach of the women’s basketball team two years before her contract expired with no exact explanation.

Her 27-year tenure at the helm ended with the most wins in program history.

Under the resignation article, the Collegian ran a piece on activist responses on the move; despite no legal ramifications for the alleged discrimination, it would be forever tied to her legacy.

“For nearly three decades, Coach Portland has had a documented history of discrimination and prejudice toward [lesbian, gay, bisexual and transgendered] athletes. Her resignation is just one of many steps that needs to occur at Penn State to improve campus climate issues both within and outside the athletic department," a statement released by SpeakOut read.

2019:

In 2019, the Collegian profiled Penn State alum Lee Cary. Cary got the distinct honor of leading the 76ers delegation in Philadelphia’s 31st Pride parade.

“I didn’t start coming out to friends or even talk about being gay until the final semester of my senior year at Penn State, so I was pretty blind to the resources and groups that were on-campus,” Cary said. “I spent a lot of time trying to be someone who I wasn’t. I internalized quite a lot of pain before I came out, hoping one day I would be different.”

2021:

Carl Nassib, a former Penn State football defensive end who played from 2013 to 2015 in Happy Valley and delivered one of the program's best defensive lineman seasons — including setting the single-season sack record with 15.5 in his final year — became the first active professional football player to publicly disclose that he is part of the LGBTQ+ community.

Nassib had just closed out his sixth season at the time of his announcement, and additionally, donated $100,000 to the Trevor Project, an advocacy group.

"Just wanted to take a quick moment to say that I'm gay," Nassib said in a video on his Instagram account. "I've been meaning to do this for a while now, but I finally feel comfortable enough to get it off my chest."

Since his announcement, the West Chester, Pennsylvania, native has continued to fight for better awareness and support for LGBTQ+ athletes. Nassib’s main venture now is his app Rayze, founded in 2020 for activists.

“A mobile first platform powering individual activism, nonprofit fundraising & corporate giving,” the app’s site reads. “We’re connecting donors, individuals, businesses and nonprofits, creating one platform for fundraising, corporate giving, campaign management, and volunteer recruitment.”

MORE SPORTS COVERAGE

title: Senior Column | Learning how to see
author: Sara O'Connell | The Daily Collegian
description:
There was a girl, too young to know her parents were never meant to be together. She suffered through their divorce process for years, roughly seven, and decided people couldn’t be trusted. She dreamed of authentic, honest people but fell short of finding them, perhaps because she didn’t actually want to, or maybe she believed she never could. She doesn’t have much recollection from her childhood anymore; maybe she blocked it out in a trauma response.

She went through school, graduated and never envisioned herself where she’s today, writing.

Growing up in a small, rural Pennsylvania town, I had no idea what to expect coming to a campus with 40,000 students. My understanding of life outside a rural public school, graduating 50 to 90 students a year, left me with mere basic knowledge and an overwhelming ignorance of what life would be like away from that part of Pennsylvania.

In a dream state, I came to Penn State, scared, clueless and undecided. I didn’t move far, but my entire world shifted when I was sent off, though I was never alone; I was with my sister, who came to Penn State the year prior.

I declared an English major, which guided me to be more inclined toward my love of writing.

I found myself loving delving into creative works in my free time and exploring various writing styles until I found my love for exploring not only my own stories, but the stories others can provide.

In high school, I wrote for the newspaper produced by staff and students, later becoming the editor. However, this was minor in comparison to exploring the voices of people who came from all parts of the country and the world.

By exploring journalism through the diverse voices of many, I learned about the power of my small town news. While it may seem insignificant in comparison, it made me realize voices need to be heard no matter where they come from.

As I progress toward the end of my time here at Penn State, in truth, I haven’t figured it all out yet. I haven’t learned where I want to be or who I want to be, but every day the journey becomes clearer.

I envision myself writing journalistically somewhere, someday, but for now, I’m open to every road the journey could take me on.

There is a girl who is graduating from college, entering the world where every little thing is unexpected but exciting. She believes life will take her on a remarkable journey, even if it only appears as though to her.

She went to college, graduated and couldn’t imagine a more exhilarating adventure ahead.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Four tips for decluttering your space this spring
author: Ava Krysko | The Daily Collegian
description:
The end of the semester is approaching and that means one thing — moving out. With the sadness of leaving for the summer comes the stress of packing. Are you dreading transporting all of your clothes, decorations and school supplies back home for break in the move-out carts that, despite what their name suggests, are a struggle to move?

As you watch your parents fight to fit everything into their car or as you stuff your suitcase to its breaking point, hoping the TSA weight limit was just a suggestion, you may start wondering what could have been done to prevent such an exhausting task.

Of course, there's a simple solution that could solve all of your problems: spring cleaning.

After all, the more you clean out now, the less you'll have to spruce when it comes time to move out. Whether you're in an apartment or dorm, everyone at college could use a good spring cleanout. Not only will it alleviate some of those nerves, but you'll also probably feel pretty accomplished with yourself.

To help you get started, here are four tips on getting rid of those dust bunnies so you can get out in the sun on campus to see actual bunnies.

1. Don't leave everything to the last minute

Although it can be tempting, trying to get all of your cleaning and decluttering done in an hour or two simply isn't enough and can leave things messier than when you started.

This means it's vital to start early. By doing a little bit at a time, it's more likely you won't feel overwhelmed or pressured to get everything done. You can even make a schedule or to-do list for yourself so things don't feel out of control.

2. Start the process by cleaning out as much as you can

That old sweater you wore once freshman year then never again? Donate it. The dirty shaker sitting under your bed from a football game? Trash it. Unless there's some sort of sentimental value, when it comes to spring cleaning, less is best.

The goal of this cleanout is to get rid of anything that isn't for you anymore. This means starting the process by getting rid of as much as you can. Going methodically through your clothes, shoes or even cosmetics is a good way to get the process started.

On that note, it may be helpful to designate an area of your living space for things you want to keep, things you'd like to donate and things that need to be thrown away.

3. Let fresh air in and play some music

Despite its bad rap, cleaning doesn't have to be boring either. By playing some music or putting on a comfort show in the background, you're all set to have some fun.

Another good idea is to open a window and let fresh air in. That way, any overdue laundry or rotten food you find during your cleanout (although, hopefully, your space is devoid of that) doesn't provide too many fumes.

4. Don't be afraid to keep going when the going gets tough

After deciding which pile your stuff belongs in (keep, donate or throw out), you may be tempted to stop this endeavor. However, it's important that you don't.

One of the principal parts of spring cleaning is giving your space a good scrub. Mopping, sweeping and vacuuming are just some of the options when it comes to this step. You also may want to dust or wipe everything down. Just remember to never give up.

When you notice that spider and its web in the long-forgotten corner of your living room, don't pretend you didn't see it. Get in there and do what needs to be done. After that, your environment will truly be clean.

Why is spring cleaning so important?

Although it may seem like a dreadful chore, spring cleaning doesn't have to be hard and it comes with a lot of benefits. Having an organized and pristine living environment can really make a difference when it comes to mental and physical health.

Getting rid of items you don't need anymore helps reduce stress and release endorphins due to feelings of success when you're all done. As for physical benefits, cleaning helps get rid of any dust or mold toxins you may be breathing in. By cleaning your room, you're helping your psyche to reset and feel at ease.

This means having a more focused mind when it comes to studying for finals and spending less time trying to find your favorite going-out top in the clutter of items in your room. Overall, taking time to spruce up your space a bit can make you more productive in the long run.

MORE BLOGS

title: ‘He’s built a legacy’ | Penn State track and field celebrates 20 years with coach John Gondak
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coming from a big Penn State family, it was a no-brainer for coach John Gondak to come on as an assistant coach for the Nittany Lions in 2006.

“While I didn’t graduate from here, I’m very close to it,” Gondak told The Daily Collegian. “I grew very fond of what Penn State is and what it represents. I just feel this is a place that is great for what we do as a distance coach and thought it was a place that I’d really like to be.”

Twenty years later, Gondak continues to coach at Penn State, now in his 12th year as the head coach for track and field and cross country.

“He’s built a pretty good program,” Handal Roban, a current athlete on Penn State’s squad, told the Collegian. “He has a couple national champions under his belt, and he’s a really good leader and a mentor.”

In his early years with the Nittany Lions, Gondak played a big role with the distance and cross country programs, helping to make steady improvements for both squads.

In his second year, he helped Bridget Franek to a Big Ten bronze medal in the 3,000-meter steeplechase, pushing the women’s squad to earn an outdoor Big Ten team title. Two years later, he helped the women’s team earn the Big Ten Triple Crown, claiming the cross country, indoor and outdoor track and field team titles.

“The culture here is just phenomenal,” Gondak said. “I think that feeling helps keep you motivated to want to provide the athletes a great experience as well.”

Following the team’s triple crown, Gondak was named Mid-Atlantic Region Assistant Coach of the Year — his first of many. In 2013, he added to that number as he coached two athletes to top-three finishes in the NCAA championship men’s 800-meters and a sixth-place finish in the men’s 1500-meters, earning Mid-Atlantic Region Men’s Outdoor Assistant Coach of the Year.

After a third historic season earned him the title of Mid-Atlantic Region Men’s Indoor Coach of the Year with three individual Big Ten champions, Gondak was named the head coach in the summer of 2014.

Gondak’s first few seasons of his head coaching career saw several big moments, including multiple All-Americans and individual Big Ten titles.

However, arguably the biggest success story from Gondak’s coaching career has been Isaiah Harris, an NCAA champion, eight-time Big Ten champion and five-time world championship qualifier.

“When I first started talking with Gondak, I knew this guy knew what he was talking about,” Harris told the Collegian. “He wasn’t trying to sell me on a false dream. You can tell when someone is confident in their own ability, and I could sense that in him.”

That testament came true early on in Harris’ career, as Gondak coached him to two conference titles during his freshman year.

“At a young age, it’s important to have people that believe in you, especially a coach,” Harris said. “I really liked the way he talked about the future, and he saw the potential in myself that I hadn’t seen.”

Over the next three years, Gondak built Harris into one of the best, if not the best, mid-distance runners in Penn State history.

In 2018, Harris capped off his collegiate career with Penn State’s first 800-meter title, showcasing the passion and dedication Gondak has for his athletes.

“He rarely got excited,” Harris said. “When I crossed the finish line at nationals and finally saw him after, he had the biggest smile. He came up and hugged me, and that was such an awesome moment because those moments are rare, but it makes it that much more special because you know he truly means it.”

A year later, the Nittany Lions earned their second 800-meter individual title as Danae Rivers won the program’s first women’s title.

While Gondak’s mid-distance squad has yet to win another individual NCAA championship since Harris and Rivers, Cheickna Traore earned the third national title under Gondak’s reign in 2024.

“I’m very proud of all the different conference championships we’ve won and the trophies at the NCAA and top-10 finishes,” Gondak said, “The most important thing is the student athletes over the years that have come through the program and had a great experience and graduated and stayed connected with us.”

As Gondak’s been coaching for Penn State, Kentucky, Toledo and Georgia Tech for the past 30 years, he’s undergone big changes in his role. Despite this, he’s tackled them head-on and worked to make the program the best it could be.

“Coaching is something you have to be very self-motivated,” Gondak said. “You could sit there at home every night and not call recruits. Or you could have that drive to compete. I’m excited about helping this program be successful.”

Over the past several years, his program has grown to become one of the best in the country.

The men’s team has earned multiple NCAA All-Americans, including Roban, Ajani Dwyer and Jake Palermo, consistently ranking as a top-20 team in the USTFCCCA poll.

“He’s built a legacy,” Roban said. “We’re going to show that not only the middle distance program is good, but all around, we’re a good program, and that’s because we have a good leader.”

In his 20th season with the blue and white, Gondak has continued to hold himself and his team to a high standard as they continue their winning ways.

“It’s very impressive to spend 20 years in the same spot as a coach,” Harris said. “That is a testament to his consistency and how he leads the program and how the university believed in him. He’s very loyal and loves being there.”

Despite Gondak’s success, he directs all of the accolades toward the team, staying out of the spotlight throughout his historic career.

“I don’t think he gets enough credit for how much he’s done, not just for the program, but for the sport as a whole,” Harris said. “He just flies under the radar and consistently produces, and that’s super honorable because it’s not an ego thing, it’s not a pride thing. He wants his athletes to become the best athletes they can.”

MORE SPORTS COVERAGE

title: Queer LLC Ally House remains a safe space amid DEI policy concerns
author: Aly Cromar I For the Collegian
description:
Ally House, Penn State's LGBTQ+ living-learning community (LLC) on campus, has provided a pivotal safe space for queer students over the years, providing them with a sense of belonging.

Ally House is meant to be a zone of safety and identity for many of its residents in a shifting political climate. Amid ongoing conversations about diversity, equity and inclusion, students say its role feels as important as ever, though not without challenges.

CJ Bean said Ally House is an essential aspect of their college experience.

“It provided a space where I can just feel comfortable knowing that most of the people in my building are safe to be myself around,” Bean, a second-year studying computer science, said. “It provided one of the first spaces where I felt comfortable being out.”

With safety being a big concern within the LLC, the inclusive facilities in Ally House were especially important to former E-board Member Ashley Wozniak. Wozniak, second-year student of nuclear engineering, said the presence of gender neutral bathrooms made her feel comfortable.

“Ally House has provided me a safe space to live,” Wozniak said.

Wozniak said the positive environment in Ally House does not come without its challenges. While she said she personally didn’t face challenges in the entire bunch, she noted internal social dynamics can sometimes feel exclusionary for those not part of established groups.

Wozniak said she experienced this firsthand during her time on the executive board.

“The rest of the members of the executive board seemed to be in their own clique, and it felt isolating at times,” Wozniak said. “I felt afraid to bring up concerns or issues I had with how Ally House was being run, as they all seemed to be in agreement already.”

She added that the concentration of like-minded individuals in the space can also lead to tension around differing viewpoints. While Ally House fosters community among students with shared experiences, Wozniak said it can sometimes feel less open to political perspectives outside the majority.

“There was an incident last year where they were bothering someone on the first floor for having a Trump 2024 flag up,” Wozniak said. “Which, of course, I didn’t support the flag, but it didn’t warrant harassment in my eyes.”

The current political climate has caused fear for some residents amid the rollback of DEI initiatives across the nation. While no direct changes to Ally House funding or operations have been reported, if it were reduced or eliminated, residents say the impact would be felt.

“I think it would be very likely that if queer spaces were eliminated on campus, many of the spaces for other marginalized groups would also be threatened,” Bean said.

AJ Charnetski said they wouldn’t feel safe on campus without these resources, as Ally House made it easier for them to find others with shared experiences.

“If queer spaces were eliminated on campus, I feel as though our freedom would be lost,” Charnetski, a second-year studying biological engineering,. “I would have to hide on campus in fear of being judged or worse. The queer spaces on campus allow me to feel like myself without fear.”

Queer students' fears have been enhanced in recent years due to the introduction of anti-DEI bills and legislation.

“I’m worried about how the current president and his administration’s view on trans people may affect us,” Bean said. “Whether they just revoke funding overall or force groups to hide support for trans people.”

Though, Wozniak said she doesn’t think it’s likely for Ally House to disappear due to anti-DEI policies, despite the university not denouncing what the current administration is doing.

Residents of the LLC said the existence of the space itself remains critical. For many, it’s a place that allows them to be themselves and build community in ways they might not be able to elsewhere.

“The absence of safe spaces will not get rid of queer folk,” Wozniak said. “But it will make our lives more difficult, more stressful and more exhausting.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: LGBTQ+ in STEM, Opulence panel explores identity, inclusion
author: Darina Ayazbayeva | For the Collegian
description:
About 30 Penn State students, faculty and staff gathered Wednesday evening for a two-hour event focused on identity, inclusion and representation in science fields, part of “LGBTQ+ in STEM Leading the Way: Perspective on Identity.”

The event is a part of an ongoing collaborative Pride Month series with the Eberly College of Science, the College of Information Sciences and Technology, the College of Engineering, the College of Earth and Mineral Sciences, and the College of Health and Human Development.

Dan Henderson, associate director for belonging and inclusion in the College of Engineering, said the goal was to bring together the STEM community and LGBTQ+ voices.

“Every spring, for a couple of years already, we try to hold an inter-STEM event for Campus Pride Month,” Henderson said. “If it weren’t important, no one would care; the fact that people show up during a busy time of the semester shows that they want a community representation and a sense of belonging.”

Henderson also said representation remains important in STEM fields, because of expectations about what people in these fields should look like or how they are supposed to act.

“Events like this give students a chance to see that there is a community for them,” he said.

The second half of the event featured a panel discussion with members of Opulence, Penn State’s Drag Ambassadors organization. The panelists, five drag queens who are also students in STEM-related fields, shared personal experiences navigating both their professional identities and their identities as a part of the LGBTQ+ community.

One of the attendees, Alexandra Jackson, a professor in the College of Engineering, said she came to the event for a better understanding of how faculty and staff can create a more welcoming atmosphere in a STEM field and how they can support the LGBTQ+ student community more.

Jackson said the university is welcoming, but conversations around the identity topic can still feel limited.

Jackson said the topic could always be discussed more, especially as STEM is traditionally a male-dominated field. She said the atmosphere of the event was very welcoming and that she would love to know how she can help promote this topic more as a faculty member.

“If we continue to reduce that stigma, we’ll be in a much better place,” Jackson said.

Gabrielle Zuiker, a staff worker with Penn State’s animal research program, also emphasized the importance of representation and discussion in scientific spaces.

“There are a lot of people who identify as not straight, so it’s important for them to know they’re not alone, ” Zuiker said. “Having different identities and perspectives in science really matters.”

While Zuiker said she does not always engage in large campus conversations because of the independent nature of her work, she believes discussions like this should happen more often.

“Some people are still not ready to talk about this,” Zuiker said. “It’s something that can feel stigmatized.”

Panelists also addressed the challenges of balancing authenticity with expectation in STEM spaces, which are often seen as more traditional or conservative. For Aurora Railsback, that sense of belonging comes with both confidence and risk.

“Even showing myself in this way can be kind of scary,” Railsback, a first-year studying aerospace engineering, said. “Sometimes you need to take different routes just to feel safe.”

Railsback also said it can feel tight being in a STEM field profession.

“I always wear suits during any professional event, just because it seems professional," Railsback said. "As a woman in a STEM field, I always need to push a little more just to be noticed."

Railsback said the event created a sense of encouragement. She said she hopes after this attendees promote more inclusivity.

“Be the person that your younger self would want to look up to,” Railsback said.

Elaine Paulk, vice president of Opulence, said awareness remains a challenge, even as acceptance grows. It’s important to continue attempting to raise awareness.

“There are still a lot of bubbles where people just don’t know about our community and us,” Paulk, a second-year studying biology, said.

Paulk described the atmosphere of the drag event as energizing and affirming. Pauik also highlights that authenticity is central to both identity and expression.

“There is no greater weapon against oppression than being yourself,” Paulk, a second-year student studying biology, said. “Be yourself, even though a lot of people would try to go against it.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: ‘There's a need just beyond the students’ | STEM colleges hold first-ever pride mixer
author: Vasco Chen | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s first “Pride in Every Field: A STEM Mixer with Rainbow Networks and Allies” took place this Wednesday at 12:30 p.m. at the Noontime Lounge in the HUB-Robeson Center.

The mixer is a collaboration between the STEM colleges to foster inclusivity, build networks and grow a community among LGBTQ+ community members, Rainbow Networks and allies. Organizer Dr. Aaron Mattingly is the director of graduate equity programs in the Eberly College of Science.

He also organizes “LGBTQ+ in STEM Leading the Way: Perspectives on Identity,” an interactive discussion panel on STEM identity and queerness.

“A lot of that event is dedicated towards listening to others, but not really, truly having a whole lot of time and space to engage with each other,” Mattingly said. “So we were like … (let’s) have everybody together in a nice space to interact, get to actually know these networks face to face, rather than just being kind of names on a list where you might feel awkward with kind of just cold emailing somebody out of the blue, being like, ‘I'm of this community, I need help in some sort of way.’”

He said there are over seventy registrants so far, and about half of them are staff and faculty members.

“It kind of shows that there's a need just beyond the students and finding community,” Mattingly said.

John Neisser, an associate teaching professor in Eberly, said Penn State has some work to do to ensure everyone is represented.

“There’s a lot of programming that’s directed to the students, which is great, but … the faculty perhaps can use more programming,” Neisser said. “There’s certainly a large population of LGBT and queer people who are the undergrad, the student age, but it’s a little bit tougher … as a professor or a staff member. Sometimes being … LGBT can be a little bit isolating.”

Dr. Andrea Tapia, dean of the College of Information Sciences and Technology, gave an opening speech before the mixer officially began.

“If you are here today as a scholar, a scientist, a student, a researcher, as your full and authentic self, you are already doing something extraordinary,” Tapia said in the speech. “We are navigating a moment when science itself is questioned, when evidence is treated like opinion, when entire fields of inquiry are dismissed with a shrug or a budget cut. At the same time, LGBTQ+ people, our identities, our histories, our contributions are being sidelined.”

Tapia also said science is the art of illuminating blind spots. Because queer people “live at the margins,” they learn to see the system and its gaps.

Courtney Taylor, a graduate assistant at the Office of Diversity and Inclusion, offers academic and holistic life support to students. She came to the mixer to gain more knowledge and on the resources available to students.

“Representation goes a long way, whether you're in the community or not,” Taylor, a second-year graduate student studying recreation, park and tourism management, said. “Just even knowing that there's people who are allies is a benefit to the students who might be interested. We all need to continue to do the work and also show other people that if you see yourself in this type of position or this role, you can still be yourself and take on this type of role.”

Marisa Ferger, an assistant teaching professor in the Department of Meteorology and Atmospheric Science, said it’s important to have the visibility of people who have been trained to show that they are an ally.

“Being able to put the sticker on your office door to say you're a member of a rainbow network or you've taken the Safer People Safer Places classes are really important,” Ferger, a member of her department’s Committee on Belonging, said. “When new students or even current students and new employees show up at the university, they know that they are welcome and that they have an ally there for them.”

Mattingly said sometimes it's unconscious bias that rears its head and contributes to queer students feeling like spaces aren’t for them.

“What can often happen too is that when you're of a more marginalized or minoritized group, the further you dig down into certain spaces,” Mattingly said. “So sometimes it is important to reach out across the larger spaces while still staying within potentially your general field.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State softball back on the road, set for clash with Ohio State
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
After seven straight home dirt contests, Penn State is set to hit the road again to face Ohio State.

The Nittany Lions were swept by Northwestern in their last conference series, but bounced back Tuesday night with an 11-3 win in a midweek meeting with Bucknell.

With the three losses last weekend, the blue and white saw its Big Ten record drop to 7-8, while standing at 27-15 overall. The Buckeyes dropped 2-of-3 games to Michigan in its last conference series, to hold an 8-7 conference record and have a 21-21 mark overall.

The Buckeyes welcome Pittsburgh for a Wednesday night matchup before hosting Penn State.

Ohio State has a powerful offense, owning the Big Ten’s sixth-best batting average, .324. The only team with a better mark that the Nittany Lions have faced is Oregon.

The Buckeyes have a knack for the long ball, owning the conference's second-best mark with 100 home run on the season. In its 13-2 win over Michigan, Ohio State tied its single-game program record after sending the ball out of the park seven times.

The scarlet and grey have seven players batting over .300, including four above .360, led by Sami Bewick’s .381 average. Taylor Cruse leads the Buckeyes with 39 RBIs, tied with Abbie Wilhelm.

Wilhelm was the highlight of Ohio State’s win over Michigan, going 3-for-4 with three home runs and five RBIs.

Penn State owns a .300 team batting average, ranking 10th in the conference. Junior Brooke Klosowicz leads the Nittany Lions with a .375 batting average, while freshman Allison Oneacre owns a team high 52 RBI, which is the fifth highest mark in the Big Ten.

Oneacre is just two RBIs away from breaking the program's single-season record set by Klosowicz a year ago.

The blue and white’s most recent outing was highlighted by a four RBI performance from freshman Breanna Hanik, as she recorded a double and a home run.

Ohio State’s pitching is led by freshman right-hander Jenna Molk. The Dover, Ohio, native accounts for 128 of the Buckeyes total 244 innings pitched in the circle. Molk owns a 3.23 ERA and a 15-12 record, while tossing 110 strikeouts on the season.

Penn State brings a more balanced approach to the circle, with five arms combining for a 4.04 ERA, tying for the sixth-best mark in the conference.

Sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton and junior right-hander McKenna Young each own top-15 ERA’s in the Big Ten. Britton has the upper edge with a 3.25 ERA, followed by Young’s 3.42.

Freshman right-hander Mackenzie Duncan is the staff's strikeout leader with 59 on the season. Junior right-hander Bridget Nemeth has worked the most innings, with 59 ⅓ pitched this year.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘This is What it Feels Like’ sets up catcalling, street harassment exhibit because it ‘can happen to anybody’
author: Jacob Godshall I The Daily Collegian
description:
In order to draw awareness to the issues of street harassment and catcalling, the Relationship Violence Outreach, Intervention and Community Education Center (R-VOICE) and HUB-Robeson Galleries teamed up with Artist and Musician Terra Lopez to bring the “This is What it Feels Like” project to Penn State.

The “This is What it Feels Like” project began in 2022 and creates an auditory experience in which participants are on the receiving end of catcalling and harassment. The project’s mission statement says that the hope is to “promote understanding and stop the misogynistic patterns that have become embedded in our culture.”

Sarah Kipp, who helped organize and construct the event, said that now is a very important time to be discussing this issue.

“Under the current administration, I do feel that toxic masculinity is a really huge problem, and so I think that drawing attention to this kind of thing is more important than ever,” Kipp, the exhibition coordinator for HUB-Robeson Galleries, said. “It is something that affects so many women, and so every person has somebody that they love that goes through this.”

The display included a wall of black curtains with a disclaimer on what the experience will be like and what it discusses.

The focus of the exhibit is on a small, curtained-in booth, dimly lit and decorated only with a mirror. During the experience, participants wear a set of headphones as they look at themselves in the mirror, and voice lines of men catcalling in English and Spanish are played. When wearing the headphones, participants hear comments like “Hey wifey come here,” “You’re so beautiful,” “Suck my d—ck,” and “What? You can’t take a compliment?” Afterward, participants wrote on a shared reflection board.

“Terra made it really easy for us because this exhibit has been done in different types of places, and she's very flexible and open minded in terms of how to make this experience work best for your space,” Kipp said. “They also have construction guidelines as to what the booth should look like, things like that, so she made it really easy for us.”

Chloe Crabb, a member of R-VOICE, said the message of the exhibit is that catcalling, street harassment and gender-based violence is never okay, “and can happen to anybody.”
